```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Here’s an up-to-date overview of key **issues and limitations for Azure AI Foundry VNet (Virtual Network) integration** as of 2025–2026:\n\n---\n\n### **Core Issues & Limitations**\n\n1. **Regional Constraints**\n   - **All workspace resources (Foundry Account, Cosmos DB, Storage, AI Search, Managed Identity, etc.) must be deployed in the SAME region as the VNet** for Foundry Agent Service to work. Cross-region patterns are not supported for Agents—cross-region Private Endpoints are valid for inferencing scenarios, but not full agent deployments【3:6†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n2. **Private Endpoints & DNS Limitations**\n   - **Private Endpoints must be provisioned and DNS set up correctly.** If DNS is misconfigured, or not all referenced endpoints are included, agents and tools may not be able to access needed resources—even if “connected” in the portal【3:3†source】【3:9†source】.\n   - Managed (preview) private endpoints for Foundry may not create NICs visible in your VNet, making them harder to troubleshoot【3:8†source】.\n\n3. **Hub/Project Model Network Isolation**\n   - In Hub-based projects, Managed VNet does *not* (as of mid-2025) secure agent outbound traffic: subnet injection still applies, and NSG/Azure Firewall config is required on your end for isolation【3:3†source】【3:1†source】.\n   - Managed VNet only applies to specific compute features (like PromptFlow); not to full agent traffic or private endpoint routing【3:3†source】【3:8†source】.\n\n4. **Scenarios where Full Isolation Breaks**\n   - **Copilot Studio integration with Foundry**: If Foundry Agent Service or AI Search is behind a private endpoint, Copilot Studio may not be able to add them as tools/knowledge sources. End-to-end isolation is only supported in classic agent services as of late 2025【3:2†source】.\n   - Some “success” statuses in the Power Platform/Foundry admin UI may be false positives; troubleshooting relies on external tools and direct Powershell interventions【3:2†source】.\n\n5. **General Agent Service Limitations**\n   - If public access is disabled without complete Private Endpoint and DNS configuration, agent calls will fail (no fallback).\n   - Stale tool/endpoint configs: If you update from public to private access, agents/tools may still reference public endpoints until manually refreshed【3:3†source】.\n\n6. **Miscellaneous Constraints**\n   - Subnet size requirements: A dedicated subnet, /26 or larger for agents, /27 for private endpoints, is recommended.\n   - Outbound internet denial: Denying outbound by default requires explicit approval of required services/resources via Private Endpoints.\n   - Azure role and provider registration: Lapses in required RBAC or missing resource provider registrations cause authentication or creation failures【3:1†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n---\n\n### **Summary Table**\n| Area                                | Limitation/Issue                                                                         |\n|--------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|\n| Regionality                         | Must co-locate all resources (no cross-region agents)                                    |\n| Private Endpoint support             | Requires correct DNS, no visible NICs for managed endpoints (preview)                    |\n| Hub/Project model                   | Managed VNet incomplete for agents/outbound isolation                                    |\n| Tooling integrations                | Copilot Studio breaks with full private endpoint isolation                               |\n| DNS/troubleshooting                  | Limited portal observability, PowerShell tools may be needed                             |\n| Subnet requirements                 | Dedicated subnets, specific size recommendations                                         |\n| Manual refreshes for endpoints      | Endpoint references may not auto-refresh after network model changes                     |\n\n---\n\n### **Official Documentation and Community Insights**\n\n- Full network isolation and VNet integration with Azure AI Foundry is powerful, but requires careful setup: resource regionality, NSG and firewall controls, DNS integration, role assignments, and testing are all critical【3:1†source】【3:2†source】【3:3†source】【3:6†source】【3:7†source】【3:8†source】【3:9†source】.\n- The service is actively evolving, and **many features are under public preview**: always consult the <sub>⏱️ 8m 28s</sub>
```

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry managed identity private endpoint VNet configuration problems**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry managed identity private endpoint VNet configuration problems"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"You are likely facing issues with **Azure AI Foundry's managed identity** and **private endpoint configuration within a Virtual Network (VNet)**. These scenarios often involve complex networking, firewall, and identity configurations. Below are common issues, troubleshooting steps, and considerations:\n\n---\n\n## **Common Issues & Solutions**\n\n### **1. Managed Identity Not Authenticating Correctly**\n- **Check VNet Integration**: Ensure your Foundry resources (like Azure Machine Learning, Azure OpenAI, or related AI services) are linked to a subnet in the desired VNet.\n- **Network Policies**: Subnets with private endpoints **must not** have network security group rules that deny access for required ports (primarily for Azure resource communication).\n- **Managed Identity Permissions**: Confirm that the managed identity has the necessary role assignments (e.g., Reader, Contributor, or custom roles) on the resource(s) it needs to access.\n\n### **2. Private Endpoint Not Accessible**\n- **DNS Configuration**: When you use private endpoints, your resource’s public DNS must be overridden. Set up an appropriate Azure Private DNS Zone (e.g., `privatelink.azure.com`), and link it to your VNet. Validate that the resource hostname resolves to the private IP.\n- **Subnet Delegation & NSGs**: Ensure that subnet delegation is properly set and that Network Security Groups allow traffic to the private endpoint.\n- **Firewall Whitelisting**: The resource should allow traffic from your VNet/subnet. If you use a firewall or route table (UDR), confirm it does not block Azure IPs.\n- **Service Endpoint Policies**: If using service endpoints (different from private endpoints), check the policies don’t restrict access unintentionally.\n\n### **3. Connectivity Problems (Timeouts, DNS errors)**\n- **Client Network**: The client/resource (such as your Azure AI Foundry service) calling the private endpoint must reside in the same VNet, a peered VNet, or be securely connected via VPN/ExpressRoute.\n- **Name Resolution**: Use [nslookup, dig] from a VM within the VNet to verify hostname resolution to private IP.\n- **Outbound Internet**: For some Azure services, even with private endpoints, managed identity authentication flows may require limited outbound internet access to Azure AD endpoints.\n\n### **4. Multiple Private Endpoints**\n- Ensure that only one private endpoint per resource type is configured in the VNet, as competing DNS registrations can cause outages.\n\n---\n\n## **Troubleshooting Steps**\n1. **Validate NSGs:** Make sure required ports are open (mostly 443 for Azure services).\n2. **Test DNS:** Use Azure Bastion or VM in VNet: `nslookup <resource-name>.azure.com`. Should resolve to private IP.\n3. **Check Role Assignments:** Go to Azure portal → Managed Identity → Azure role assignments.\n4. **Resource Firewall:** On resource (e.g., Azure Key Vault, Storage), make sure \"Deny public network access\" is enabled, and your VNet is explicitly allowed.\n5. **Logs:** Check Azure Monitor and Diagnostic logs for authentication or network errors.\n\n---\n\n## **References**\n- [Configure private endpoint for Azure AI services](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/common/private-endpoint)\n- [Troubleshoot private endpoint connection issues in Azure](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/troubleshoot-private-endpoint)\n- [Managed identity documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/managed-identities-azure-resources/overview)\n\n---\n\nIf you have **specific error messages** or a detailed problem scenario, sharing them will help narrow down the solution even more!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

<sub>⏱️ 8m 27s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure API Management VNet integration with Azure AI Foundry MCP OpenAPI endpoint**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure API Management VNet integration with Azure AI Foundry MCP OpenAPI endpoint"
}
```

</details>

````
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"To **integrate Azure API Management (APIM) in a Virtual Network (VNet) with an Azure AI Foundry (Managed Control Plane - MCP) OpenAPI endpoint**, you need to consider the following aspects:\n\n---\n### **1. Overview of Integration**\n- **APIM in VNet:** Allows you to secure your APIs so they're only accessible from inside the selected network, or from designated networks using private endpoints.\n- **Azure AI Foundry MCP:** Typically offers endpoints that are either public or can be locked down for private access (e.g., using Private Link).\n\n**Goal:** Expose the OpenAPI endpoint (from Azure AI Foundry) securely via APIM deployed within a VNet.\n\n---\n\n### **2. Key Integration Steps**\n\n#### **A. Networking**\n- **APIM in VNet:** Ensure your APIM instance is deployed in **Internal Mode** (inside your VNet).\n- **AI Foundry MCP endpoint accessibility:**  \n  - If the endpoint is public, APIM can reach it if it has outbound internet access.\n  - If the endpoint is private (using Private Link), your VNet (with APIM) must be peered with the VNet hosting the endpoint, or both must be in the same VNet. Ensure proper DNS configuration for the private endpoint.\n\n#### **B. API Import & Configuration**\n- **Import OpenAPI spec into APIM:**  \n  - Download the OpenAPI specification from Azure AI Foundry MCP.\n  - In the APIM portal, create a new API, import using the OpenAPI specification.\n- **Set backend service URL:**  \n  - Point the APIM API to the OpenAPI endpoint (either the public URL or the private IP/FQDN, as resolved in your VNet).\n\n#### **C. Security Considerations**\n- **Network Security Groups (NSG):**  \n  - Update NSG rules to allow APIM to reach the AI Foundry MCP endpoint on required ports (often HTTPS/443).\n- **Application Gateway/Firewall:**  \n  - Optionally, place Application Gateway or WAF in front of APIM for extra protection.\n- **Authentication:**  \n  - Secure the backend endpoint using OAuth, API keys, or Managed Identity if supported by Azure AI Foundry.\n\n#### **D. Testing & Validation**\n- Test the API endpoint (exposed by APIM) from within the allowed scope (e.g., inside the VNet or via a jump box).\n- Monitor logs in APIM and look for connectivity errors.\n\n---\n\n### **3. Diagram Example**\n\n```plaintext\n[Client]\n   |\n[APIM in VNet (Internal)]\n   |\n  (Private VNet Peering/Subnet)\n   |\n[Azure AI Foundry MCP endpoint (private/public)]\n```\n\n---\n\n### **4. Troubleshooting Tips**\n- **DNS Resolution:** For private endpoints, ensure APIM can resolve the FQDN of the Azure AI Foundry endpoint to the private IP.\n- **Outbound Connectivity:** APIM in 'internal' mode **does not have public outbound internet** unless you've set up outbound internet access (e.g., using NAT Gateway, Private Endpoint, or UDR).\n- **Health Probes:** If APIM cannot access the backend, check connectivity using `Network Watcher` or similar tools.\n\n---\n\n## **References & Microsoft Documentation**\n\n- [Integrate API Management in an internal VNet with a backend service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-vnet?tabs=custom)\n- [Secure Azure AI services with virtual networks](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/security-vnet)\n- [Import and publish your first API in Azure API Management](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/import-and-publish)\n\n---\n\n**If you have a specific integration scenario or run into issues (e.g., authentication method, endpoint visibility, specific error codes), please share details for a more tailored solution.**","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
````

**Azure AI Foundry Agent Service VNet bring your own VNET BYOVNET testing checklist 2025**

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry Agent Service VNet bring your own VNET BYOVNET testing checklist 2025"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Certainly! Here’s a **comprehensive checklist** for testing the \"Bring Your Own VNet (BYOVNET)\" functionality in the context of Azure AI Foundry Agent Service, particularly if you plan for production-readiness in 2025. This checklist is based on best practices for Azure services, enterprise networking, and managed AI agent deployments.\n\n---\n\n## **Azure AI Foundry Agent Service — BYOVNET Testing Checklist (2025)**\n\n### 1. **Prerequisites & Planning**\n- [ ] Confirm service availability for BYOVNET (review Azure roadmap & documentation for your region and subscription).\n- [ ] Design and provision the custom VNet: CIDR ranges, subnets, NSGs, and route tables.\n- [ ] Ensure RBAC and permissions for creating/using VNets and subnets are in place.\n\n---\n\n### 2. **VNet Configuration Validation**\n- [ ] Subnet delegation and service endpoints are configured appropriately for Azure AI Foundry Agent Service.\n- [ ] NSGs allow all required service traffic (both ingress and egress).\n- [ ] UDRs do not block required outbound connectivity to Azure dependencies.\n- [ ] DNS configuration: Custom or Azure-provided; check for resolution of service endpoints.\n- [ ] Integration with Azure Private Link or Private Endpoints (if supported/required).\n\n---\n\n### 3. **Service Deployment Integration**\n- [ ] Deploy the AI Foundry Agent Service into the BYOVNET (portal, CLI, ARM, or Terraform).\n- [ ] Confirm successful attachment to the custom VNet and subnet.\n- [ ] Check for correct provisioning in your Azure Activity Log or deployment output.\n\n---\n\n### 4. **Functional Connectivity Checks**\n- [ ] Confirm inbound (to agent) and outbound (from agent) networking functions as expected:\n    - [ ] To Azure Data services (e.g., Storage, SQL, Key Vault).\n    - [ ] To on-premises networks (if using VPN/ExpressRoute).\n    - [ ] To internet (if required, and as governed by org policy).\n- [ ] Verify agent can reach and register with Azure management plane endpoints.\n- [ ] Run internal diagnostics for agent communications and telemetry uploads.\n\n---\n\n### 5. **Security and Compliance**\n- [ ] Ensure all traffic is encrypted in transit.\n- [ ] NSG rules meet company security policy.\n- [ ] No unintended open ports or services in the VNet/subnet.\n- [ ] Verify compliance with company guidelines (audit logs enabled, DDoS protection, etc.).\n\n---\n\n### 6. **Monitoring & Logging**\n- [ ] Enable and validate logging for VNet, subnet, and agent service.\n- [ ] Integrate with Azure Monitor, Network Watcher, and relevant SIEM.\n- [ ] Set up alerts for connectivity failures and security breaches.\n\n---\n\n### 7. **Failover & DR Scenarios**\n- [ ] Test backup and restore procedures (for the service in VNet).\n- [ ] Validate VNet/service failover in case of regional outage (if supported).\n- [ ] Check impact of NSG/UDR changes during service lifecycle.\n\n---\n\n### 8. **Documentation & Handover**\n- [ ] Record VNet/subnet settings, all NSG/route table configurations, and related service parameters.\n- [ ] Prepare KB articles/runbooks for IT and support teams.\n\n---\n\n### 9. **Final Validation**\n- [ ] Re-run all key agent scenarios (inference, training, API calls) via VNet.\n- [ ] Repeat steps after any networking or agent configuration changes.\n\n---\n\n### 10. **Azure Support & Limitations Review**\n- [ ] Check for any agent service-specific BYOVNET caveats in updated Azure documentation (for 2025).\n- [ ] Engage with Microsoft support for unresolved connectivity or provisioning issues.\n\n---\n\nThis checklist encapsulates best practices as of 2025 and is adaptable; details may adjust based on the evolving Azure AI platform or your organizational requirements. Always validate specifics against the latest Azure Foundry Agent Service documentation and consult Microsoft product support as needed for any newly released features or changes.\n\nIf you need a **ready-to-use template** (Excel, Word, etc.) or want detailed CLI/Portal steps for any section, just ask!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

<sub>⏱️ 8m 47s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure API Management MCP server Model Context Protocol VNet private endpoint 2025**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure API Management MCP server Model Context Protocol VNet private endpoint 2025"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"It sounds like you’re interested in the **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** as it relates to **Azure API Management (APIM)**, particularly focusing on **VNet (Virtual Network) private endpoints** and their roadmap or support into 2025.\n\nLet's break down each component and provide current (2024) and potential (2025) context, drawing on what's public and what trends are likely:\n\n---\n\n### **1. Azure API Management & VNet Integration**\n\nAPIM can be deployed in the following network modes:\n- **External/VNet integration** (api management within your virtual network)\n- **Internal** (exposed only inside the VNet)\n- Supports **private endpoints** for client connections\n\nIn 2023-2024, Microsoft GA’d (Generally Available) support for **Private Link/private endpoints** for APIM, making secure network integration much easier and compliant for enterprise workloads .\n\n---\n\n### **2. MCP (Model Context Protocol) and APIM**\n\n- **MCP** is an internal Microsoft protocol for communication between management and data planes in Azure PaaS cells.\n- As of late 2023, Microsoft had already migrated most major PaaS services' management to use MCP “control planes” rather than direct REST/ARM, improving reliability, observability, and geo-replication.\n\n**For Azure API Management:**\n- MCP isn’t directly configurable by customers, but it does underpin Azure’s reliability and service awareness in private networking scenarios.\n- With VNet/private endpoints, APIM uses these (and therefore MCP-based services) for secure management, scaling, and monitoring.\n\n---\n\n### **3. Private Endpoint Support Roadmap (2024-2025)**\n\n**For 2025, expect:**\n- **Wider private endpoint coverage in APIM:** all management APIs/routes (including developer portal, gateway, and publisher portal) are moving to full private endpoint support.\n- **Deeper MCP protocol integration:** Enhanced “cellular” resilience — failover, backups, and management operations are increasingly abstracted via MCP, especially in private network scenarios, to reduce blast radius during Azure incidents.\n- **Simplified bring-your-own-network (BYON) patterns:** Enhanced VNet integration (less IP management, fewer NSG gotchas).\n\n---\n\n### **4. Best Practices & Considerations**\n\n- When configuring APIM with a private endpoint, use **managed identities** for secure management operations, as these leverage the latest MCP enhancements.\n- Keep APIM instances updated with latest features/patches — Private endpoint and MCP capabilities are often enhanced in waves.\n- Review Microsoft’s [APIM networking documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-vnet#private-endpoint-support) regularly for changes to private endpoint behavior .\n\n---\n\n### **Summary Table: VNet/Private Endpoint + MCP in APIM (2025)**\n\n| Feature                       | 2023-2024 Status      | 2025+ Outlook                     |\n|-------------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------|\n| VNet-integration (Internal)   | GA, Stable            | Minor enhancements                |\n| Private Endpoints (Gateway)   | GA, Stable            | Full API surface, role isolation  |\n| Private Endpoints (Mgmt/Portal) | GA (Mgmt); Portal preview | Full parity, RBAC, auditing     |\n| MCP protocol enhancements     | Partial, backend-only | User-facing reliability benefit   |\n| Observable APIM management    | Basic monitoring      | Advanced, MCP-driven wavelength   |\n\n---\n\n**References:**\n- [Azure APIM VNet Integration Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-vnet#private-endpoint-support) \n- [Azure Private Endpoints](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview)\n\n---\n\n**If you need step-by-step configuration or want diagrams/sample code for connecting APIM (with private endpoints on VNet) to other Azure services under MCP control plane, let me know!**","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

````
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Certainly! I'll walk you through an Azure AI Foundry **managed identity RBAC role assignment** and **private endpoint validation test** scenario. This is commonly required to ensure that your managed identity has the correct permissions to access Azure AI Foundry resources over a private endpoint—typically a key pattern for secure enterprise deployments.\n\nBelow is a **step-by-step validation test** you can use or adapt for your Dev/Test/Prod pipelines:\n\n---\n\n## Scenario\n- **Resource:** Azure AI Foundry service (e.g., Azure Machine Learning, Azure OpenAI, etc.)\n- **Access method:** Managed Identity (User-assigned or system-assigned)\n- **Security:** Private endpoint enabled\n- **Goal:** Validate managed identity has proper RBAC for AI Foundry resource via the private endpoint\n\n---\n\n## 1. Role Assignment\n\n**Step 1:** Identify the managed identity  \n(e.g., Azure VM, Web App, Function App, etc.)\n\n**Step 2:** Assign the proper RBAC role  \nFor example, to grant access to Azure AI services:\n\n```bash\naz role assignment create \\\n  --assignee <managed-identity-object-id> \\\n  --role \"Contributor\" \\\n  --scope /subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/<resource-group>/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/<ai-account>\n```\n> Use the minimum required role (e.g., \"Cognitive Services User\") for least privilege.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Private Endpoint Configuration\n\n**Step 1:** Confirm AI Foundry resource has a private endpoint connection  \nIn the Azure Portal:  \n**AI Resource** → **Networking** → **Private endpoint connections**\n\n**Step 2:** Ensure DNS routing is **correct**  \nThe resource's public DNS endpoint should resolve to the private endpoint IP from within your VNet.\n\n```powershell\nnslookup <ai-resource-name>.cognitiveservices.azure.com\n```\n*Output should return a private IP address within your VNet/subnet.*\n\n---\n\n## 3. Validation Test\n\n**From a VM or service within the same VNet with the managed identity enabled:**  \n\n### (A) Acquire a token using the Managed Identity\n\n**Using curl and Azure CLI:**\n\n```bash\n# Acquire token\nACCESS_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \\\n    --resource https://<ai-resource-name>.cognitiveservices.azure.com --query accessToken -o tsv)\n\n# Or with MSI endpoint directly (Linux VM):\nACCESS_TOKEN=$(curl -H \"Metadata: true\" \\\n    \"http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://<ai-resource-name>.cognitiveservices.azure.com\" \\\n    | jq -r '.access_token')\n```\n\n### (B) Make an API call to the AI Foundry resource\n\nFor cognitive services, for example:\n```bash\ncurl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN\" \\\n     https://<ai-resource-name>.cognitiveservices.azure.com/vision/v3.2/analyze?visualFeatures=Categories \\\n     -d @\"testimage.jpg\" -H \"Content-Type: application/octet-stream\"\n```\n\n*You should get a valid response if:*  \n- **Managed identity has proper RBAC permission**  \n- **Network traffic happens via private endpoint** (check with network monitoring tools or firewall logs)\n\n---\n\n## 4. Validation Checklist\n\n- [ ] Managed identity has correct RBAC at the Azure AI resource scope\n- [ ] Calls from managed identity traffic hit the service over the private endpoint, not the public endpoint (traffic from outside the VNet should be blocked)\n- [ ] API calls succeed (200/201 responses—not 403/401/404).\n\n---\n\n## Troubleshooting Tips\n\n- **401 Unauthorized:** Check RBAC assignment and identity used.\n- **403 Forbidden:** Might be due to network restrictions or incorrect RBAC role.\n- **No network connectivity:** Ensure NSG and route tables allow traffic to the private endpoint.\n- **DNS resolution returns public IP:** Ensure private DNS zone is correctly linked to the VNet.\n\n---\n\nIf you need an example PowerShell or ARM/Bicep template for this scenario, or want a script for automated validation, let me know!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
````

<sub>⏱️ 10m 9s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Oto plan — podsumowanie tego co chcę zrobić:

## 🎯 Copilot CLI Skill: `foundry-vnet-test`

Stworzę **reużywalny skill**, który przeprowadzi **6 faz testów** i da raport:

| Faza | Co testujemy |
|------|-------------|
| **0** | **Pre-flight** — az CLI, rejestracja providerów, quotas, region |
| **1** | **Sieć** — VNet, subnety (/26, /27), NSG, Private DNS Zones, UDR |
| **2** | **Foundry + PE** — Private Endpoints approved, DNS resolution → prywatne IP, brak public access |
| **3** | **Agent Service** — tworzenie agenta, inference w VNet, region match |
| **4** | **APIM (dev)** — VNet integration, import OpenAPI/MCP, backend connectivity |
| **5** | **Tożsamości** — Managed Identity, RBAC role assignments, token acquisition |
| **6** | **E2E + Raport** — pełny flow Client→APIM→Foundry→Backend, raport ✅❌⚠️ |

**Z researchu** znalazłem kluczowe pułapki:
- Wszystkie zasoby **muszą być w tym samym regionie** co VNet
- Managed VNet (preview) **nie zabezpiecza** outbound agentów
- APIM Internal **nie ma outbound internet** bez NAT Gateway
- Po zmianie public→private trzeba **ręcznie odświeżyć** endpointy

**Deliverables**: skill.md + 7 skryptów PowerShell + config JSON + README dla klienta.

Czy plan wygląda OK? Mogę zacząć implementację, albo chcesz coś zmienić/dodać?

<sub>⏱️ 12m 44s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry "bring your own VNet" BYOVNET subnet delegation private endpoint configuration 2025**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry \"bring your own VNet\" BYOVNET subnet delegation private endpoint configuration 2025"
}
```

</details>

````
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"To configure Azure AI Foundry's \"Bring Your Own VNet\" (BYOVNET) for subnet delegation and private endpoint setup in 2025, follow these recommended practices, based on the latest enterprise, Microsoft, and community documentation:\n\n---\n\n## 1. **Prepare Prerequisites**\n- **Existing VNet**: You must have a virtual network (VNet) with sufficient address space for at least two subnets: one for agent compute (delegated) and another for private endpoints.\n- **Permissions**: Ensure you have Owner or Role Based Access Administrator on the subscription to perform role assignments and network config.\n- **Registered Providers**: Register necessary Azure resource providers:\n  ```\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.KeyVault'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.CognitiveServices'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Storage'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.MachineLearningServices'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Search'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Network'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.App'\n  az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.ContainerService'\n  ```\n- **RBAC & Identity**: Assign `Azure AI User` RBAC roles for project users for proper access.\n\n---\n\n## 2. **Network Subnet Design**\n- **Delegated Agent Subnet**:\n  - Create a subnet (e.g., `/26` or larger for scaling) in your VNet.\n  - Delegate this subnet specifically to `Microsoft.App/environments` (for Foundry's network injection).\n- **Private Endpoints Subnet**:\n  - Create a separate subnet (e.g., `/27` or larger) for private endpoints. **Do not delegate this subnet.**\n- **DNS Configuration**: Ensure Azure private DNS zones or custom DNS resolvers are set up and linked to your VNet so that resources accessed via private endpoints resolve correctly.\n\n---\n\n## 3. **Subnet Delegation (Example using Azure CLI)**\n```bash\naz network vnet subnet create \\\n  --vnet-name MyFoundryVNet \\\n  --resource-group MyResourceGroup \\\n  --name FoundryAgentSubnet \\\n  --address-prefixes 10.1.0.0/26 \\\n  --delegations Microsoft.App/environments\n```\n\n---\n\n## 4. **Private Endpoint Deployment**\n- For each resource required by AI Foundry (Cosmos DB, Key Vault, Storage, AI Search, etc.), create a private endpoint in your designated private endpoint subnet.\n- Ensure you link the required Azure Private DNS Zones (e.g., `privatelink.blob.core.windows.net`, `privatelink.documents.azure.com`) to your VNet.\n\n---\n\n## 5. **Network Security Groups (NSGs) and Firewall**\n- Apply NSGs to both agent and private endpoint subnets. Restrict traffic to only what is required for Foundry agents and supporting services.\n- Use Azure Firewall or equivalent for controlling outbound and cross-subnet access as per your organization’s security requirements.\n\n---\n\n## 6. **Deployment Automation**\n- Microsoft provides both Bicep and Terraform templates for BYOVNET scenarios:\n  - [Bicep/Terraform Example](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet)\n- Templates will automate subnet delegation, resource creation, private endpoint setup, and RBAC assignments.\n\n---\n\n## 7. **Key Considerations & Supported Regions**\n- All resources (Foundry workspace, CosmosDB, Storage, etc.) must be in the same region as your VNet.\n- Private Class A, B, and C subnets are supported—check the latest documentation for GA region availability (Class B/C are GA in all Foundry-supported regions, Class A is GA in selected ones)【3:1†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n---\n\n## 8. **Reference Architecture**\n- The secured architecture disables public access by setting Public Network Access to \"Disabled\" and utilizes only private endpoints for resource access.\n- Network-injected agents use delegated customer subnets, maintaining full traffic isolation within your infrastructure.\n- See Microsoft official documentation for step-by-step “Agent Service with BYOVNET” setup and private networking reference architectures:\n  - [Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/how-to-agent-service-networking)【3:0†source】\n  - [Network Isolation for Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/how-to-network-isolation)【3:5†source】\n\n---\n\n**In summary:**  \n- Create a delegated subnet for agent compute (`Microsoft.App/environments`)\n- Use a separate, non-delegated subnet for private endpoints\n- Deploy required Azure Private Endpoints and configure DNS\n- Use Bicep/Terraform templates from Microsoft\n- Restrict access using NSGs and/or Azure Firewall\n- Ensure region and placement rules.\n\nFor the latest deployment templates and code examples, refer to Microsoft’s [foundry-samples GitHub repository](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet)【3:1†source】【3:7†source】【3:9†source】.","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":3549,"end_index":3561,"url_citation":{"title":"15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet/"}},{"text":"","start_index":3561,"end_index":3573,"url_citation":{"title":"mike-westaway/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet","url":"https://github.com/mike-westaway/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet"}},{"text":"","start_index":4169,"end_index":4181,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks"}},{"text":"","start_index":4304,"end_index":4316,"url_citation":{"title":"How to configure network isolation for Microsoft Foundry","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/how-to/configure-private-link"}},{"text":"","start_index":4945,"end_index":4957,"url_citation":{"title":"15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet/"}},{"text":"","start_index":4957,"end_index":4969,"url_citation":{"title":"mike-westaway/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet","url":"https://github.com/mike-westaway/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet"}},{"text":"","start_index":4969,"end_index":4981,"url_citation":{"title":"Deploy Secure Azure AI Foundry with a managed virtual network","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/samples/azure/azure-quickstart-templates/aifoundry-networking-aoao/"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"Azure AI Foundry BYOVNET subnet delegation private endpoint configuration 2025","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=Azure+AI+Foundry+BYOVNET+subnet+delegation+private+endpoint+configuration+2025"}],"annotations":null}
````

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"To bring your own virtual network (BYO VNET) for Azure AI Foundry Agent Service in 2025 and set the required subnet and Network Security Group (NSG) rules, here’s a consolidated guide based on the latest official documentation and enterprise best practices:\n\n### 1. **Subnet Requirements**\n- **Agent Subnet:**\n  - **Delegation:** The subnet must be delegated to `Microsoft.App/environments` for the Foundry Agent Services to deploy.\n  - **Size:** Minimum recommended is `/26` (64 IPs) or larger depending on your workload scaling needs.\n- **Private Endpoints Subnet:**\n  - **Purpose:** Hosts private endpoints for Foundry resources.\n  - **No Delegation:** This subnet generally doesn’t require delegation.\n  - **Size:** Minimum `/27` recommended.\n\n### 2. **NSG Rules (Network Security Groups)**\n- **Default: Deny All Inbound and Outbound**\n  - The principle is \"deny by default\" for all inbound and outbound traffic, enabling only purposeful access for necessary communication.\n- **Required Inbound Rules:**\n  - Allow inbound traffic from:\n    - **VirtualNetwork** for agent-to-agent or other resource communication as needed.\n    - Specific management IPs or service tags for monitoring or Azure services (e.g., AzureLoadBalancer, ApiManagement) if you use those integrations.\n  - E.g.,\n    - Allow `VirtualNetwork` to `VirtualNetwork` on necessary ports (e.g., application ports).\n    - Allow `ApiManagement` service tag to `VirtualNetwork` on port 3443 (if using APIM).\n    - Allow `AzureLoadBalancer` service tag to `VirtualNetwork` on required ports (APIM: 6390).\n\n- **Required Outbound Rules:**\n  - Allow outbound traffic from the agent subnet to:\n    - The specific Azure service endpoints you need (use service tags like `AzureStorage`, `AzureCosmosDB`, etc.).\n    - Your own networks or on-premises locations if access is needed.\n    - Optionally, specific internet addresses if your solution requires it (not recommended unless strictly needed with governance).\n\n### 3. **Private DNS and Endpoints**\n- Use Azure Private DNS Zones and ensure your VNET is linked to these for Azure resources (e.g., `privatelink.blob.core.windows.net`, etc.).\n- Configure Private Endpoints for each Azure service your agents interact with.\n\n### 4. **Role-Based Access and Prerequisites**\n- Assign **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** as follows:\n  - **Owner** or **Role Based Access Administrator** for resource creation and network setup.\n  - Register the following providers:\n    - `Microsoft.KeyVault`, `Microsoft.CognitiveServices`, `Microsoft.Storage`, `Microsoft.MachineLearningServices`, `Microsoft.Search`, `Microsoft.Network`, `Microsoft.App`, `Microsoft.ContainerService`【3:0†source】【3:1†source】【3:5†source】.\n\n### 5. **Key Security Considerations**\n- Firewall and NSG rules block all inbound traffic except what you explicitly allow.\n- No public egress unless you configure and approve it.\n- All communication between Azure AI Foundry Agent Service and other resources is confined to your virtual network and dedicated private endpoints.\n- For enhanced outbound controls, use Azure Firewall or a network virtual appliance to centralize inspection and logging.\n\n### 6. **Implementation**\nUse ARM/Bicep or Terraform templates provided by Microsoft or the Azure community for secure, repeatable, and policy-compliant deployments.\n\n### Helpful Links for Deeper Reference:\n- [Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service - Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/agent-private-networking)【3:0†source】\n- [Securing Microsoft Foundry with Bring Your Own VNET (Cloud & AI Platforms Blog, 2025)](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/cloud-ai-platforms-blog/securing-microsoft-foundry-with-bring-your-own-vnet/ba-p/4101681)【3:1†source】\n- [Configure Foundry Tools virtual networks - Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry-tools/vnet)【3:5†source】\n\nIf you specify your exact scenario (APIM integration, private endpoints to specific services, etc.), more tailored NSG rule examples can be provided.","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":2672,"end_index":2684,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks"}},{"text":"","start_index":2684,"end_index":2696,"url_citation":{"title":"Securing Microsoft Foundry with Bring Your Own VNET · Cloud & AI ...","url":"https://nirmalt.com/posts/securingmicrosoftfoundrywithbyovnet/"}},{"text":"","start_index":2696,"end_index":2708,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure Virtual Networks for Foundry Tools - Foundry Tools","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/cognitive-services-virtual-networks"}},{"text":"","start_index":3528,"end_index":3540,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks"}},{"text":"","start_index":3759,"end_index":3771,"url_citation":{"title":"Securing Microsoft Foundry with Bring Your Own VNET · Cloud & AI ...","url":"https://nirmalt.com/posts/securingmicrosoftfoundrywithbyovnet/"}},{"text":"","start_index":3906,"end_index":3918,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure Virtual Networks for Foundry Tools - Foundry Tools","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/cognitive-services-virtual-networks"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"Azure AI Foundry Agent Service virtual network subnet requirements NSG rules 2025","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=Azure+AI+Foundry+Agent+Service+virtual+network+subnet+requirements+NSG+rules+2025"}],"annotations":null}
```

<sub>⏱️ 14m 47s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Poprawiony plan — **wyłącznie BYO VNet**, zero Managed VNet:

## 🎯 Skill: `foundry-vnet-test` (BYO VNet)

**Architektura do przetestowania:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Customer BYO VNet                              │
│                                                 │
│  Agent Subnet /26          PE Subnet /27        │
│  delegacja:                bez delegacji         │
│  Microsoft.App/            PE: OpenAI, Storage, │
│    environments            KeyVault, CosmosDB,  │
│  (Foundry Agent injected)  AI Search            │
│                                                 │
│  APIM Subnet /27                                │
│  APIM Dev (Internal mode)                       │
│                                                 │
│  + 7 Private DNS Zones linked                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

| Faza | Co testujemy |
|------|-------------|
| **0** | **Pre-flight** — az CLI, 8 providerów, Owner/RBAC Admin, region match |
| **1** | **BYO VNet** — agent subnet + delegacja `Microsoft.App/environments`, PE subnet bez delegacji, APIM subnet, NSG (port 3443, 6390), 7 DNS Zones linked, UDR |
| **2** | **Private Endpoints** — status Approved/Succeeded, DNS A-records → prywatne IP, public access disabled |
| **3** | **Agent Service** — network injection w BYO VNet, inference test, backend connectivity |
| **4** | **APIM Dev** — VNet Internal mode, OpenAPI import, MCP endpoint, connectivity → Foundry |
| **5** | **Tożsamości** — MI + RBAC (5 ról), token acquisition, brak key-based auth |
| **6** | **E2E + Raport** — full flow + raport ✅❌⚠️ + GO/NO-GO |

**Kluczowe BYO VNet wymagania** (znalezione w docs):
- Agent subnet **musi mieć** delegację `Microsoft.App/environments`
- PE subnet **nie może** mieć delegacji
- Wszystko **w jednym regionie**
- NSG: deny-all + explicit allow dla service tags

Wygląda OK? Mogę zaczynać implementację?

````
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"To deploy Microsoft (Azure) AI Foundry's Agent Service in a **BYO VNet (Bring Your Own Virtual Network)** configuration with private endpoints using a **Bicep template** in 2025, you can leverage official open-source Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) samples and best practices from Microsoft.\n\n### Key Resources and Steps\n\n#### 1. **Reference Bicep Repo and Template**\n- **GitHub Source**: Use the [foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup) directory for a comprehensive, up-to-date Bicep solution for Foundry Agent with private networking【3:1†source】【3:5†source】.\n- These templates are regularly updated and align with the 2025/2026 platform (the new Foundry model with `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts`).\n\n#### 2. **What This Sets Up**\n- **Virtual Network (BYO VNet)**: Your own VNet with delegated subnets for the Agent Service.\n- **Private Endpoints**: For Azure AI services (Agents), Storage, Search, Key Vault, and more.\n- **Subnets**: Separate subnets are set up for agents, private endpoints, and optionally for jumpboxes or admin access.\n- **Private DNS Zones**: For name resolution of internal Azure services.\n- **RBAC and Security**: Role assignments and network rules to ensure only private access.\n\n#### 3. **How to Deploy**\n- **Clone the repo**:\n  ```bash\n  git clone https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples.git\n  cd foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup\n  ```\n- **Customize Parameters**: Edit the `.bicepparam` file as needed — set your VNet, subnet, and resource names.\n- **Deploy** using Azure CLI:\n  ```bash\n  az deployment group create \\\n    --resource-group <your-rg> \\\n    --template-file main.bicep \\\n    --parameters @main.bicepparam\n  ```\n\n#### 4. **Supported Regions and Subnet Classes**\n- Class A, B, and C subnets are supported in various regions (e.g., East US, West Europe, Japan East — see the template README for the latest list).\n\n#### 5. **Architecture Example**\n- The Bicep templates will provision:\n  - Azure VNet with custom address space/subnets\n  - Private endpoints for Storage, Key Vault, AI Project, etc.\n  - Private DNS zones and links to your VNet\n  - Agent service with network injection and isolation\n\n#### 6. **Documentation & Learn More**\n- **Official Sample and Deep Dives**:\n  - [README and full deployment steps](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)\n  - [Microsoft Learn - Deploy Secure Foundry with Bicep](https://learn.microsoft.com/samples/azure/azure-ai-studio-secure-bicep/)\n  - [Architecture deep wiki explainer](https://deepwiki.ai/wiki/azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples/Private-Network-Setup)【3:3†source】【3:6†source】【3:8†source】.\n\n---\n\n### **Summary**\n- Use the [GitHub samples](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup) for a BYO VNet, private endpoint, agent service Bicep deployment.\n- These are the latest, officially maintained IaC patterns for enterprise-ready, network-isolated Foundry environments. Simply clone, parameterize, and deploy for a secure, private setup in 2025 and beyond.\n\n**Quick links to get started:**\n- [GitHub: 15-private-network-standard-agent-setup Bicep](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)【3:1†source】【3:5†source】\n- [Microsoft Learn: Secure Foundry Deploy (Bicep)](https://learn.microsoft.com/samples/azure/azure-ai-studio-secure-bicep/)【3:6†source】\n\nIf you need a starter `main.bicep` or `main.bicepparam` sample, just ask!","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":738,"end_index":750,"url_citation":{"title":"foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/blob/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup/README.md"}},{"text":"","start_index":750,"end_index":762,"url_citation":{"title":"foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep at main ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/"}},{"text":"","start_index":2935,"end_index":2947,"url_citation":{"title":"Private Network Setup | azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples/4.3-private-network-setup"}},{"text":"","start_index":2947,"end_index":2959,"url_citation":{"title":"Deploy Secure Azure AI Foundry via Bicep - {"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"If you are looking for Bicep-based samples that demonstrate setting up a private network environment for Azure AI Foundry (now Microsoft Foundry) using a \"Bring Your Own Virtual Network\" (BYO VNet) approach, the official Microsoft Foundry Samples repository on GitHub provides detailed examples.\n\n### Key Locations in the Repository\n\n- **Standard Agent Setup with Private Network and BYO VNet (Bicep):**\n  - [15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)\n    - This directory demonstrates how to set up an Azure AI Agent Service with end-to-end network isolation, using your own VNet (BYO VNet) and subnet delegation. It includes Bicep files (`main.bicep`, `add-project.bicep`) and instructions in the [README.md](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/blob/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup/README.md)【3:0†source】.\n\n- **Basic Private Network Deployment Example (Bicep):**\n  - [10-private-network-basic](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/10-private-network-basic)\n    - This is a simpler example, focusing on deploying a Foundry resource with private endpoint and public network access disabled. Includes `main.bicep` and a [README.md](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/blob/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/10-private-network-basic/README.md)【3:1†source】.\n\n#### BYO VNet Details\nThe *standard agent setup* sample supports:\n- Deploying with an existing, user-supplied VNet (BYO VNet) and delegated subnet (including instructions and parameters for customization).\n- Full control over network isolation, private endpoint connectivity, and RBAC.\n- Works for Class A, B, and C subnets (with regional support).\n\n#### Repository Root\n- **GitHub Repo:** [microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples)【3:4†source】.\n\n### Getting Started\n1. **Prepare or select a resource group** in Azure.\n2. **Deploy the provided Bicep sample** (`main.bicep`) from the relevant directory. Instructions are in the accompanying README file.\n3. **Customize parameters** as needed for your VNet/subnet setup.\n\nYou can browse further directories in the repository for advanced setups, hybrid environments, or integration with APIM, managed identities, and more. If you want a direct template for BYO VNet, start with the [15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup) folder.\n\n> **Note:** There is also a Terraform equivalent at `infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet`, but your query specifically asked for Bicep【3:5†source】.\n\nIf you need instructions or a code snippet from a specific file, let me know!","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":1010,"end_index":1022,"url_citation":{"title":"foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/blob/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup/README.md"}},{"text":"","start_index":1555,"end_index":1567,"url_citation":{"title":"foundry-samples/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/10-private ...","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/10-private-network-basic"}},{"text":"","start_index":2049,"end_index":2061,"url_citation":{"title":"Embedded samples in Azure AI Foundry docs - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples"}},{"text":"","start_index":2937,"end_index":2949,"url_citation":{"title":"15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-terraform/15b-private-network-standard-agent-setup-byovnet/"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"microsoft-foundry foundry-samples bicep private-network byovnet site:github.com","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=microsoft-foundry+foundry-samples+bicep+private-network+byovnet+site%3Agithub.com"}],"annotations":null}
````

**Azure API Management developer VNet internal mode Bicep template private endpoint 2025**

```json
  "query": "Azure API Management developer VNet internal mode Bicep template private endpoint 2025"
}
```

</details>

````
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Certainly! To **deploy an Azure API Management (APIM) instance in Developer SKU, using VNet Internal mode, with a private endpoint, using Bicep (2025-relevant syntax)**, you’ll need:\n\n- An **Azure VNet** and subnet for APIM\n- The **APIM resource** with `virtualNetworkType: \"Internal\"`\n- A **Private Endpoint** for APIM’s Gateway\n- Required **DNS configuration** is normally handled outside Bicep, but the outputs can help guide DNS setup.\n\nHere’s a streamlined and updated Bicep template, reflecting 2025 best practices:\n\n---\n\n### Parameters\n\n```bicep\nparam location string = resourceGroup().location\nparam apimServiceName string\nparam publisherEmail string\nparam publisherName string\nparam vnetName string\nparam subnetName string\nparam apimSku string = 'Developer'\nparam apimApiVersion string = '2022-08-01' // Use latest available if newer\n\n```\n\n---\n\n### Variables\n\n```bicep\nvar apimSubnetId = resourceId('Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets', vnetName, subnetName)\nvar privateEndpointName = '${apimServiceName}-pe'\n```\n\n---\n\n### APIM Resource\n\n```bicep\nresource apim 'Microsoft.ApiManagement/service@${apimApiVersion}' = {\n  name: apimServiceName\n  location: location\n  sku: {\n    name: apimSku\n    capacity: 1\n  }\n  publisherEmail: publisherEmail\n  publisherName: publisherName\n  virtualNetworkType: 'Internal'\n  virtualNetworkConfiguration: {\n    subnetResourceId: apimSubnetId\n  }\n  identity: {\n    type: 'SystemAssigned'\n  }\n  tags: {\n    Environment: 'Dev'\n  }\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### Private Endpoint\n\n```bicep\nresource privateEndpoint 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints@2021-08-01' = {\n  name: privateEndpointName\n  location: location\n  properties: {\n    subnet: {\n      id: apimSubnetId\n    }\n    privateLinkServiceConnections: [\n      {\n        name: 'apimConnection'\n        properties: {\n          privateLinkServiceId: apim.id\n          groupIds: [\n            'gateway'\n          ]\n        }\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### Private DNS Zone Link (Optional but Recommended)\n\nThis is for making the private endpoint discoverable; DNS zone should be created/linked for proper name resolution.\n\n```bicep\nresource privateDnsZone 'Microsoft.Network/privateDnsZones@2020-06-01' = {\n  name: 'privatelink.azure-api.net'\n  location: 'global'\n}\n\nresource dnsZoneVnetLink 'Microsoft.Network/privateDnsZones/virtualNetworkLinks@2020-06-01' = {\n  name: '${privateDnsZone.name}-link'\n  parent: privateDnsZone\n  location: 'global'\n  properties: {\n    virtualNetwork: {\n      id: resourceId('Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks', vnetName)\n    }\n    registrationEnabled: false\n  }\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n### Outputs\n\n```bicep\noutput apimResourceId string = apim.id\noutput privateEndpointId string = privateEndpoint.id\noutput dnsZoneName string = privateDnsZone.name\n```\n\n---\n\n**NOTES:**\n1. **Assign APIM to a subnet** that is dedicated (no other resources in the same subnet).\n2. Make sure you have proper **IMDS, NSG, and UDR rules** for private connectivity.\n3. For real-world deployments, APIM needs permissions to join the subnet. Also, assign Azure RBAC as needed.\n4. Replace the versions with the newest available if necessary.\n\n**Need parameter file, more DNS details, or full ARM-extracted Bicep?** Just ask!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
````

1. successfully downloaded text file (SHA: 9bffa6610aba06bbdc9da4ece16644a17ec8d680)---
2. description: This set of templates demonstrates how to set up Azure AI Agent Service with virtual network isolation with private network links to connect the agent to your secure data.
3. page_type: sample
4. products:
5. - azure
6. - azure-resource-manager
7. urlFragment: network-secured-agent
8. languages:
9. - bicep
10. - json
11. ---
12. 
13. # Azure AI Agent Service: Standard Agent Setup with E2E Network Isolation
14. 
15. > **IMPORTANT**
16. > 
17. > Private Class A subnet support is GA and available in the following regions. **Supported regions: Australia East, Brazil South, Canada East, East US, East US 2, France Central, Germany West Central, Italy North, Japan East, South Africa North, South Central US, South India, Spain Central, Sweden Central, UAE North, UK South, West Europe, West US, West US 3.**
18. >
19. > Private Class B and C subnet support is already GA and available in all regions supported by Azure AI Foundry Agent Service. Deployment templates and setup steps are identical for Class A, B, and C subnets. For more on the supported regions of the Azure AI Foundry Agent service, see [Models supported by Azure AI Foundry Agent Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/agents/concepts/model-region-support?tabs=global-standard)
20. 
21. ---
22. ## Overview
23. This infrastructure-as-code (IaC) solution deploys a network-secured Azure AI agent environment with private networking and role-based access control (RBAC).
24. 
25. Standard setup supports private network isolation through utilizing **Bring Your Own Virtual Network (BYO VNet)** approach, also known as **custom VNet support with subnet delegation.** 
26. 
27. This implementation gives you full control over the inbound and outbound communication paths for your agent. You can restrict access to only the resources explicitly required by your agent, such as storage accounts, databases, or APIs, while blocking all other traffic by default. This approach ensures that your agent operates within a tightly scoped network boundary, reducing the risk of data leakage or unauthorized access. By default, this setup simplifies security configuration while enforcing strong isolation guarantees, ensuring that each agent deployment remains secure, compliant, and aligned with enterprise networking policies. 
28. 
29. ---
30. 
31. ## Key Information
32. 
33. **Region and Resource Placement Requirements**
34. - **All Foundry workspace resources should be in the same region as the VNet**, including CosmosDB, Storage Account, AI Search, Foundry Account, Project, Managed Identity. The only exception is within the Foundry Account, you may choose to deploy your model to a different region, and any cross-region communication will be handled securely within our network infrastructure.
35.   - **Note:** Your Virtual Network can be in a different resource group than your Foundry workspace resources 
36. 
37. 
38. [![Deploy To Azure](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/master/1-CONTRIBUTION-GUIDE/images/deploytoazure.svg?sanitize=true)](https://portal.azure.com/#create/Microsoft.Template/uri/https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Fazure-ai-foundry%2Ffoundry-samples%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Finfrastructure%2Finfrastructure-setup-bicep%2F15-private-network-standard-agent-setup%2Fazuredeploy.json)
39. 
40. ---
41. 
42. ## Prerequisites
43. 
44. 1. **Active Azure subscription with appropriate permissions**
45.    - **Azure AI Account Owner**: Needed to create a cognitive services account and project 
46.    - **Owner or Role Based Access Administrator**: Needed to assign RBAC to the required resources (Cosmos DB, Azure AI Search, Storage) 
47.    - **Azure AI User**: Needed to create and edit agents
48. 
49. 1. **Register Resource Providers**
50. 
51.    Make sure you have an active Azure subscription that allows registering resource providers. For example, subnet delegation requires the Microsoft.App provider to be registered in your subscription. If it's not already registered, run the commands below:
52. 
53.    ```bash
54.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.KeyVault'
55.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.CognitiveServices'
56.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Storage'
57.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Search'
58.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.Network'
59.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.App'
60.    az provider register --namespace 'Microsoft.ContainerService'
61.    ```
62. 
63. 1. Network administrator permissions (if operating in a restricted or enterprise environment)
64. 
65. 1. Sufficient quota for all resources in your target Azure region
66.     * If no parameters are passed in, this template creates an Azure AI Foundry resource, Foundry project, Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL, Azure AI Search, and Azure Storage account
67. 1. Azure CLI installed and configured on your local workstation or deployment pipeline server
68. 
69. ---
70. 
71. ## Pre-Deployment Steps
72. 
73. ### Networking Requirements
74. 1. Review network requirements and plan Virtual Network address space (e.g., 192.168.0.0/16 or an alternative non-overlapping address space)
75. 
76. 2. Two subnets are needed as well:  
77.     - **Agent Subnet** (e.g., 192.168.0.0/24): Hosts Agent client for Agent workloads, delegated to Microsoft.App/environments. The recommended size should be /24 for this delegated subnet. 
78.     - **Private endpoint Subnet** (e.g. 192.168.1.0/24): Hosts private endpoints 
79.     - Ensure that the address spaces for the used VNET does not overlap with any existing networks in your Azure environment or reserved IP ranges like the following: 169.254.0.0/16,172.30.0.0/16,172.31.0.0/16,192.0.2.0/24,0.0.0.0/8,127.0.0.0/8,100.100.0.0/17,100.100.192.0/19,100.100.224.0/19,100.64.0.0/11.
80.     This includes all address space(s) you have in your VNET if you have more than one, and peered VNETs.
81.   
82.   > **Notes:** 
83.   - If you do not provide an existing virtual network, the template will create a new virtual network with the default address spaces and subnets described above. If you use an existing virtual network, make sure it already contains two subnets (Agent and Private Endpoint) before deploying the template.
84.   - You must ensure the Foundry account was successfully created so that underlying caphost has also succeeded. Then proceed to deploying the project caphost bicep. 
85.   - You must ensure the subnet is not already in use by another account. It must be an exclusive subnet for the Foundry account.
86.   - You must ensure the subnet is exclusively delegated to __Microsoft.App/environments__ and cannot be used by any other Azure resources.
87.   
88. 
89. ### Account Deletion Prerequisites and Cleanup Guidance
90. 
91. Before deleting an **Account** resource, it is essential to first delete the associated **Account Capability Host**. Failure to do so may result in residual dependencies—such as subnets and other provisioned resources (e.g., ACA applications)—remaining linked to the capability host. This can lead to errors such as **"Subnet already in use"** when attempting to reuse the same subnet in a different account deployment.
92. 
93. **Cleanup Options**
94. 
95. **1. Full Account Removal**: To completely remove an account, you must delete and purge the account. Simply deleting the account is not sufficient, you must purge so that deletion of the associated capability host is triggered. The service will automatically handle the removal of the capability host and any linked resources in the background. To purge the account, use the following [link](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/recover-purge-resources?tabs=azure-portal#purge-a-deleted-resource). Please allow approximately max of 20 minutes for all resources to be fully unlinked from the account.
96.  
97. **2. Retain Account, Remove Capability Host**: If you intend to retain the account but remove the capability host, execute the script `deleteCaphost.sh` located in this folder. After deletion, allow approximately max of 20 minutes for all resources to be fully unlinked from the account. To recreate the capability host for the account, use the script `createCaphost.sh` located in the same folder.
98. 
99. 
100. > **Important**: Before deleting the account capability host, ensure that the **project capability host** is deleted.
101. 
102. ### Template Customization
103. 
104. Note: If not provided, the following resources will be created automatically for you:
105. - VNet and two subnets
106. - Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL  
107. - Azure AI Search
108. - Azure Storage
109. 
110. #### Parameters
111. 
112. 1. **Use Existing Virtual Network and Subnets**
113. 
114. To use an existing VNet and subnets, set the existingVnetResourceId parameter to the full Azure Resource ID of the target VNet and its address range, and provide the names of the two required subnets.  If the existing VNet is associated with private DNS zones, set the existingDnsZones parameter to the resource group name in which the zones are located. For example:
115. - param existingVnetResourceId = "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/<resource-group-name>/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/<vnet-name>"
116. - param agentSubnetName string = 'agent-subnet' //optional, default is 'agent-subnet'
117. - param agentSubnetPrefix string = '192.168.0.0/24' //optional, default is '192.168.0.0/24'
118. - param peSubnetName string = 'pe-subnet' //optional, default is 'pe-subnet'
119. - param peSubnetPrefix string = '192.168.1.0/24' //optional, default is '192.168.1.0/24'
120. - param dnsZonesSubscriptionId string = '' //optional, leave empty to use current subscription, or set to a subscription ID if DNS zones are in a different subscription
````

</details>

```
successfully downloaded text file (SHA: 20f1eaaad431b537730835920a68da1f38b61320)using './main.bicep'

param location = 'westus'
param aiServices = 'foundry'
param modelName = 'gpt-4.1'
param modelFormat = 'OpenAI'
param modelVersion = '2025-04-14'
param modelSkuName = 'GlobalStandard'
param modelCapacity = 30
param firstProjectName = 'project'
param projectDescription = 'A project for the AI Foundry account with network secured deployed Agent'
param displayName = 'project'
param peSubnetName = 'pe-subnet'

// Resource IDs for existing resources
// If you provide these, the deployment will use the existing resources instead of creating new ones
param existingVnetResourceId = ''
param vnetName = 'agent-vnet-test'
param agentSubnetName = 'agent-subnet'
param aiSearchResourceId = ''
param azureStorageAccountResourceId = ''
param azureCosmosDBAccountResourceId = ''

// Subscription ID where DNS zones are located (leave empty to use deployment subscription)
// ⚠️ If set to a different subscription, ALL zones below MUST have resource groups specified
param dnsZonesSubscriptionId = ''

// DNS zone map: provide resource group name to use existing zone, or leave empty to create new
// Note: Empty values only allowed when dnsZonesSubscriptionId is empty or matches current subscription
param existingDnsZones = {
  'privatelink.services.ai.azure.com': ''
  'privatelink.openai.azure.com': ''
  'privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com': ''               
  'privatelink.search.windows.net': ''           
  'privatelink.blob.core.windows.net': ''                            
  'privatelink.documents.azure.com': ''                       
}

//DNSZones names for validating if they exist
param dnsZoneNames = [
  'privatelink.services.ai.azure.com'
  'privatelink.openai.azure.com'
  'privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com'
  'privatelink.search.windows.net'
  'privatelink.blob.core.windows.net'
  'privatelink.documents.azure.com'
]


// Network configuration (behavior depends on `existingVnetResourceId`)
//
// - NEW VNet (existingVnetResourceId is empty):
//     The values below are used to CREATE the VNet and the two subnets.
//     Provide explicit, non-overlapping CIDR ranges when creating a new VNet.
//
// - EXISTING VNet (existingVnetResourceId is provided):
//     The module will reference the existing VNet. Subnet handling depends on the
//     values you provide:
//       * If `agentSubnetPrefix` or `peSubnetPrefix` are empty, the module may
//         auto-derive subnet CIDRs from the existing VNet's address space
//         (using cidrSubnet). This can produce /24 (or configured) subnets
//         starting at index 0, 1, etc.
//       * If you provide explicit subnet prefixes, the module will attempt to
//         create or update subnets with those prefixes in the existing VNet.
//
// Important operational notes and risks (when existingVnetResourceId is provided):
// - Avoid CIDR overlaps with any existing subnets in the target VNet. Overlap
//   leads to `NetcfgSubnetRangesOverlap` and failed deployments.
// - For highest safety when using an existing VNet, supply the existing `agentSubnetPrefix` and `peSubnetPrefix`. 
param vnetAddressPrefix = ''
param agentSubnetPrefix = ''
param peSubnetPrefix = ''
```

120. - param dnsZonesSubscriptionId string = '' //optional, leave empty to use current subscription, or set to a subscription ID if DNS zones are in a different subscription
121. - param existingDnsZones = {
122.        
123.          'privatelink.services.ai.azure.com': 'privzoneRG' //add resource group name where your private DNS zone is located
124.        
125.          'privatelink.openai.azure.com': '' //Leave empty to create new private dns zone... }
126. 
127. 💡 If subnets information is provided then make sure it exist within the specified VNet to avoid deployment errors. If subnet information is not provided, the template will create subnets with the default address space.
128. 
129. 💡 **Cross-Subscription DNS Zones**: All DNS zones specified in `existingDnsZones` will be referenced from the subscription specified in `dnsZonesSubscriptionId`. Leave this parameter empty (default) to use the current deployment subscription, or set it to a subscription ID if your DNS zones are located in a different subscription.
130. 
131. ⚠️ **Important**: When `dnsZonesSubscriptionId` is set to a different subscription, ALL DNS zones in `existingDnsZones` must have resource groups specified (non-empty values). The template does not support creating new DNS zones in a different subscription. Empty resource groups are only allowed when creating zones in the current deployment subscription.
132. 
133. 
134. 2. **Use an existing Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL**
135. 
136. To use an existing Cosmos DB for NoSQL resource, set cosmosDBResourceId parameter to the full Azure Resource ID of the target Cosmos DB.
137. - param azureCosmosDBAccountResourceId string =  /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/{cosmosDbAccountName}
138. 
139. > **⚠️ Important: Cosmos DB Connection Requirements**
140. >
141. > When creating the Cosmos DB connection (e.g., via REST API or ARM), ensure the following:
142. > - The `authType` **must** be set to `AAD`. This is the only supported authentication type for the Cosmos DB connection used by the Agent Service.
143. > - The `metadata` section **must** include the `ResourceId` property, set to the full Azure Resource ID of your Cosmos DB account. The Agent Service relies on this property to correctly identify and connect to your Cosmos DB resource. Omitting `ResourceId` from the metadata will cause the connection to fail.
144. >
145. > Example connection properties:
146. > ```json
147. > {
148. >   "category": "CosmosDB",
149. >   "authType": "AAD",
150. >   "metadata": {
151. >     "ApiType": "Azure",
152. >     "ResourceId": "/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/{cosmosDbAccountName}",
153. >     "location": "{region}"
154. >   }
155. > }
156. > ```
157. 
158. 
159. 3. **Use an existing Azure AI Search resource**
160. 
161. To use an existing Azure AI Search resource, set aiSearchServiceResourceId parameter to the full Azure resource Id of the target Azure AI Search resource. 
162.  - param aiSearchResourceId string = /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Search/searchServices/{searchServiceName}
163. 
164. 
165. 4. **Use an existing Azure Storage account**
166. 
167. To use an existing Azure Storage account, set aiStorageAccountResourceId parameter to the full Azure resource Id of the target Azure Storage account resource. 
168. - param aiStorageAccountResourceId string = /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/{storageAccountName}
169. 
170. ---
171. 
172. ## Deploy the bicep template
173. 
174. Choose your deployment method: Use the "Deploy to Azure" button from the provided README for an guided experience in Azure Portal
175. 
176. **Option 1: Automatic deployment** 
177. Click the deploy to Azure button above to open the Azure portal and deploy the template directly. 
178. - Fill in the parameters as needed, including the existing VNet and subnets if applicable. 
179. 
180. 
181. **Option 2: Manually deploy the bicep template**
182. - **Create a New (or Use Existing) Resource Group**
183. 
184.    ```bash
185.    az group create --name <new-rg-name> --location <your-rg-region>
186.    ```
187. - Deploy the main.bicep file
188.   - Edit the main.bicepparams file to use an existing Virtual Network & subnets, Azure Cosmos DB, Azure Storage, and Azure AI Search.
189. 
190.    ```bash
191.       az deployment group create --resource-group <your-resource-group> --template-file main.bicep --parameters main.bicepparam
192.    ```
193. 
194. > **Note:** To access your Foundry resource securely, use either a VM, VPN, or ExpressRoute.
195. 
196. ---  
197. 
198. ## Network Secured Agent Project Architecture Deep Dive
199. 
200. ### Core Components
201. 
202. **Azure AI Foundry** resource
203. - Central orchestration point
204. - Manages service connections
205. - Set networking and policy configurations
206. 
207. **Foundry** project
208. - Defines the workspace configuration 
209. - Service integration 
210. - Agents are created within a specific project, and each project acts as an isolated workspace. This means:
211.   - All agents in the same project share access to the same file storage, thread storage (conversation history), and search indexes.
212.   - Data is isolated between projects. Agents in one project cannot access resources from another. Projects are currently the unit of  sharing and isolation in Foundry. See the what is AI foundry article for more information on Foundry projects. 
213. 
214. **Bring Your Own (BYO) Azure Resources**: ensures all sensitive data remains under customer control. All agents created using our service are stateful, meaning they retain information across interactions. With this setup, agent states are automatically stored in customer-managed, single-tenant resources. The required Bring Your Own Resources include: 
215. - BYO File Storage: All files uploaded by developers (during agent configuration) or end-users (during interactions) are stored directly in the customer’s Azure Storage account.
216. - BYO Search: All vector stores created by the agent leverage the customer’s Azure AI Search resource.
217. - BYO Thread Storage: All customer messages and conversation history will be stored in the customer’s own Azure Cosmos DB account.
218. 
219. By bundling these BYO features (file storage, search, and thread storage), the standard setup guarantees that your deployment is secure by default. All data processed by Azure AI Foundry Agent Service is automatically stored at rest in your own Azure resources, helping you meet internal policies, compliance requirements, and enterprise security standards.
220. 
221. ### Azure Resources Created
222. 
223. Azure AI Foundry (Cognitive Services)
224. - Type: Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts
225. - API version: 2025-04-01-preview
226. - Kind: AIServices
227. - SKU: S0
228. - Identity: System-assigned
229. - Features:
230.   - Custom subdomain name
231.   - Disabled public network access
232.   - Network ACLs with Azure Services bypass 
233. 
234. AI Model Deployment 
235. - Type: Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments 
236. - API version: 2025-04-01-preview
237. - SKU: Based on modelSkuName parameter, capacity set by modelCapacity 
238. - Model properties:
239.   - Name: From modelName parameter
240.   - Format: From modelFormat parameter
241.   - Version: From modelVersion parameter 
242. 
243. Azure AI Search 
244. - Type: Microsoft.Search/searchServices
245. - API version: 2024-06-01-preview
246. - SKU: standard 
247. - Partition Count: 1 
248. - Replica Count: 1 
249. - Hosting Mode: default 
250. - Semantic Search: disabled
````

</details>
