278. 
279. ### Network Security Design
280. This implementation utilizes a BYO VNet (Bring Your Own Virtual Network) approach, also known as custom VNet support with subnet delegation. Within your existing virtual network, one delegated subnet will be created.
281. 
282. Network Security
283. - Public network access disabled
284. - Private endpoints for all services
285. - Network ACLs with deny by default
286. 
287. **Network Infrastructure**
288. - A Virtual Network (192.168.0.0/16) is created (if existing isn't passed in)
289. - Agent Subnet (192.168.0.0/24): Hosts Agent client
290. - Private endpoint Subnet (192.168.1.0/24): Hosts private endpoints
291. 
292. **Private Endpoints** 
293. Private endpoints ensure secure, internal-only connectivity. Private endpoints are created for the following:
294. - Azure AI Foundry
295. - Azure AI Search
296. - Azure Storage
297. - Azure Cosmos DB
298. 
299. **Private DNS Zones**
300. | Private Link Resource Type | Sub Resource | Private DNS Zone Name | Public DNS Zone Forwarders |
301. |----------------------------|--------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
302. | **Azure AI Foundry**       | account      | `privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com`<br>`privatelink.openai.azure.com`<br>`privatelink.services.ai.azure.com` | `cognitiveservices.azure.com`<br>`openai.azure.com`<br>`services.ai.azure.com` |
303. | **Azure AI Search**        | searchService| `privatelink.search.windows.net` | `search.windows.net` |
304. | **Azure Cosmos DB**        | Sql          | `privatelink.documents.azure.com` | `documents.azure.com` |
305. | **Azure Storage**          | blob         | `privatelink.blob.core.windows.net` | `blob.core.windows.net` |
306. 
307. ### Authentication & Authorization
308. 
309. - **Managed Identity**
310.   - Zero-trust security model
311.   - No credential storage
312.   - Platform-managed rotation
313. 
314.   This template uses System Managed Identity, but User Assigned Managed Identity is also supported.
315. 
316. - **Role Assignments**
317.   - **Azure AI Search**
318.     - Search Index Data Contributor (`8ebe5a00-799e-43f5-93ac-243d3dce84a7`)
319.     - Search Service Contributor (`7ca78c08-252a-4471-8644-bb5ff32d4ba0`)
320.   - **Azure Storage Account**
321.     - Storage Blob Data Owner (`b7e6dc6d-f1e8-4753-8033-0f276bb0955b`)
322.     - Storage Queue Data Contributor (`974c5e8b-45b9-4653-ba55-5f855dd0fb88`) (if Azure Function tool enabled)
323.     - Two containers will automatically be provisioned during the project create capability host process:
324.       - Azure Blob Storage Container: `<workspaceId>-azureml-blobstore`
325.         - Storage Blob Data Contributor
326.       - Azure Blob Storage Container: `<workspaceId>-agents-blobstore`
327.         - Storage Blob Data Owner
328.   - **Cosmos DB for NoSQL**
329.     - Cosmos DB Operator (`230815da-be43-4aae-9cb4-875f7bd000aa`)
330.     - Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor
331.     - Three containers will automatically be provisioned during the create capability host process:
332.       - Cosmos DB for NoSQL container: `<${projectWorkspaceId}>-thread-message-store`
333.       - Cosmos DB for NoSQL container: `<${projectWorkspaceId}>-system-thread-message-store`
334.       - Cosmos DB for NoSQL container: `<${projectWorkspaceId}>-agent-entity-store`
335. 
336. 
337. ---
338. 
339. ## Module Structure
340. 
341. ```text
342. modules-network-secured/
343. ├── add-project-capability-host.bicep               # Configuring the project's capability host
344. ├── ai-account-identity.bicep                       # Azure AI Foundry deployment and configuration
345. ├── ai-project-identity.bicep                       # Foundry project deployment and connection configuration           
346. ├── ai-search-role-assignments.bicep                # AI Search RBAC configuration
347. ├── azure-storage-account-role-assignments.bicep    # Storage Account RBAC configuration  
348. ├── blob-storage-container-role-assignments.bicep   # Blob Storage Container RBAC configuration
349. ├── cosmos-container-role-assignments.bicep         # CosmosDB container Account RBAC configuration
350. ├── cosmosdb-account-role-assignment.bicep          # CosmosDB Account RBAC configuration
351. ├── existing-vnet.bicep                             # Bring your existing virtual network to template deployment
352. ├── format-project-workspace-id.bicep               # Formatting the project workspace ID
353. ├── network-agent-vnet.bicep                        # Logic for routing virtual network set-up if existing virtual network is selected
354. ├── private-endpoint-and-dns.bicep                  # Creating virtual networks and DNS zones. 
355. ├── standard-dependent-resources.bicep              # Deploying CosmosDB, Storage, and Search
356. ├── subnet.bicep                                    # Setting the subnet for Agent network injection
357. ├── validate-existing-resources.bicep               # Validate existing CosmosDB, Storage, and Search to template deployment
358. └── vnet.bicep                                      # Deploying a new virtual network
359. ```
360. 
361. > **Note:** If you bring your own VNET for this template, ensure the subnet for Agents has the correct subnet delegation to `Microsoft.App/environments`. If you have not specified the delegated subnet, the template will complete this for you.
362. 
363. ## Maintenance
364. 
365. ### Regular Tasks
366. 
367. 1. Review role assignments
368. 2. Monitor network security
369. 3. Check service health
370. 4. Update configurations as needed
371. 
372. ### Troubleshooting
373. 
374. 1. Verify private endpoint connectivity
375. 2. Check DNS resolution
376. 3. Validate role assignments
377. 4. Review network security groups
378. 
379. ---
380. ---
381. # (Optional) Adding Multiple Projects to AI Foundry Deployment
382. 
383. This guide explains how to add additional projects to your existing AI Foundry deployment with network security and capability hosts.
384. 
385. ## Overview
386. 
387. After deploying your initial AI Foundry setup using `main.bicep`, you can add additional projects using the modular approach provided in this repository. Each new project will:
388. 
389. - ✅ **Reuse existing shared infrastructure** (AI Services account, Storage, Cosmos DB, AI Search, VNet)
390. - ✅ **Create independent projects** with unique identities and connections
391. - ✅ **Set up proper role assignments** and capability hosts for each project
392. - ✅ **Maintain network security** configurations from your original deployment
393. - ✅ **Deploy independently** without affecting existing projects
394. 
395. ## Files Added
396. 
397. ### Core Deployment Files
398. 
399. | File | Purpose |
400. |------|---------|
````

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 18m 58s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Gotowe. Oto wyjaśnienie co i jak:

---

## 📋 Jak to będzie działać

### 1️⃣ Bicep deployuje infrastrukturę
Bazujemy na **oficjalnym MS template** ([foundry-samples/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)) — jest to **oficjalnie wspierany** template do BYO VNet. Dodajemy do niego **moduł APIM**.

```
az deployment group create --template-file main.bicep    ← Foundry + BYO VNet + PE + RBAC
az deployment group create --template-file apim.bicep    ← APIM Dev (Internal) + subnet
az apim api import ...                                   ← OpenAPI + MCP import
```

### 2️⃣ Azure CLI testuje po deploy'u
Każdy skrypt to seria `az` komend sprawdzających stan zasobów:
- `az network vnet subnet show` → delegacja `Microsoft.App/environments` ✅/❌
- `az network private-endpoint show` → status `Approved` ✅/❌
- `az network private-dns zone show` → linked do VNet ✅/❌
- `az role assignment list` → wymagane RBAC roles ✅/❌
- `az rest` → inference call do agenta ✅/❌

### 3️⃣ Raport końcowy
```
═══════════════════════════════════════
  FOUNDRY BYO VNet TEST REPORT
═══════════════════════════════════════
✅ PASS  VNet CIDR: 192.168.0.0/16
✅ PASS  Agent subnet delegacja: Microsoft.App/environments
✅ PASS  PE Foundry: Approved, IP 192.168.1.4
❌ FAIL  APIM → Foundry connectivity: timeout
⚠️ WARN  Storage: SharedKey not disabled
═══════════════════════════════════════
  VERDICT: NO-GO (1 FAIL, 1 WARN)
═══════════════════════════════════════
```

### 4️⃣ Dla klienta
Klient dostaje folder → wypełnia `test-config.json` swoimi wartościami → uruchamia skrypty lub skill. Nie potrzebuje Copilot CLI — może uruchomić `.ps1` bezpośrednio.

---

**Potwierdzam**: BYO VNet jest w pełni wspierany przez Bicep — Microsoft ma oficjalny template z 15 modułami. Mogę zaczynać implementację?

---

{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"As of 2024-2026, the Azure AI Foundry portal (ai.azure.com), now more often called Microsoft Foundry, supports robust network isolation and private endpoint architectures but there are some key nuances regarding Bring Your Own Virtual Network (BYO VNet) and browser-based portal access looking into 2025:\n\n### Current Private Networking Patterns\n- **Classic Foundry** (older experience): Allows you to secure Foundry resources via private endpoints in your own Azure VNet. You create private endpoints and control network access using your VNet, firewall, and NSGs. All traffic, both inbound (from users, including browser-based portal access) and outbound (to storage, databases, etc.), can be kept inside your VNet boundary, supporting strict enterprise compliance. However, end-to-end network isolation (including browser/portal access via private endpoint) is currently only supported in the classic portal or SDK/CLI—not in the new Foundry portal UI【3:4†source】.\n\n- **Foundry Agent Service**: Supports deployment directly into a customer-managed VNET via subnet delegation, enabling all compute and agent communications to remain within your private networking boundaries (with correct DNS, NSG, firewall controls, and private DNS zones)【3:6†source】【3:1†source】【3:9†source】.\n\n- **Platform-Managed (\"New\") Foundry Portal (ai.azure.com)**: As of early 2026, direct browser access to the Foundry portal via private endpoints (i.e., accessing the web UI without traversing the public internet) is **not fully supported** in the new experience. Currently:\n  - You can secure agent services, training, and most API/data access using private endpoints within your own VNet.\n  - Access to the web portal (ai.azure.com) itself still generally goes over the public internet.\n  - For full privacy, organizations are advised to use the older classic Foundry interface via SDK/CLI for end-to-end isolation. Portal/browser-based access through a private endpoint is a heavily requested feature but is not confirmed on the public roadmap for 2025 release as of now【3:4†source】【3:5†source】.\n\n### Bring Your Own VNet (BYO VNet)\n- BYO VNet is **supported** for core Foundry services, agent deployments, and private endpoint control.\n- For portal/browser access, BYO VNet alone does not currently guarantee private endpoint-only access for the UI but does for most back-end services (APIs, storage, compute)【3:6†source】【3:2†source】.\n\n### Roadmap & 2025 Outlook\n- Microsoft documentation and experts state that **portal/browser access via private endpoint in your own VNet is not expected to be available in the first half of 2025** in the new (ai.azure.com) experience. Full isolation for browser portal might follow later, but current guidance is to use classic Foundry for strict enterprise compliance or direct SDK/CLI for 100% private access【3:4†source】【3:5†source】.\n\n- **Hybrid Patterns**: Most enterprises use a combination—browser access to the portal is over public internet but strictly limited by IP allowlists/VPN, while agent services, API calls, and all data/storage remain 100% in-private via VNet and private endpoints【3:2†source】.\n\n### Deployment Considerations\n- You can still have: \n  - Private endpoint access to all your data and managed services in Foundry.\n  - All agent compute in your own VNet, with strict NSG/firewall rules.\n  - Centralized outbound control using Azure Firewall.\n  - But, some admin/operator tasks through the portal will cross the public internet unless Microsoft releases portal private endpoint support.\n\n### References for Deep Dive\n- [How to configure network isolation for Microsoft Foundry (Microsoft Learn)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks)【3:0†source】\n- [Securing Microsoft Foundry with BYO VNET (2025)](https://cloudplatforms.mstn.com/posts/securing-foundry-byovnet/)【3:1†source】\n- [How to Secure Azure AI Foundry Hub Project Connectivity (2025)](https://cloudplatforms.mstn.com/posts/secure-ai-foundry-hub/)【3:2†source】\n- [Official limitations for private network access in new Foundry portal](https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/azure-ai-docs/blob/main/articles/foundry-classic/how-to/configure-private-link.md)【3:4†source】\n\n**Summary:**  \nFull BYO VNet and private endpoint support for all services except browser/portal at ai.azure.com is available in 2025; browser access via private endpoint is not yet natively supported in the new portal but is possible in classic Foundry using SDK/CLI. Most sensitive and regulated workloads achieve compliance using a hybrid pattern (portal via public + all data/agent access via private endpoints). Always refer to up-to-date Microsoft Docs as features evolve <sub>⏱️ 34m 2s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry private network access portal jumpbox VM bastion VPN P2S configuration**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

  "query": "Azure AI Foundry private network access portal jumpbox VM bastion VPN P2S configuration"
}
```

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Certainly! Here’s a structured guide for configuring a **private network access** architecture for **Azure AI Foundry** that typically incorporates **Portal Jumpbox VM**, **Azure Bastion**, and **VPN P2S (Point-to-Site)**. This setup allows secure, private access to your resources in Azure, especially when dealing with sensitive workloads.\n\n---\n\n## 1. **High-Level Architecture**\n\n- **Azure Foundry Services**: Deployed in a private subnet (without public endpoints).\n- **Jumpbox VM (aka Bastion Host)**: Sits in the DMZ or management subnet for administrative access.\n- **Azure Bastion**: Provides secure RDP/SSH access to VMs via the Azure Portal without exposing VM public IPs.\n- **VPN Gateway (P2S)**: Allows developers/admins to connect from their local machines securely into the VNet.\n\n---\n\n## 2. **Step-by-Step Configuration**\n\n### **a) Configure Virtual Network/Subnets**\n\n1. **Create a VNet** (e.g., `FoundryVNet`).\n2. **Create subnets**:\n   - `PrivateSubnet` – Holds AI Foundry and other backend services.\n   - `MgmtSubnet` or `BastionSubnet` – For jumpbox and Bastion.\n   - `GatewaySubnet` – Required for VPN Gateway.\n\n---\n\n### **b) Deploy Azure AI Foundry (Private Only)**\n- During deployment, ensure the Foundry resource does **not** have a public endpoint.\n- Integrate with the `PrivateSubnet` using VNet Integration or Private Endpoint (if required by Foundry).\n\n---\n\n### **c) Deploy Jumpbox VM**\n- Deploy a small, hardened Windows or Linux VM in `MgmtSubnet`.\n- Assign a **private IP** only (no public IP, unless as a break-glass option).\n- Restrict NSG rules for management and monitoring access only.\n\n---\n\n### **d) Configure Azure Bastion**\n\n1. Ensure a `BastionSubnet` exists (named **exactly** as `AzureBastionSubnet`).\n2. Deploy **Azure Bastion** in this subnet.\n3. Use Bastion through the Azure Portal to RDP/SSH into the jumpbox or any VM with only a private IP.\n\n---\n\n### **e) Enable VPN Gateway (P2S) for Remote Access**\n\n1. **GatewaySubnet** is required. Deploy Azure VPN Gateway in this subnet.\n2. Configure for **Point-to-Site (P2S)** access:\n   - Choose authentication: Azure AD, certificates, or RADIUS.\n   - Generate and distribute client configuration (VPN client profile) to allowed users.\n3. Users can now connect to the VNet securely from anywhere.\n4. Once connected, users can RDP/SSH to the jumpbox, then hop to resources as needed.\n\n---\n\n### **f) NSG & RBAC Security**\n\n- Use NSGs to restrict traffic between subnets, allowing only necessary flows (e.g., allow Bastion to VMs on RDP/SSH ports).\n- Use RBAC to restrict Bastion and VPN usage to authorized users only.\n- Turn on logging (NSG flow logs, Diagnostics, Azure Monitor).\n\n---\n\n## 3. **Summary of Access Scenarios**\n\n- **Azure Portal via Bastion**: Secure, browser-based access for admins.\n- **Remote developers**: Connect VPN (P2S), then access resources as if on private network.\n- **Jumpbox**: Optional, provides another secure layer for administration or legacy workflows.\n\n---\n\n## 4. **References/Official Docs**\n- [Azure Bastion Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bastion/)\n- [VPN Gateway P2S—Configuration Guide](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway-howto-point-to-site-resource-manager-portal)\n- [Secure Azure services with Private Link](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview)\n\n---\n\nIf you specify your **particular Azure AI Foundry deployment type or have unique security/compliance controls**, I can tailor the instructions further or provide scripts/ARM/Bicep templates for automation. Let me know!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```


---

<sub>⏱️ 34m 29s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Dobre pytanie — to jest **kluczowy niuans** BYO VNet. Oto sytuacja:

## 🔒 Dostęp do ai.azure.com z zasobami za Private Endpoints

Portal `ai.azure.com` sam działa w publicznym internecie, ale musi **rozwiązać DNS** do prywatnych IP zasobów i **połączyć się** z nimi. Kiedy public access jest wyłączony → portal z Twojego laptopa **nie zobaczy** zasobów.

### Opcje dostępu:

| Opcja | Jak działa | Wdrożenie |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Azure Bastion + VM** (rekomendowane do testów) | VM w VNet z przeglądarką → RDP przez Bastion → otwierasz ai.azure.com na VM | Bicep: Bastion + VM + subnet `AzureBastionSubnet` |
| **VPN P2S** (Point-to-Site) | VPN client na Twoim laptopie → łączysz się do VNet → Twoja przeglądarka resoluje prywatne DNS | Bicep: VPN Gateway + `GatewaySubnet` + certy |
| **ExpressRoute** | Istniejące połączenie enterprise → automatyczny dostęp | Tylko jeśli klient już ma |

**Dla testów** najlepsza jest opcja **Bastion + VM** — szybka do deploy'u, nie wymaga konfiguracji VPN na kliencie, testuje pełny flow prywatny.

### ✅ `view`

<details>
<summary>614 lines</summary>

```
1. // ============================================================================
2. // Foundry BYO VNet - Main Bicep Template
3. // Deploys: VNet, Subnets, Foundry Account, Project, PE, DNS, RBAC
4. // Based on: microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup
5. // ============================================================================
6. 
7. @description('Azure region for all resources')
8. param location string = 'swedencentral'
9. 
10. @description('Foundry account name prefix')
11. param aiServicesName string = 'ai-foundry-byovnet'
12. 
13. @description('Model deployment configuration')
14. param modelName string = 'gpt-4.1'
15. param modelFormat string = 'OpenAI'
16. param modelVersion string = '2025-04-14'
17. param modelSkuName string = 'GlobalStandard'
18. param modelCapacity int = 30
19. 
20. @description('Project configuration')
21. param projectName string = 'project-agent-test'
22. param projectDescription string = 'Foundry Agent with BYO VNet network isolation test'
23. 
24. @description('VNet configuration')
25. param vnetName string = 'vnet-foundry-test'
26. param vnetAddressPrefix string = '192.168.0.0/16'
27. param agentSubnetName string = 'snet-agent'
28. param agentSubnetPrefix string = '192.168.0.0/24'
29. param peSubnetName string = 'snet-pe'
30. param peSubnetPrefix string = '192.168.1.0/24'
31. 
32. @description('Bastion + Jumpbox VM for portal access testing')
33. param deployBastion bool = true
34. param vmAdminUsername string = 'azureadmin'
35. @secure()
36. param vmAdminPassword string
37. 
38. @description('VPN P2S Gateway for client remote access')
39. param deployVpnGateway bool = true
40. 
41. @description('Unique suffix for resource names')
42. param uniqueSuffix string = uniqueString(resourceGroup().id)
43. 
44. // ============================================================================
45. // Variables
46. // ============================================================================
47. var aiServicesUniqueName = '${aiServicesName}-${uniqueSuffix}'
48. var storageAccountName = 'stfoundry${uniqueSuffix}'
49. var searchServiceName = 'srch-foundry-${uniqueSuffix}'
50. var cosmosAccountName = 'cosmos-foundry-${uniqueSuffix}'
51. 
52. var privateDnsZones = [
53.   'privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com'
54.   'privatelink.openai.azure.com'
55.   'privatelink.services.ai.azure.com'
56.   'privatelink.search.windows.net'
57.   'privatelink.blob.core.windows.net'
58.   'privatelink.documents.azure.com'
59. ]
60. 
61. // ============================================================================
62. // VNet + Subnets
63. // ============================================================================
64. resource vnet 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks@2024-05-01' = {
65.   name: vnetName
66.   location: location
67.   properties: {
68.     addressSpace: {
69.       addressPrefixes: [vnetAddressPrefix]
70.     }
71.     subnets: [
72.       {
73.         name: agentSubnetName
74.         properties: {
75.           addressPrefix: agentSubnetPrefix
76.           delegations: [
77.             {
78.               name: 'Microsoft.App.environments'
79.               properties: {
80.                 serviceName: 'Microsoft.App/environments'
81.               }
82.             }
83.           ]
84.         }
85.       }
86.       {
87.         name: peSubnetName
88.         properties: {
89.           addressPrefix: peSubnetPrefix
90.         }
91.       }
92.       {
93.         name: 'AzureBastionSubnet'
94.         properties: {
95.           addressPrefix: '192.168.3.0/26'
96.         }
97.       }
98.       {
99.         name: 'snet-mgmt'
100.         properties: {
101.           addressPrefix: '192.168.4.0/27'
102.         }
103.       }
104.       {
105.         name: 'GatewaySubnet'
106.         properties: {
107.           addressPrefix: '192.168.5.0/27'
108.         }
109.       }
110.     ]
111.   }
112. }
113. 
114. // ============================================================================
115. // Private DNS Zones + VNet Links
116. // ============================================================================
117. resource dnsZones 'Microsoft.Network/privateDnsZones@2024-06-01' = [
118.   for zone in privateDnsZones: {
119.     name: zone
120.     location: 'global'
121.   }
122. ]
123. 
124. resource dnsZoneLinks 'Microsoft.Network/privateDnsZones/virtualNetworkLinks@2024-06-01' = [
125.   for (zone, i) in privateDnsZones: {
126.     name: '${zone}-link'
127.     parent: dnsZones[i]
128.     location: 'global'
129.     properties: {
130.       virtualNetwork: {
131.         id: vnet.id
132.       }
133.       registrationEnabled: false
134.     }
135.   }
136. ]
137. 
138. // ============================================================================
139. // Storage Account (BYO - Agent file storage)
140. // ============================================================================
141. resource storageAccount 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts@2023-05-01' = {
142.   name: storageAccountName
143.   location: location
144.   kind: 'StorageV2'
145.   sku: {
146.     name: 'Standard_GRS'
147.   }
148.   properties: {
149.     minimumTlsVersion: 'TLS1_2'
150.     allowBlobPublicAccess: false
151.     publicNetworkAccess: 'Disabled'
152.     allowSharedKeyAccess: false
153.     networkAcls: {
154.       defaultAction: 'Deny'
155.       bypass: 'AzureServices'
156.     }
157.   }
158. }
159. 
160. resource blobService 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices@2023-05-01' = {
161.   parent: storageAccount
162.   name: 'default'
163. }
164. 
165. // ============================================================================
166. // Azure AI Search (BYO - Agent vector store)
167. // ============================================================================
168. resource searchService 'Microsoft.Search/searchServices@2024-06-01-preview' = {
169.   name: searchServiceName
170.   location: location
171.   sku: {
172.     name: 'standard'
173.   }
174.   identity: {
175.     type: 'SystemAssigned'
176.   }
177.   properties: {
178.     hostingMode: 'default'
179.     partitionCount: 1
180.     replicaCount: 1
181.     publicNetworkAccess: 'disabled'
182.     authOptions: {
183.       aadOrApiKey: {
184.         aadAuthFailureMode: 'http401WithBearerChallenge'
185.       }
186.     }
187.   }
188. }
189. 
190. // ============================================================================
191. // Cosmos DB (BYO - Agent thread storage)
192. // ============================================================================
193. resource cosmosAccount 'Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts@2024-11-15' = {
194.   name: cosmosAccountName
195.   location: location
196.   kind: 'GlobalDocumentDB'
197.   properties: {
198.     databaseAccountOfferType: 'Standard'
199.     consistencyPolicy: {
200.       defaultConsistencyLevel: 'Session'
201.     }
202.     locations: [
203.       {
204.         locationName: location
205.         failoverPriority: 0
206.       }
207.     ]
208.     publicNetworkAccess: 'Disabled'
209.     disableLocalAuth: true
210.   }
211. }
212. 
213. // ============================================================================
214. // Azure AI Foundry Account (Cognitive Services)
215. // ============================================================================
216. resource aiServices 'Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts@2025-04-01-preview' = {
217.   name: aiServicesUniqueName
218.   location: location
219.   kind: 'AIServices'
220.   sku: {
221.     name: 'S0'
222.   }
223.   identity: {
224.     type: 'SystemAssigned'
225.   }
226.   properties: {
227.     customSubDomainName: aiServicesUniqueName
228.     publicNetworkAccess: 'Disabled'
229.     networkAcls: {
230.       defaultAction: 'Deny'
231.       bypass: 'AzureServices'
232.     }
233.   }
234. }
235. 
236. // ============================================================================
237. // Model Deployment
238. // ============================================================================
239. resource modelDeployment 'Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments@2025-04-01-preview' = {
240.   parent: aiServices
241.   name: modelName
242.   sku: {
243.     name: modelSkuName
244.     capacity: modelCapacity
245.   }
246.   properties: {
247.     model: {
248.       name: modelName
249.       format: modelFormat
250.       version: modelVersion
251.     }
252.   }
253. }
254. 
255. // ============================================================================
256. // Foundry Project
257. // ============================================================================
258. resource project 'Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/projects@2025-04-01-preview' = {
259.   parent: aiServices
260.   name: projectName
261.   location: location
262.   identity: {
263.     type: 'SystemAssigned'
264.   }
265.   properties: {
266.     description: projectDescription
267.   }
268. }
269. 
270. // ============================================================================
271. // Private Endpoints
272. // ============================================================================
273. resource peAiServices 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints@2024-05-01' = {
274.   name: 'pe-${aiServicesUniqueName}'
275.   location: location
276.   properties: {
277.     subnet: {
278.       id: vnet.properties.subnets[1].id
279.     }
280.     privateLinkServiceConnections: [
281.       {
282.         name: 'ai-services-connection'
283.         properties: {
284.           privateLinkServiceId: aiServices.id
285.           groupIds: ['account']
286.         }
287.       }
288.     ]
289.   }
290. }
291. 
292. resource peStorage 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints@2024-05-01' = {
293.   name: 'pe-${storageAccountName}'
294.   location: location
295.   properties: {
296.     subnet: {
297.       id: vnet.properties.subnets[1].id
298.     }
299.     privateLinkServiceConnections: [
300.       {
301.         name: 'storage-connection'
302.         properties: {
303.           privateLinkServiceId: storageAccount.id
304.           groupIds: ['blob']
305.         }
306.       }
307.     ]
308.   }
309. }
310. 
311. resource peSearch 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints@2024-05-01' = {
312.   name: 'pe-${searchServiceName}'
313.   location: location
314.   properties: {
315.     subnet: {
316.       id: vnet.properties.subnets[1].id
317.     }
318.     privateLinkServiceConnections: [
319.       {
320.         name: 'search-connection'
321.         properties: {
322.           privateLinkServiceId: searchService.id
323.           groupIds: ['searchService']
324.         }
325.       }
326.     ]
327.   }
328. }
329. 
330. resource peCosmos 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints@2024-05-01' = {
331.   name: 'pe-${cosmosAccountName}'
332.   location: location
333.   properties: {
334.     subnet: {
335.       id: vnet.properties.subnets[1].id
336.     }
337.     privateLinkServiceConnections: [
338.       {
339.         name: 'cosmos-connection'
340.         properties: {
341.           privateLinkServiceId: cosmosAccount.id
342.           groupIds: ['Sql']
343.         }
344.       }
345.     ]
346.   }
347. }
348. 
349. // ============================================================================
350. // Private DNS Zone Groups (auto-register A records)
351. // ============================================================================
352. resource dnsGroupAiServices 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints/privateDnsZoneGroups@2024-05-01' = {
353.   parent: peAiServices
354.   name: 'default'
355.   properties: {
356.     privateDnsZoneConfigs: [
357.       {
358.         name: 'cognitiveservices'
359.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[0].id }
360.       }
361.       {
362.         name: 'openai'
363.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[1].id }
364.       }
365.       {
366.         name: 'servicesai'
367.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[2].id }
368.       }
369.     ]
370.   }
371. }
372. 
373. resource dnsGroupStorage 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints/privateDnsZoneGroups@2024-05-01' = {
374.   parent: peStorage
375.   name: 'default'
376.   properties: {
377.     privateDnsZoneConfigs: [
378.       {
379.         name: 'blob'
380.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[4].id }
381.       }
382.     ]
383.   }
384. }
385. 
386. resource dnsGroupSearch 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints/privateDnsZoneGroups@2024-05-01' = {
387.   parent: peSearch
388.   name: 'default'
389.   properties: {
390.     privateDnsZoneConfigs: [
391.       {
392.         name: 'search'
393.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[3].id }
394.       }
395.     ]
396.   }
397. }
398. 
399. resource dnsGroupCosmos 'Microsoft.Network/privateEndpoints/privateDnsZoneGroups@2024-05-01' = {
400.   parent: peCosmos
401.   name: 'default'
402.   properties: {
403.     privateDnsZoneConfigs: [
404.       {
405.         name: 'cosmos'
406.         properties: { privateDnsZoneId: dnsZones[5].id }
407.       }
408.     ]
409.   }
410. }
411. 
412. // ============================================================================
413. // RBAC Role Assignments (Foundry MI → BYO resources)
414. // ============================================================================
415. var roleDefinitions = {
416.   searchIndexDataContributor: '8ebe5a00-799e-43f5-93ac-243d3dce84a7'
417.   searchServiceContributor: '7ca78c08-252a-4471-8644-bb5ff32d4ba0'
418.   storageBlobDataOwner: 'b7e6dc6d-f1e8-4753-8033-0f276bb0955b'
419.   storageQueueDataContributor: '974c5e8b-45b9-4653-ba55-5f855dd0fb88'
420.   cosmosDbOperator: '230815da-be43-4aae-9cb4-875f7bd000aa'
421. }
422. 
423. // AI Search RBAC
424. resource searchRbac1 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
425.   name: guid(searchService.id, aiServices.id, roleDefinitions.searchIndexDataContributor)
426.   scope: searchService
427.   properties: {
428.     principalId: aiServices.identity.principalId
429.     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', roleDefinitions.searchIndexDataContributor)
430.     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
431.   }
432. }
433. 
434. resource searchRbac2 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
435.   name: guid(searchService.id, aiServices.id, roleDefinitions.searchServiceContributor)
436.   scope: searchService
437.   properties: {
438.     principalId: aiServices.identity.principalId
439.     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', roleDefinitions.searchServiceContributor)
440.     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
441.   }
442. }
443. 
444. // Storage RBAC
445. resource storageRbac1 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
446.   name: guid(storageAccount.id, aiServices.id, roleDefinitions.storageBlobDataOwner)
447.   scope: storageAccount
448.   properties: {
449.     principalId: aiServices.identity.principalId
450.     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', roleDefinitions.storageBlobDataOwner)
451.     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
452.   }
453. }
454. 
455. resource storageRbac2 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
456.   name: guid(storageAccount.id, aiServices.id, roleDefinitions.storageQueueDataContributor)
457.   scope: storageAccount
458.   properties: {
459.     principalId: aiServices.identity.principalId
460.     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', roleDefinitions.storageQueueDataContributor)
461.     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
462.   }
463. }
464. 
465. // CosmosDB RBAC
466. resource cosmosRbac1 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
467.   name: guid(cosmosAccount.id, aiServices.id, roleDefinitions.cosmosDbOperator)
468.   scope: cosmosAccount
469.   properties: {
470.     principalId: aiServices.identity.principalId
471.     roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', roleDefinitions.cosmosDbOperator)
472.     principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
473.   }
474. }
475. 
476. // ============================================================================
477. // Outputs
478. // ============================================================================
479. output vnetId string = vnet.id
480. output agentSubnetId string = vnet.properties.subnets[0].id
481. output peSubnetId string = vnet.properties.subnets[1].id
482. output aiServicesId string = aiServices.id
483. output aiServicesName string = aiServices.name
484. output aiServicesPrincipalId string = aiServices.identity.principalId
485. output aiServicesEndpoint string = 'https://${aiServicesUniqueName}.cognitiveservices.azure.com'
486. output projectId string = project.id
487. output projectName string = project.name
488. output storageAccountId string = storageAccount.id
489. output storageAccountName string = storageAccount.name
490. output searchServiceId string = searchService.id
491. output searchServiceName string = searchService.name
492. output cosmosAccountId string = cosmosAccount.id
493. output cosmosAccountName string = cosmosAccount.name
494. 
495. // ============================================================================
496. // Azure Bastion + Jumpbox VM (for portal access testing)
497. // ============================================================================
498. resource bastionPip 'Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses@2024-05-01' = if (deployBastion) {
499.   name: 'pip-bastion'
500.   location: location
501.   sku: { name: 'Standard' }
502.   properties: {
503.     publicIPAllocationMethod: 'Static'
504.   }
505. }
506. 
507. resource bastion 'Microsoft.Network/bastionHosts@2024-05-01' = if (deployBastion) {
508.   name: 'bastion-foundry-test'
509.   location: location
510.   sku: { name: 'Standard' }
511.   properties: {
512.     ipConfigurations: [
513.       {
514.         name: 'bastionIpConfig'
515.         properties: {
516.           publicIPAddress: { id: bastionPip.id }
517.           subnet: { id: vnet.properties.subnets[2].id } // AzureBastionSubnet
518.         }
519.       }
520.     ]
521.   }
522. }
523. 
524. resource jumpboxNic 'Microsoft.Network/networkInterfaces@2024-05-01' = if (deployBastion) {
525.   name: 'nic-jumpbox'
526.   location: location
527.   properties: {
528.     ipConfigurations: [
529.       {
530.         name: 'ipconfig1'
531.         properties: {
532.           privateIPAllocationMethod: 'Dynamic'
533.           subnet: { id: vnet.properties.subnets[3].id } // snet-mgmt
534.         }
535.       }
536.     ]
537.   }
538. }
539. 
540. resource jumpboxVm 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines@2024-07-01' = if (deployBastion) {
541.   name: 'vm-jumpbox'
542.   location: location
543.   properties: {
544.     hardwareProfile: { vmSize: 'Standard_B2s' }
545.     osProfile: {
546.       computerName: 'jumpbox'
547.       adminUsername: vmAdminUsername
548.       adminPassword: vmAdminPassword
549.     }
550.     storageProfile: {
551.       imageReference: {
552.         publisher: 'MicrosoftWindowsDesktop'
553.         offer: 'windows-11'
554.         sku: 'win11-24h2-pro'
555.         version: 'latest'
556.       }
557.       osDisk: {
558.         createOption: 'FromImage'
559.         managedDisk: { storageAccountType: 'Standard_LRS' }
560.       }
561.     }
562.     networkProfile: {
563.       networkInterfaces: [{ id: jumpboxNic.id }]
564.     }
565.   }
566. }
567. 
568. // ============================================================================
569. // VPN Gateway P2S (for client remote access)
570. // ============================================================================
571. resource vpnGatewayPip 'Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses@2024-05-01' = if (deployVpnGateway) {
572.   name: 'pip-vpn-gateway'
573.   location: location
574.   sku: { name: 'Standard' }
575.   properties: {
576.     publicIPAllocationMethod: 'Static'
577.   }
578. }
579. 
580. resource vpnGateway 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworkGateways@2024-05-01' = if (deployVpnGateway) {
581.   name: 'vpngw-foundry-test'
582.   location: location
583.   properties: {
584.     gatewayType: 'Vpn'
585.     vpnType: 'RouteBased'
586.     sku: { name: 'VpnGw1' tier: 'VpnGw1' }
587.     ipConfigurations: [
588.       {
589.         name: 'default'
590.         properties: {
591.           publicIPAddress: { id: vpnGatewayPip.id }
592.           subnet: { id: vnet.properties.subnets[4].id } // GatewaySubnet
593.         }
594.       }
595.     ]
596.     vpnClientConfiguration: {
597.       vpnClientAddressPool: {
598.         addressPrefixes: ['172.16.0.0/24']
599.       }
600.       vpnClientProtocols: ['OpenVPN']
601.       vpnAuthenticationTypes: ['AAD']
602.       aadTenant: 'https://login.microsoftonline.com/${subscription().tenantId}'
603.       aadAudience: 'c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8'
604.       aadIssuer: 'https://sts.windows.net/${subscription().tenantId}/'
605.     }
606.   }
607. }
608. 
609. // Additional outputs for portal access
610. output bastionId string = deployBastion ? bastion.id : ''
611. output jumpboxPrivateIp string = deployBastion ? jumpboxNic.properties.ipConfigurations[0].properties.privateIPAddress : ''
612. output vpnGatewayId string = deployVpnGateway ? vpnGateway.id : ''
613. output vpnGatewayPip string = deployVpnGateway ? vpnGatewayPip.properties.ipAddress : ''
614.
```

</details>

1. using './main.bicep'
2. 
3. param location = 'swedencentral'
4. param aiServicesName = 'ai-foundry-byovnet'
5. param modelName = 'gpt-4.1'
6. param modelFormat = 'OpenAI'
7. param modelVersion = '2025-04-14'
8. param modelSkuName = 'GlobalStandard'
9. param modelCapacity = 30
10. param projectName = 'project-agent-test'
11. param projectDescription = 'Foundry Agent with BYO VNet network isolation test'
12. param vnetName = 'vnet-foundry-test'
13. param vnetAddressPrefix = '192.168.0.0/16'
14. param agentSubnetName = 'snet-agent'
15. param agentSubnetPrefix = '192.168.0.0/24'
16. param peSubnetName = 'snet-pe'
17. param peSubnetPrefix = '192.168.1.0/24'

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\scripts\00-preflight.ps1.**

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\scripts\00-preflight.ps1

<details>
<summary>145 lines</summary>

```
1. <#
2. .SYNOPSIS
3.     Pre-flight checks for Foundry BYO VNet deployment.
4. .DESCRIPTION
5.     Validates: az CLI login, subscription, provider registration, permissions, region.
6.     Run this BEFORE deploying any Bicep templates.
7. #>
8. param(
9.     [Parameter(Mandatory=$false)]
10.     [string]$ConfigPath = "$PSScriptRoot\..\config\test-config.json"
11. )
12. 
13. $ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"
14. 
15. # --- Load config ---
16. $config = Get-Content $ConfigPath -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json
17. $subscriptionId = $config.subscription_id
18. $location = $config.location
19. $providers = $config.required_providers
20. 
21. $results = @()
22. $pass = 0; $fail = 0; $warn = 0
23. 
24. function Add-Result($test, $status, $detail) {
25.     $icon = switch ($status) { "PASS" { [char]0x2705 } "FAIL" { [char]0x274C } "WARN" { [char]0x26A0  } }
26.     $script:results += [PSCustomObject]@{ Icon=$icon; Status=$status; Test=$test; Detail=$detail }
27.     switch ($status) { "PASS" { $script:pass++ } "FAIL" { $script:fail++ } "WARN" { $script:warn++ } }
28. }
29. 
30. Write-Host "`n========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
31. Write-Host "  FAZA 0: PRE-FLIGHT CHECKS" -ForegroundColor Cyan
32. Write-Host "========================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan
33. 
34. # --- 1. Az CLI login ---
35. Write-Host "Checking az CLI login..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
36. try {
37.     $account = az account show -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
38.     if ($account.id) {
39.         Add-Result "Az CLI login" "PASS" "Logged in as: $($account.user.name)"
40.     } else {
41.         Add-Result "Az CLI login" "FAIL" "Not logged in. Run: az login"
42.     }
43. } catch {
44.     Add-Result "Az CLI login" "FAIL" "az CLI not installed or not logged in"
45. }
46. 
47. # --- 2. Subscription ---
48. Write-Host "Setting subscription $subscriptionId..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
49. try {
50.     az account set --subscription $subscriptionId 2>&1 | Out-Null
51.     $current = az account show --query id -o tsv 2>&1
52.     if ($current.Trim() -eq $subscriptionId) {
53.         Add-Result "Subscription" "PASS" "Active: $subscriptionId"
54.     } else {
55.         Add-Result "Subscription" "FAIL" "Could not set subscription $subscriptionId"
56.     }
57. } catch {
58.     Add-Result "Subscription" "FAIL" "Subscription $subscriptionId not accessible"
59. }
60. 
61. # --- 3. Provider registration ---
62. Write-Host "Checking provider registration..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
63. foreach ($provider in $providers) {
64.     $state = az provider show --namespace $provider --query "registrationState" -o tsv 2>&1
65.     if ($state.Trim() -eq "Registered") {
66.         Add-Result "Provider: $provider" "PASS" "Registered"
67.     } else {
68.         Write-Host "  Registering $provider..." -ForegroundColor Gray
69.         az provider register --namespace $provider --wait 2>&1 | Out-Null
70.         $stateAfter = az provider show --namespace $provider --query "registrationState" -o tsv 2>&1
71.         if ($stateAfter.Trim() -eq "Registered") {
72.             Add-Result "Provider: $provider" "PASS" "Registered (just now)"
73.         } else {
74.             Add-Result "Provider: $provider" "WARN" "Registration pending: $stateAfter"
75.         }
76.     }
77. }
78. 
79. # --- 4. Permissions check ---
80. Write-Host "Checking permissions..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
81. try {
82.     $roleAssignments = az role assignment list --assignee (az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv 2>&1).Trim() `
83.         --scope "/subscriptions/$subscriptionId" -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
84.     $roles = $roleAssignments | ForEach-Object { $_.roleDefinitionName }
85.     
86.     if ($roles -contains "Owner") {
87.         Add-Result "Permissions" "PASS" "Owner role on subscription"
88.     } elseif ($roles -contains "Contributor") {
89.         Add-Result "Permissions" "WARN" "Contributor role (may need Role Based Access Administrator for RBAC)"
90.     } else {
91.         Add-Result "Permissions" "WARN" "Roles found: $($roles -join ', '). May need Owner or Contributor."
92.     }
93. } catch {
94.     Add-Result "Permissions" "WARN" "Could not verify permissions: $($_.Exception.Message)"
95. }
96. 
97. # --- 5. Region availability ---
98. Write-Host "Checking region $location..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
99. try {
100.     $regions = az account list-locations --query "[].name" -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
101.     if ($regions -contains $location) {
102.         Add-Result "Region: $location" "PASS" "Available in subscription"
103.     } else {
104.         Add-Result "Region: $location" "FAIL" "Region not available"
105.     }
106. } catch {
107.     Add-Result "Region" "WARN" "Could not verify region availability"
108. }
109. 
110. # --- 6. Resource Group ---
111. Write-Host "Checking/creating resource group..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
112. $rg = $config.resource_group
113. try {
114.     $existing = az group show --name $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
115.     if ($existing.name -eq $rg) {
116.         if ($existing.location -eq $location) {
117.             Add-Result "Resource Group: $rg" "PASS" "Exists in $location"
118.         } else {
119.             Add-Result "Resource Group: $rg" "FAIL" "Exists but in $($existing.location), expected $location"
120.         }
121.     }
122. } catch {
123.     Write-Host "  Creating RG $rg in $location..." -ForegroundColor Gray
124.     az group create --name $rg --location $location -o none 2>&1
125.     Add-Result "Resource Group: $rg" "PASS" "Created in $location"
126. }
127. 
128. # --- Print results ---
129. Write-Host "`n========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
130. Write-Host "  PRE-FLIGHT RESULTS" -ForegroundColor Cyan
131. Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
132. foreach ($r in $results) {
133.     $color = switch ($r.Status) { "PASS" { "Green" } "FAIL" { "Red" } "WARN" { "Yellow" } }
134.     Write-Host "$($r.Icon) $($r.Status)  $($r.Test): $($r.Detail)" -ForegroundColor $color
135. }
136. Write-Host "`n--- Summary: $pass PASS, $fail FAIL, $warn WARN ---" -ForegroundColor Cyan
137. 
138. if ($fail -gt 0) {
139.     Write-Host "`nPre-flight FAILED. Fix issues above before deploying.`n" -ForegroundColor Red
140.     exit 1
141. } else {
142.     Write-Host "`nPre-flight PASSED. Ready to deploy.`n" -ForegroundColor Green
143.     exit 0
144. }
145.
```

</details>


### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\scripts\10-test-portal-access.ps1.**

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\scripts\10-test-portal-access.ps1

<details>
<summary>167 lines</summary>

```
1. <#
2. .SYNOPSIS
3.     Test portal access to ai.azure.com via Bastion + Jumpbox VM and VPN P2S Gateway.
4. .DESCRIPTION
5.     Validates: Bastion deployment, jumpbox VM status, VPN Gateway P2S config,
6.     DNS resolution from within VNet, and connectivity to Foundry portal.
7. #>
8. param(
9.     [Parameter(Mandatory=$false)]
10.     [string]$ConfigPath = "$PSScriptRoot\..\config\test-config.json"
11. )
12. 
13. $ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"
14. $config = Get-Content $ConfigPath -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json
15. $rg = $config.resource_group
16. 
17. $results = @()
18. $pass = 0; $fail = 0; $warn = 0
19. 
20. function Add-Result($test, $status, $detail) {
21.     $icon = switch ($status) { "PASS" { [char]0x2705 } "FAIL" { [char]0x274C } "WARN" { [char]0x26A0 } }
22.     $script:results += [PSCustomObject]@{ Icon=$icon; Status=$status; Test=$test; Detail=$detail }
23.     switch ($status) { "PASS" { $script:pass++ } "FAIL" { $script:fail++ } "WARN" { $script:warn++ } }
24. }
25. 
26. Write-Host "`n========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
27. Write-Host "  FAZA 7: PORTAL ACCESS TEST" -ForegroundColor Cyan
28. Write-Host "========================================`n" -ForegroundColor Cyan
29. 
30. # --- 1. Azure Bastion ---
31. Write-Host "Checking Azure Bastion..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
32. try {
33.     $bastion = az network bastion show --name "bastion-foundry-test" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
34.     if ($bastion.provisioningState -eq "Succeeded") {
35.         Add-Result "Bastion: bastion-foundry-test" "PASS" "Provisioned, SKU: $($bastion.sku.name)"
36.     } else {
37.         Add-Result "Bastion: bastion-foundry-test" "FAIL" "State: $($bastion.provisioningState)"
38.     }
39. } catch {
40.     Add-Result "Bastion" "FAIL" "Not found in $rg"
41. }
42. 
43. # --- 2. Bastion Subnet ---
44. Write-Host "Checking AzureBastionSubnet..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
45. try {
46.     $bastionSubnet = az network vnet subnet show --vnet-name $config.vnet.name --name "AzureBastionSubnet" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
47.     $prefix = $bastionSubnet.addressPrefix
48.     $cidr = [int]($prefix -split '/')[1]
49.     if ($cidr -le 26) {
50.         Add-Result "AzureBastionSubnet" "PASS" "CIDR: $prefix (>= /26)"
51.     } else {
52.         Add-Result "AzureBastionSubnet" "FAIL" "CIDR: $prefix (needs /26 or larger)"
53.     }
54. } catch {
55.     Add-Result "AzureBastionSubnet" "FAIL" "Subnet not found"
56. }
57. 
58. # --- 3. Jumpbox VM ---
59. Write-Host "Checking Jumpbox VM..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
60. try {
61.     $vm = az vm show --name "vm-jumpbox" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
62.     $vmStatus = az vm get-instance-view --name "vm-jumpbox" --resource-group $rg --query "instanceView.statuses[1].displayStatus" -o tsv 2>&1
63.     if ($vmStatus.Trim() -eq "VM running") {
64.         Add-Result "Jumpbox VM" "PASS" "Running, Size: $($vm.hardwareProfile.vmSize)"
65.     } elseif ($vm.name) {
66.         Add-Result "Jumpbox VM" "WARN" "Exists but status: $($vmStatus.Trim()). Start with: az vm start --name vm-jumpbox --resource-group $rg"
67.     }
68. } catch {
69.     Add-Result "Jumpbox VM" "FAIL" "Not found in $rg"
70. }
71. 
72. # --- 4. Jumpbox Private IP (no public) ---
73. Write-Host "Checking Jumpbox network..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
74. try {
75.     $nic = az network nic show --name "nic-jumpbox" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
76.     $privateIp = $nic.ipConfigurations[0].privateIPAddress
77.     $hasPublicIp = $null -ne $nic.ipConfigurations[0].publicIPAddress
78.     if (-not $hasPublicIp -and $privateIp) {
79.         Add-Result "Jumpbox Network" "PASS" "Private IP: $privateIp, No public IP"
80.     } elseif ($hasPublicIp) {
81.         Add-Result "Jumpbox Network" "WARN" "Has public IP - should be private only for security"
82.     }
83. } catch {
84.     Add-Result "Jumpbox Network" "WARN" "Could not verify NIC"
85. }
86. 
87. # --- 5. VPN Gateway ---
88. Write-Host "Checking VPN Gateway..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
89. try {
90.     $vpnGw = az network vnet-gateway show --name "vpngw-foundry-test" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
91.     if ($vpnGw.provisioningState -eq "Succeeded") {
92.         Add-Result "VPN Gateway" "PASS" "Provisioned, SKU: $($vpnGw.sku.name)"
93.     } else {
94.         Add-Result "VPN Gateway" "FAIL" "State: $($vpnGw.provisioningState)"
95.     }
96. } catch {
97.     Add-Result "VPN Gateway" "FAIL" "Not found in $rg"
98. }
99. 
100. # --- 6. VPN P2S Configuration ---
101. Write-Host "Checking VPN P2S config..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
102. try {
103.     $vpnGw = az network vnet-gateway show --name "vpngw-foundry-test" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
104.     $p2s = $vpnGw.vpnClientConfiguration
105.     if ($p2s.vpnClientAddressPool.addressPrefixes.Count -gt 0) {
106.         $authType = if ($p2s.vpnAuthenticationTypes) { $p2s.vpnAuthenticationTypes -join ', ' } else { 'Certificate' }
107.         Add-Result "VPN P2S Config" "PASS" "Pool: $($p2s.vpnClientAddressPool.addressPrefixes[0]), Auth: $authType"
108.     } else {
109.         Add-Result "VPN P2S Config" "FAIL" "No client address pool configured"
110.     }
111. } catch {
112.     Add-Result "VPN P2S Config" "WARN" "Could not verify P2S config"
113. }
114. 
115. # --- 7. GatewaySubnet ---
116. Write-Host "Checking GatewaySubnet..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
117. try {
118.     $gwSubnet = az network vnet subnet show --vnet-name $config.vnet.name --name "GatewaySubnet" --resource-group $rg -o json 2>&1 | ConvertFrom-Json
119.     Add-Result "GatewaySubnet" "PASS" "CIDR: $($gwSubnet.addressPrefix)"
120. } catch {
121.     Add-Result "GatewaySubnet" "FAIL" "Subnet not found"
122. }
123. 
124. # --- 8. DNS resolution test (run on jumpbox via Bastion if possible) ---
125. Write-Host "Checking DNS zones linked to VNet..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
126. $dnsZones = $config.dns_zones
127. foreach ($zone in $dnsZones) {
128.     try {
129.         $link = az network private-dns link vnet list --zone-name $zone --resource-group $rg --query "[?virtualNetwork.id!=null].name" -o tsv 2>&1
130.         if ($link.Trim()) {
131.             Add-Result "DNS Link: $zone" "PASS" "Linked to VNet"
132.         } else {
133.             Add-Result "DNS Link: $zone" "FAIL" "Not linked to VNet"
134.         }
135.     } catch {
136.         Add-Result "DNS Link: $zone" "WARN" "Zone not found or no access"
137.     }
138. }
139. 
140. # --- Print results ---
141. Write-Host "`n========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
142. Write-Host "  PORTAL ACCESS TEST RESULTS" -ForegroundColor Cyan
143. Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
144. foreach ($r in $results) {
145.     $color = switch ($r.Status) { "PASS" { "Green" } "FAIL" { "Red" } "WARN" { "Yellow" } }
146.     Write-Host "$($r.Icon) $($r.Status)  $($r.Test): $($r.Detail)" -ForegroundColor $color
147. }
148. Write-Host "`n--- Summary: $pass PASS, $fail FAIL, $warn WARN ---" -ForegroundColor Cyan
149. 
150. Write-Host "`n--- Portal Access Instructions ---" -ForegroundColor Magenta
151. Write-Host @"
152. 
153.   BASTION (do testow):
154.     1. Azure Portal > vm-jumpbox > Connect > Bastion
155.     2. Login: azureadmin / <password>
156.     3. Open Edge > https://ai.azure.com
157.     4. Verify you can see Foundry project and resources
158. 
159.   VPN P2S (dla klienta):
160.     1. Download VPN client: az network vnet-gateway vpn-client generate --name vpngw-foundry-test --resource-group $rg
161.     2. Install VPN profile on client machine
162.     3. Connect VPN > open browser > https://ai.azure.com
163.     4. Configure DNS: forward Private DNS Zones to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16)
164. "@
165. 
166. if ($fail -gt 0) { exit 1 } else { exit 0 }
167.
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>71 lines</summary>

```
1. {
2.   "subscription_id": "38b8b5d4-b3a1-4892-b2a7-3f5bcfdad59f",
3.   "resource_group": "rg-foundry-byovnet",
4.   "location": "swedencentral",
5. 
6.   "vnet": {
7.     "name": "vnet-foundry-test",
8.     "address_prefix": "192.168.0.0/16",
9.     "agent_subnet": {
10.       "name": "snet-agent",
11.       "prefix": "192.168.0.0/24"
12.     },
13.     "pe_subnet": {
14.       "name": "snet-pe",
15.       "prefix": "192.168.1.0/24"
16.     },
17.     "apim_subnet": {
18.       "name": "snet-apim",
19.       "prefix": "192.168.2.0/27"
20.     }
21.   },
22. 
23.   "foundry": {
24.     "account_name": "ai-foundry-byovnet",
25.     "project_name": "project-agent-test",
26.     "model_name": "gpt-4.1",
27.     "model_format": "OpenAI",
28.     "model_version": "2025-04-14",
29.     "model_sku": "GlobalStandard",
30.     "model_capacity": 30
31.   },
32. 
33.   "apim": {
34.     "name": "apim-foundry-test",
35.     "sku": "Developer",
36.     "publisher_email": "admin@contoso.com",
37.     "publisher_name": "Foundry VNet Test",
38.     "vnet_mode": "Internal"
39.   },
40. 
41.   "dns_zones": [
42.     "privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com",
43.     "privatelink.openai.azure.com",

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry standard agent setup bring your own virtual network required private endpoints capability host project connections RBAC**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry standard agent setup bring your own virtual network required private endpoints capability host project connections RBAC"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"You’re asking about setting up **Azure AI Foundry Standard Agent** with the following requirements:  \n- Bring Your Own Virtual Network (**BYO VNet**)\n- Use of **Private Endpoints**\n- Ability to **host project connections**\n- Integration with **RBAC** (Role-Based Access Control)\n\nHere's an overview of key steps and what each of these requirements entails:\n\n---\n\n### 1. **Bring Your Own Virtual Network (BYO VNet)**\n- **BYO VNet** allows you to deploy Azure resources (such as the Foundry agent) within your organization's own virtual network, rather than a Microsoft-managed one.\n- This is essential for network isolation, custom routing, and enhanced security.\n\n**How:**  \n- When deploying a Foundry standard agent, you typically specify your VNet and subnet in the deployment template or via Azure Portal.\n- Ensure that your network has necessary subnet capacity and no address conflicts with Microsoft or on-premises resources.\n\n---\n\n### 2. **Private Endpoints**\n- **Private Endpoints** are used to enable private connectivity to Azure resources over your VNet, eliminating exposure to the public internet.\n- Required for services like Azure Storage, Azure SQL, Azure Key Vault, and the Foundry endpoints themselves.\n\n**How:**  \n- For each Azure service used by Foundry (e.g., storage, databases), create a Private Endpoint.\n- Update DNS (either Azure DNS or on-prem DNS) to resolve service names to private IPs.\n- When configuring the Foundry agent, specify or ensure it uses these private endpoints for all traffic.\n\n---\n\n### 3. **Host Project Connections**\n- The agent should be able to create and manage network connections necessary for Foundry projects (to data sources, APIs, etc.).\n- These connections must route through private networks whenever possible in a secure, auditable way.\n\n**How:**  \n- Configure outbound NSG rules and/or user-defined routes in your VNet to allow the agent to reach required data sources.\n- If connecting to on-premises systems, set up VNet peering or VPN/ExpressRoute.\n- Register necessary project connections within the Foundry platform, ensuring all connections leverage the private network path.\n\n---\n\n### 4. **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)**\n- **RBAC** controls who can deploy, administer, and use the Foundry agents and their project connections.\n- Integration with Azure AD for resource-level permissions is standard.\n\n**How:**  \n- Assign users/groups to appropriate Azure roles (e.g., \"Owner\", \"Contributor\", custom roles) at the resource group, VNet, or Foundry Agent resource level.\n- Ensure the managed identity (or service principal) used by the agent has “Reader” or more on required VNets/subnets and “Contributor” or more on networking resources it needs to manipulate.\n\n---\n\n### **Quick Checklist for Azure AI Foundry Standard Agent Setup**\n\n1. **Create/Select your VNet & subnet.**\n2. **Deploy Agent**—ensure it’s placed in your own VNet, not ‘None’ or Microsoft-managed VNet.\n3. **Configure Private Endpoints** for all Azure resources Foundry will use.\n4. **Update DNS** to resolve private endpoints correctly.\n5. **Set up NSG and routing** for outbound access where needed.\n6. **Define RBAC** permissions for agent identity and users.\n7. **Register/host project connections** via the Foundry portal or CLI, ensuring connections go through the private network.\n\n---\n\n### **Documentation & References**\n- For step-by-step details, visit:\n  - [Azure AI Foundry Standard Agent—Networking Guidance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/networking-best-practices)\n  - [Azure Private Endpoint documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview)\n  - [Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/overview)\n\nLet me know if you need specific ARM templates, CLI commands, or JSON policy examples for any step!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Here’s a summary of the standard setup for Azure AI Foundry Agents with private networking, including requirements for storage, search, Cosmos DB, RBAC roles, and private DNS zones, according to current Microsoft documentation:\n\n---\n\n### **Standard (Private Network) Setup Overview**\n- **Private Networking**: Foundry Agent Service standard setup can leverage full network isolation, requiring all traffic to go through private endpoints—ensuring no public or internet-exposed resources are involved. This is supported by Virtual Networks, Private Endpoints, and injected network controls【3:1†source】【3:7†source】.\n- **Bring Your Own (BYO) Resources**: You must provision and manage your own Azure resources for:\n  - **Azure Storage** (for files and intermediate system data)\n  - **Azure AI Search** (for vector store/indexed data)\n  - **Azure Cosmos DB** (for thread storage, agent metadata, conversation history)\n\n---\n\n### **Required Azure Resources**\n1. **Azure Storage Account**\n   - Two blob containers: One for user files, one for intermediate data (such as chunks/embeddings)【3:3†source】【3:5†source】.\n2. **Azure AI Search**\n   - Used for all vector stores created by the agents.\n3. **Azure Cosmos DB (for NoSQL)**\n   - Your Cosmos DB instance will have three containers:\n     - `thread-message-store` (user messages)\n     - `system-thread-message-store` (system messages)\n     - `agent-entity-store` (agent/configuration data)\n   - Minimum 3000 RU/s required per project (each container = 1000 RU/s per project)【3:0†source】【3:9†source】.\n\n---\n\n### **Network Isolation & Private DNS**\n- **Virtual Network/Subnets**: All Foundry workspace resources should be in the same region and VNet. Subnet integration allows agent compute to connect locally with BYO resources via private IPs.\n- **Private Endpoints**: Required for Storage, Cosmos DB, and Azure AI Search; public network access should be disabled.\n- **Private DNS zones**: Must be set up to resolve the private endpoint names for Storage, Cosmos DB, and AI Search—ensuring traffic doesn’t leak to the public internet【3:1†source】.\n\n---\n\n### **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**\n- **Required Azure built-in roles**:\n  - **Subscription-level/Resource group-level Owner** or **User Access Administrator**\n  - **Role-Based Access Control Administrator** for assigning roles across Cosmos DB, Storage, and Search.\n  - **Azure AI Account Owner** (for Foundry setup and operations)【3:4†source】【3:6†source】.\n- **Agent-specific RBAC**:\n  - Team members using the Agent Playground or SDK should be assigned the built-in **Azure AI User** RBAC role for the project (minimum permissions: agents/\\*/read, agents/\\*/action, agents/\\*/delete).\n- **Permission Registration**: Make sure to register all relevant provider namespaces (Microsoft.KeyVault, Microsoft.Storage, Microsoft.Network, Microsoft.Search, Microsoft.CosmosDB, etc.) in the subscription.\n\n---\n\n### **Documentation and Setup Links**\n- [Set up standard agent resources for Foundry Agent Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/agent-service-standard-resources)【3:0†source】\n- [Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/agent-service-private-networking)【3:4†source】\n- [Agent Service RBAC roles](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/roles)【3:6†source】\n- [Foundry Private Network sample (Bicep/ARM)](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)【3:1†source】【3:2†source】\n\n---\n\n**Summary Table**\n\n| Resource        | What It Stores                    | Network Requirement           | RBAC Role Required            |\n|-----------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|\n| Azure Storage   | Uploaded files, embeddings, data | Private endpoint/VNet         | Storage Blob Data Contributor |\n| Cosmos DB       | Threads, messages, metadata      | Private endpoint/VNet         | Cosmos DB Account Contributor |\n| Azure AI Search | Indexed/vector data              | Private endpoint/VNet         | Search Service Contributor    |\n\n---\n\nFor the full official documentation, refer to [Set up standard agent resources for Foundry Agent Service - Microsoft Learn][1] and [Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service - Microsoft Learn][2].\n\n---\n\n**Citations:**  \n[1]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/agent-service-standard-resources  \n[2]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/agent-service-private-networking  \nAnd others as referenced above.","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":588,"end_index":600,"url_citation":{"title":"Private Network Setup | azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples/4.3-private-network-setup"}},{"text":"","start_index":600,"end_index":612,"url_citation":{"title":"Built-in Enterprise Readiness with Azure AI Agent Service","url":"https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/built-in-enterprise-readiness-with-azure-ai-agent-service/4386541"}},{"text":"","start_index":1081,"end_index":1093,"url_citation":{"title":"Securely Build and Manage Agents in Azure AI Foundry","url":"https://argonsys.com/microsoft-cloud/library/securely-build-and-manage-agents-in-azure-ai-foundry/"}},{"text":"","start_index":1093,"end_index":1105,"url_citation":{"title":"Securely Build and Manage Agents in Azure AI Foundry | Microsoft ...","url":"https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/securely-build-and-manage-agents-in-azure-ai-foundry/4415186"}},{"text":"","start_index":1517,"end_index":1529,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up standard agent resources for Foundry Agent Service - Microsoft ...","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/concepts/standard-agent-setup"}},{"text":"","start_index":1529,"end_index":1541,"url_citation":{"title":"Azure Cosmos DB integration with Foundry Agent Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/gen-ai/azure-agent-service"}},{"text":"","start_index":2075,"end_index":2087,"url_citation":{"title":"Private Network Setup | azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/azure-ai-foundry/foundry-samples/4.3-private-network-setup"}},{"text":"","start_index":2432,"end_index":2444,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service - ### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure VPN Gateway point-to-site Azure AD auth aadAudience c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8 current documentation**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure VPN Gateway point-to-site Azure AD auth aadAudience c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8 current documentation"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"The App ID `c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8` is the Microsoft-registered Azure VPN Client App ID (also called the \"audience\" or `aadAudience` value) used for Azure VPN Gateway Point-to-site (P2S) connections that leverage Azure AD (now Microsoft Entra ID) authentication.\n\n**Current Official Guidance as of 2026:**\n\n- **Audience Value:** When configuring your VPN gateway or client configuration for Microsoft Entra ID authentication, set the \"Audience\" (or `aadAudience` or \"Application ID\" in some interfaces) field to `c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8`.\n- **Benefits:** Using the Microsoft-registered App ID means you no longer need to manually register an application in your tenant for the VPN Client, making deployment simpler and more secure. Your tenant can use this App ID out-of-the-box with no extra registration.\n- **Compatibility:** You must use this Audience value for:\n  - Azure VPN Client for Windows (latest versions, backward compatible with old values)\n  - Azure VPN Client for macOS\n  - Azure VPN Client for Linux (not backward compatible with old manual values; must use this or a custom audience value)\n- **Location to Configure:** \n  - In the Azure Portal, on your Virtual Network Gateway, go to the \"Point-to-site configuration\" page.\n  - For \"Authentication type,\" select \"Azure Active Directory.\"\n  - For \"Audience,\" enter: `c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8`.\n\n**Key Documentation Resources:**\n- [Configure P2S VPN for Microsoft Entra ID authentication - Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-gateway)【3:5†source】\n- [Migrate from manually registered App to Microsoft-registered App for VPN](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-vpn-client-microsoft-registered-app)【3:2†source】\n- [About supported audience values for Azure VPN Client](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-gateway#azure-vpn-client-audience-values)【3:0†source】\n\n**Migration Notes:**\n- If you previously used a manually-registered app with another GUID, update both your gateway and all client profiles to the new audience value.\n- Update the gateway first, then regenerate and distribute new client configuration packages.\n\nThis app ID is now the default and recommended approach for all new P2S VPN setups that use Entra ID authentication. For custom scenarios or to restrict access by user/group, you may still use a custom audience ID, but Microsoft recommends using the public app ID when possible due to its improved security and simplified implementation【3:7†source】.\n\nIf you need step-by-step or have other specific Azure P2S, VPN, or Entra ID integration questions, let me know!","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":1591,"end_index":1603,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure P2S VPN Gateway for Microsoft Entra ID authentication","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-gateway"}},{"text":"","start_index":1783,"end_index":1795,"url_citation":{"title":"Migrate manually registered Azure VPN client to Microsoft-registered ...","url":"https://docs.azure.cn/en-us/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-gateway-update"}},{"text":"","start_index":1966,"end_index":1978,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure P2S User VPN for Microsoft Entra ID authentication ...","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-wan/point-to-site-entra-gateway"}},{"text":"","start_index":2578,"end_index":2590,"url_citation":{"title":"azure-docs/articles/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-register ... - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/azure-docs/blob/main/articles/vpn-gateway/point-to-site-entra-register-custom-app.md"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"Azure VPN Gateway point-to-site Azure AD authentication aadAudience c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8 documentation","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=Azure+VPN+Gateway+point-to-site+Azure+AD+authentication+aadAudience+c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8+documentation"}],"annotations":null}

### ✅ `web_fetch`

**https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/concepts/standard-agent-setup**

  "url": "https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/concepts/standard-agent-setup",
  "max_length": 12000
}
