<sub>⏱️ 287m 53s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

✅ **5/5 ról przypisanych!** 

Krótko:
- **Azure AI User** = tylko odczyt, nie pozwala budować agentów
- **Azure AI Developer** = pełny dostęp do tworzenia agentów, deploymentów, connections

Dodałem Ci:
1. **Azure AI Developer** — na konto AI (tworzenie agentów)
2. **Cognitive Services OpenAI User** — dostęp do modeli
3. **Storage Blob Data Contributor** — pliki agentów
4. **Search Index Data Contributor** — vector store
5. **Cosmos DB Operator** — thread storage

**Co zrobić:** Odczekaj ~2 min na propagację RBAC, wyloguj się z ai.azure.com i zaloguj ponownie. Powinno działać.

Dodam to też do Bicepa i test skryptów żeby przyszli klienci od razu mieli te role.

---

<sub>⏱️ 288m 57s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Dobre pytanie! Nadałem na **Account (konto AI Services)** — i to jest poprawne podejście, bo:

**Account-level** (tak jak zrobiłem):
- Role **dziedziczą się w dół** na wszystkie projekty
- Jeden assignment = dostęp do wszystkich projektów pod kontem
- Upraszcza zarządzanie

**Project-level** (też działa):
- Role tylko na konkretny projekt
- Lepsza izolacja gdy masz wiele projektów i różne zespoły
- Trzeba nadawać osobno na każdy projekt

**W naszym przypadku** mamy 1 konto + 1 projekt, więc efekt jest taki sam. Ale gdyby klient miał kilka projektów i chciał ograniczyć dostęp per-zespół, to lepiej nadawać na projekt.

Rola **Azure AI Developer** na konto + role na zasobach (Storage, Search, CosmosDB) — to minimum do budowania agentów w portalu. `Azure AI User` daje tylko read-only.

Dodam tę informację do README dla klientów. Działa już portal?

---

<sub>⏱️ 289m 57s</sub>

### 👤 User

ale że dostaje się z Interentu to powinno chyba nie działać mam wyłączony dostęp z Interenetu i chyba tak powinno być teraz będziemy próbować dostać się przez bastion tam powinno działąć

<sub>⏱️ 290m 10s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Dokładnie, masz rację! 👍

**Public access = Disabled** — więc portal ai.azure.com z Internetu **nie powinien** działać. To jest poprawne zachowanie i potwierdza, że BYO VNet izolacja działa.

Portal ai.azure.com działa tak:
- **UI** ładuje się z Internetu (to publiczna strona)
- Ale **API calls** (tworzenie agentów, odczyt zasobów) idą do endpointów Foundry → te są zablokowane przez Private Endpoints

**Żeby dostać się do portalu** musisz:
1. Wejść przez **Bastion** na Jumpbox VM
2. Na VM otworzyć przeglądarkę → ai.azure.com
3. Zalogować się jako admin@MngEnvMCAP833611.onmicrosoft.com
4. Stamtąd DNS rozwiąże się na private IP-ki → wszystko zadziała

**Jak się połączyć:**
- Azure Portal → `rg-foundry-byovnet` → `vm-jumpbox` → **Connect** → **Bastion**
- Login: `azureadmin` / hasło które ustawiłeś (`Pa$$w0rd2018!`)
- Na VM otwórz Edge → ai.azure.com

To jest właśnie test który chcieliśmy sprawdzić — dostęp do portalu **tylko z wnętrza VNet**. Daj znać jak zadziała przez Bastion!

<sub>⏱️ 292m 16s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

**Izolacja działa poprawnie!** Z Internetu:

| Test | Wynik | Opis |
|------|-------|------|
| Create Agent | ❌ **404** | Zablokowany |
| OpenAI Chat | ❌ **403** | `Public access is disabled. Please configure private endpoint.` |
| List Agents | ❌ **404** | Zablokowany |

Żaden z endpointów nie jest dostępny z zewnątrz. Agenta **nie da się stworzyć z Internetu** — dokładnie tak jak powinno być.

Teraz z **Bastion/Jumpbox** (wnętrze VNet) te same komendy powinny zwrócić **200**. Przetestuj z VM:
```
az login
az account get-access-token --resource "https://cognitiveservices.azure.com" --query accessToken -o tsv
```
I potem otwórz ai.azure.com w Edge na VM.

<sub>⏱️ 295m 6s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Problem jasny! **Cosmos DB Operator** to rola control plane. Z `disableLocalAuth: true` potrzeba **data plane RBAC** — Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor:

---

<sub>⏱️ 295m 37s</sub>

### ✅ `powershell`

**Assign CosmosDB data plane roles**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "command": "$rg = \"rg-foundry-byovnet\"\n$cosmosName = \"cosmos-foundry-unrkke4ibdxcm\"\n$projectMI = \"836b9920-537f-4fa3-a95e-2942e7eca04a\"\n$accountMI = \"f1c3c13f-8f90-4ee6-a2dc-95c736548e5e\"\n\n# Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor = 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002\n$roleId = \"00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002\"\n$cosmosId = az cosmosdb show --name $cosmosName --resource-group $rg --query \"id\" -o tsv 2>&1\n\n# Assign to Project MI\nWrite-Host \"Assigning Cosmos DB Data Contributor to Project MI...\" -ForegroundColor Yellow\naz cosmosdb sql role assignment create `\n  --account-name $cosmosName `\n  --resource-group $rg `\n  --role-definition-id $roleId `\n  --principal-id $projectMI `\n  --scope $cosmosId `\n  -o none 2>&1\nWrite-Host \"Project MI exit: $LASTEXITCODE\"\n\n# Assign to Account MI\nWrite-Host \"Assigning Cosmos DB Data Contributor to Account MI...\" -ForegroundColor Yellow\naz cosmosdb sql role assignment create `\n  --account-name $cosmosName `\n  --resource-group $rg `\n  --role-definition-id $roleId `\n  --principal-id $accountMI `\n  --scope $cosmosId `\n  -o none 2>&1\nWrite-Host \"Account MI exit: $LASTEXITCODE\"",
  "description": "Assign CosmosDB data plane roles",
  "initial_wait": 30
}
```

</details>

````
1. # Foundry BYO VNet Integration Test
2. 
3. Zestaw testów walidujących wdrożenie Microsoft Foundry z BYO VNet (Bring Your Own Virtual Network).
4. 
5. ## Co to robi?
6. 
7. 1. **Deployuje** całą infrastrukturę przez Bicep + Azure CLI
8. 2. **Testuje** każdy komponent (sieć, PE, DNS, RBAC, tożsamości, portal)
9. 3. **Generuje raport** GO/NO-GO z listą co działa a co nie
10. 
11. ## Architektura
12. 
13. ```
14. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
15. │  Customer BYO VNet (192.168.0.0/16)                          │
16. │                                                              │
17. │  snet-agent /24           snet-pe /24                        │
18. │  Delegacja:               PE: Foundry (CognitiveServices)    │
19. │  Microsoft.App/           PE: Storage (blob)                 │
20. │    environments           PE: CosmosDB (sql)                 │
21. │  Foundry Agent Service    PE: AI Search                      │
22. │                                                              │
23. │  AzureBastionSubnet /26   snet-mgmt /27                      │
24. │  Azure Bastion            Jumpbox VM (Win11, private IP)     │
25. │                                                              │
26. │  GatewaySubnet /27        Private DNS Zones (7 linked)       │
27. │  VPN P2S Gateway                                             │
28. └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
29. ```
30. 
31. ## Prerequisites
32. 
33. 1. **Azure CLI** zainstalowane i zalogowane (`az login`)
34. 2. **Uprawnienia**: Owner lub Role Based Access Administrator na subskrypcji
35. 3. **Region**: Foundry Agent Service musi być dostępny w wybranym regionie
36.    - Class A: Australia East, East US, East US 2, France Central, Sweden Central, West Europe, West US...
37.    - Class B/C: Wszystkie regiony wspierające Foundry Agent Service
38. 
39. ## Szybki start
40. 
41. ### 1. Konfiguracja
42. 
43. Edytuj `config/test-config.json`:
44. 
45. ```json
46. {
47.   "subscription_id": "YOUR-SUBSCRIPTION-ID",
48.   "resource_group": "rg-foundry-byovnet",
49.   "location": "swedencentral",
50.   "vnet": {
51.     "name": "vnet-foundry-test",
52.     "address_prefix": "192.168.0.0/16",
53.     "agent_subnet": { "name": "snet-agent", "prefix": "192.168.0.0/24" },
54.     "pe_subnet": { "name": "snet-pe", "prefix": "192.168.1.0/24" }
55.   },
56.   "foundry": {
57.     "account_name": "ai-foundry-byovnet",
58.     "project_name": "project-agent-test",
59.     "model_name": "gpt-4.1"
60.   }
61. }
62. ```
63. 
64. ### 2. Ustaw hasło VM
65. 
66. ```powershell
67. $env:VM_ADMIN_PASSWORD = "TwojeHaslo123!"
68. ```
69. 
70. ### 3. Uruchom
71. 
72. ```powershell
73. # Krok 1: Pre-flight (sprawdza az CLI, providery, uprawnienia)
74. .\scripts\00-preflight.ps1
75. 
76. # Krok 2: Deploy infrastruktury (15-30 min, VPN Gateway ~25 min)
77. .\scripts\01-deploy-foundry.ps1
78. 
79. # Krok 3: Testy walidacyjne
80. .\scripts\04-test-network.ps1         # VNet, subnety, delegacja, DNS
81. .\scripts\05-test-pe.ps1              # Private Endpoints, public access
82. .\scripts\08-test-identity.ps1        # Managed Identity, RBAC
83. .\scripts\10-test-portal-access.ps1   # Bastion, VM, VPN Gateway
84. 
85. # Krok 4: Raport końcowy
86. .\scripts\09-e2e-report.ps1
87. ```
88. 
89. ### 4. Dostęp do portalu ai.azure.com
90. 
91. **Przez Bastion (do testów):**
92. 1. Azure Portal → `vm-jumpbox` → Connect → Bastion
93. 2. Login: `azureadmin` / hasło z env
94. 3. Otwórz Edge → `https://ai.azure.com`
95. 4. Sprawdź czy widzisz projekt i zasoby
96. 
97. **Przez VPN P2S (dla klienta):**
98. 1. Pobierz profil VPN:
99.    ```powershell
100.    az network vnet-gateway vpn-client generate \
101.      --name vpngw-foundry-test \
102.      --resource-group rg-foundry-byovnet
103.    ```
104. 2. Zainstaluj profil VPN na laptopie
105. 3. Połącz się → otwórz `https://ai.azure.com`
106. 4. Skonfiguruj DNS forwarding dla Private DNS Zones → `168.63.129.16`
107. 
108. ## Raport
109. 
110. Po uruchomieniu `09-e2e-report.ps1` dostaniesz raport:
111. 
112. ```
113. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
114.   FOUNDRY BYO VNet - E2E TEST REPORT
115. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
116. ✅ PASS  [Network] VNet exists: vnet-foundry-test
117. ✅ PASS  [Network] Agent subnet delegation: Microsoft.App/environments
118. ✅ PASS  [PE] pe-ai-foundry (account): Status: Approved
119. ❌ FAIL  [RBAC] Project RBAC: Cosmos DB Operator: NOT assigned
120. ⚠️ WARN  [Portal] Jumpbox VM: VM deallocated
121. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
122.   VERDICT: NO-GO
123.   Total: 18 PASS | 1 FAIL | 1 WARN
124. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
125. ```
126. 
127. Raport zapisywany jest też do pliku `report.txt`.
128. 
129. ## Cleanup
130. 
131. ```powershell
132. az group delete --name rg-foundry-byovnet --yes --no-wait
133. ```
134. 
135. > **Uwaga:** Przed usunięciem RG, usuń Capability Host i purguj Foundry Account:
136. > ```powershell
137. > # Usunięcie wymaga purge, nie tylko delete!
138. > az cognitiveservices account purge --name <account-name> --resource-group <rg> --location <region>
139. > ```
140. 
141. ## Bazowy template
142. 
143. Inspirowane oficjalnym template Microsoft:
144. [foundry-samples/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)
145. 
146. ## Struktura plików
147. 
148. ```
149. foundry-vnet-test/
150. ├── skill.md                    # Definicja skilla Copilot CLI
151. ├── bicep/
152. │   ├── main.bicep              # Foundry + BYO VNet + PE + RBAC + Bastion + VPN
153. │   └── main.bicepparam         # Parametry
154. ├── scripts/
155. │   ├── 00-preflight.ps1        # Pre-flight checks
156. │   ├── 01-deploy-foundry.ps1   # Deploy Bicep
157. │   ├── 04-test-network.ps1     # Test VNet/subnety/DNS
158. │   ├── 05-test-pe.ps1          # Test Private Endpoints
159. │   ├── 08-test-identity.ps1    # Test MI/RBAC
160. │   ├── 09-e2e-report.ps1       # E2E raport GO/NO-GO
161. │   └── 10-test-portal-access.ps1  # Test Bastion/VPN/Portal
162. ├── config/
163. │   └── test-config.json        # Konfiguracja
164. └── README.md                   # Ten plik
165. ```
166.
````

</details>


---

<summary>Detail</summary>

Audit all files in C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\ to verify that all fixes from deployment issues have been applied correctly. Here's the list of known issues that MUST be fixed in the source files:

**main.bicep fixes:**
1. Storage connection category: MUST be `AzureStorageAccount` (NOT `AzureBlob`)
2. VPN PIP: MUST have `zones: ['1', '2', '3']` on the vpnGatewayPip resource
3. subnetArmId: MUST use `resourceId('Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets', ...)` NOT `vnet.properties.subnets[0].id`
4. Storage connection metadata: MUST include `AccountName` and `ContainerName`
5. CosmosDB connection: MUST include `target` property with documents.azure.com URL and metadata
6. VPN Gateway SKU: MUST be `VpnGw1AZ` (NOT `VpnGw1`)
7. Project must have `dependsOn` to serialize after account provisioning
8. CosmosDB data plane RBAC: MUST have `Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts/sqlRoleAssignments` resources for both Project MI and Account MI (role definition ID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002)
9. Account MI MUST have `storageQueueDataContributor` role assignment

**Deploy script (01-deploy-foundry.ps1) fixes:**
10. Must have retry logic for `AccountProvisioningStateInvalid` error — the account with networkInjections goes to "Accepted" state on each PUT, so the deploy needs to detect this, wait for "Succeeded", and retry

**Test script (08-test-identity.ps1) fixes:**
11. Role assignment list must use `--all` flag (NOT `--resource-group`) to find per-resource scoped roles

**Test scripts (09-e2e-report.ps1, 10-test-portal-access.ps1) fixes:**
12. Must use `az resource show` instead of `az network bastion show` and `az network vnet-gateway show` (those commands hang/are very slow)

**README.md:**
13. Must document lessons learned including: Azure AI Developer role (not User), CosmosDB data plane RBAC, AzureStorageAccount category, race condition, VPN zones, subnetArmId

For each fix, check if it's actually present in the file. Report: FIX #N: ✅ PRESENT or ❌ MISSING with the relevant line numbers.

Also check the retry logic in 01-deploy-foundry.ps1 — specifically verify the `$deployCmd[9]` index is correct for updating the deployment name (count the array elements to make sure index 9 corresponds to the --name value).

Agent completed. agent_id: audit-fixes, agent_type: explore, status: completed, description: Audit all deployment fixes, elapsed: 114s, total_turns: 0, duration: 112s

## AUDIT REPORT: FOUNDRY VNet TEST DEPLOYMENT FIXES

### **main.bicep FIXES**

**FIX #1: Storage connection category must be `AzureStorageAccount`**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 294
- `category: 'AzureStorageAccount'` (NOT AzureBlob)

**FIX #2: VPN PIP must have zones ['1', '2', '3']**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 736
- `zones: ['1', '2', '3']` on vpnGatewayPip resource

**FIX #3: subnetArmId must use resourceId(), NOT vnet.properties.subnets[0].id**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 245
- `subnetArmId: resourceId('Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets', vnetName, agentSubnetName)`

**FIX #4: Storage connection metadata must include AccountName and ContainerName**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Lines 299-300
- `AccountName: storageAccount.name`
- `ContainerName: 'default'`

**FIX #5: CosmosDB connection must include target property with documents.azure.com URL**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 324
- `target: 'https://${cosmosAccount.name}.documents.azure.com:443/'`
- Metadata also includes: ApiType, ResourceId, DatabaseName, CollectionName

**FIX #6: VPN Gateway SKU must be VpnGw1AZ (NOT VpnGw1)**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 748
- `sku: { name: 'VpnGw1AZ', tier: 'VpnGw1AZ' }`

**FIX #7: Project must have dependsOn to serialize after account provisioning**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Line 284
- `dependsOn: [modelDeployment, peAiServices]` with comment "serialize: wait for account + PE to be fully provisioned"

**FIX #8: CosmosDB data plane RBAC for both Project MI and Account MI (role: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002)**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Lines 411-429
- `cosmosDataRbacProject` (lines 411-419): Project MI with correct role ID
- `cosmosDataRbacAccount` (lines 421-429): Account MI with correct role ID
- Both use: `roleDefinitionId: '${cosmosAccount.id}/sqlRoleDefinitions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002'`

**FIX #9: Account MI must have storageQueueDataContributor role assignment**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Lines 452-460
- `storageQueueRbacAccount` resource assigns `storageQueueDataContributor` (974c5e8b-45b9-4653-ba55-5f855dd0fb88) to Account MI

---

### **Deploy Script (01-deploy-foundry.ps1) FIXES**

**FIX #10: Retry logic for AccountProvisioningStateInvalid error**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Lines 63-93
- Detects "AccountProvisioningStateInvalid|Account .+ in state Accepted" (line 68)
- Waits for Succeeded state (lines 74-79)
- Retries deployment (lines 82-85)

**⚠️ CRITICAL ISSUE: Array Index Error in Retry Logic**
- **Line 83**: `$deployCmd[9] = "foundry-byovnet-$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss')"`
- **PROBLEM**: Array index 9 is the `--parameters` filename (`$bicepDir\main.bicepparam`), NOT the deployment name
- **Correct index should be 11** (the deployment name value)
- **Current state**: ❌ **INCORRECT ARRAY INDEX** - This will corrupt the deployment command!
- **Array breakdown**:
  - [0]: "az"
  - [1]: "deployment"
  - [2]: "group"
  - [3]: "create"
  - [4]: "--resource-group"
  - [5]: $rg
  - [6]: "--template-file"
  - [7]: "$bicepDir\main.bicep"
  - [8]: "--parameters"
  - [9]: "$bicepDir\main.bicepparam" ← **BEING UPDATED (WRONG!)**
  - [10]: "--name"
  - [11]: "foundry-byovnet-20250101-120000" ← **SHOULD BE UPDATED HERE**
  - [12]: "--verbose"

---

### **Test Scripts FIXES**

**FIX #11: Role assignment list must use --all flag (NOT --resource-group)**
- ✅ **PRESENT** - Lines 52, 78 in 08-test-identity.ps1
- `az role assignment list --assignee $accountMi.principalId --all -o json`
- `az role assignment list --assignee $projectJson.identity.principalId --all -o json`

  - Line 93: `az resource show --name "bastion-foundry-test" --resource-group $rg --resource-type "Microsoft.Network/bastionHosts"`

## ⚠️ CRITICAL ISSUE TO FIX

**File**: `C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\scripts\01-deploy-foundry.ps1`
**Line**: 83
**Current**: `$deployCmd[9] = "foundry-byovnet-$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss')"`
**Should be**: `$deployCmd[11] = "foundry-byovnet-$(Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss')"`

This bug will cause the deployment parameters path to be overwritten with a deployment name string, resulting in deployment command failure on retry attempts.

<sub>⏱️ 313m 47s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

APIM 9 min, nadal trwa. Warsztat gotowy — **WORKSHOP.md** zawiera:

📋 **9 faz** krok po kroku:
1. Przygotowanie (config, az login)
2. Pre-flight (15 checków)
3. Deploy infrastruktury (Bicep, ~30 min)
4. Walidacja sieci (VNet, subnety, DNS)
5. Walidacja Private Endpoints
6. Walidacja Agent Service + izolacja
7. **⭐ Test portalu przez Bastion** — tworzenie agenta na VM
8. APIM (opcjonalnie)
9. Raport GO/NO-GO

Plus: timeline, koszty, pułapki, role, cleanup, notatki dla prowadzącego.

<sub>⏱️ 318m 14s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry networking options BYO VNet vs Managed VNet comparison requirements 2025**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry networking options BYO VNet vs Managed VNet comparison requirements 2025"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Here’s a detailed comparison of **Azure AI Foundry networking options: Bring Your Own (BYO) VNet vs. Managed VNet**, with an emphasis on requirements and recommendations relevant for 2025.\n\n---\n\n## 1. **Managed VNet (Microsoft-Managed)**\n- **Description:**  \n  Azure provisions and manages the virtual network isolation for your Foundry resource. You don’t need to create or manage your own VNet for the Foundry services; Microsoft configures everything.\n- **Isolation Modes:**  \n  - *Allow Internet Outbound:* Allows all outbound traffic to the internet.\n  - *Allow Only Approved Outbound:* Restricts outbound traffic to specific Azure services via private endpoints, service tags, or FQDN-based rules.\n- **Recommended For:**  \n  - Organizations that want simplified setup with built-in network security.\n  - Those who don’t need granular control over the networking topologies.\n- **Requirements:**  \n  - No need to provision or maintain VNets, subnets, or configure firewalls/NSGs for Foundry resources.\n  - Must review preview limitations (as Managed VNet may still be in preview as of 2025); not recommended for strict compliance or regulated environments【3:0†source】【3:2†source】.\n\n- **Limitations:**  \n  - Less flexibility for integrating with complex enterprise networks.\n  - Some capabilities may be limited or not production-ready if still in preview.\n- **Private Endpoints:**  \n  Managed by Microsoft; customers do not see NICs in their subscription.\n- **Switching Isolation Modes:**  \n  Requires redeploying the Foundry hub if you need to change the isolation mode.\n\n---\n\n## 2. **BYO VNet (Bring Your Own Virtual Network)**\n- **Description:**  \n  You provision and control the VNet where the Foundry resources are deployed (\"VNet injection\").\n- **Recommended For:**  \n  - Enterprises with strict compliance, governance, and security requirements.\n  - Scenarios needing integration with existing hub-and-spoke network architectures, custom firewalls, or complex routing.\n- **Requirements:**  \n  - **Existing VNet/subnet structure:** Must have VNets with enough address space and required subnets.\n  - **Subnet Delegation:** Required for Foundry compute (e.g., delegated to `Microsoft.App/environments`).\n  - **Azure Firewall/NVA:** For outbound access control and monitoring.\n  - **Private DNS Zones:** For private endpoint resolution, either linked directly or using custom DNS.\n  - **Role-based Access:** Sufficient Azure RBAC to provision, delegate subnets, and assign required roles.\n  - **Resource Provisioning:** Must create and manage Azure Storage, Cosmos DB, AI Search, and any additional dependencies in your own subscription.\n  - **Manual Integration:** More hands-on effort to configure NSGs, route tables, and ensure allowed connectivity for required Azure dependencies.\n- **Advantages:**  \n  - Maximum control over inbound/outbound networking, integration with on-premises (via ExpressRoute/VPN), compliance with regulatory requirements.\n  - Full visibility and management of all private endpoints and network interfaces.\n- **Template Support:**  \n  Microsoft and the community provide Bicep and Terraform templates for BYO VNet deployments.\n\n---\n\n## 3. **Key Comparison Table (2025):**\n\n| Feature                | Managed VNet                           | BYO VNet                           |\n|------------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|\n| Ownership              | Microsoft                              | Customer                            |\n| Network Control        | Limited                                | Full (inbound/outbound, NSGs, etc.) |\n| Compliance/Regulatory  | Suitable for general, less-regulated   | Suitable for regulated enterprise   |\n| Isolation Modes        | Set by Azure, limited customization    | Fully customizable                  |\n| Private Endpoint Mgmt  | Invisible to customer, managed by MS   | Fully visible and managed           |\n| Integration            | Simple, minimal setup                  | Complex, integrates with enterprise |\n| Custom Routing/Firewall| Not available                          | Fully supported                     |\n| Production Ready (2025)| Likely GA, check preview status        | Yes (supported, recommended for prod)|\n| Setup Complexity       | Low                                    | High                                |\n| On-prem Integration    | Limited                                | Full (ExpressRoute, VPN)            |\n\n---\n\n## 4. **When to Use Each Option**\n\n- **Use Managed VNet if:**\n  - You want rapid onboarding with minimal infrastructure overhead.\n  - You don’t have strict compliance requirements.\n  - Preview features are acceptable for your use case.\n\n- **Use BYO VNet if:**\n  - You need end-to-end control over networking policies and integration with corporate networks.\n  - Compliance, audit, and isolation requirements are strict and non-negotiable.\n  - You are deploying to production and/or regulated industries.\n\n---\n\n## 5. **2025 Considerations**\n- Managed VNet may reach GA but always check the latest Azure documentation for preview features.\n- For regulated workloads (finance, health, government), BYO VNet is generally preferred.\n- Both modes support private endpoint connection to core Azure data services, but BYO offers deeper customization.\n- Azure regularly updates templates and best practices—leverage current Bicep/Terraform modules for automation.\n\n---\n\n**References & Further Reading:**  \n- [Microsoft Docs: Managed VNet for Foundry (Preview)]【3:0†source】\n- [Securing Microsoft Foundry with Bring Your Own VNET]【3:1†source】\n- [GitHub: AI Foundry Networking Isolation](https://github.com/strudel0209/ai-foundry-infra-networking)【3:2†source】\n- [Standard vs. Private Networking in Azure Foundry](https://argonsys.com/microsoft-cloud/library/built-in-enterprise-readiness-with-azure-ai-agent-service/)【3:4†source】\n- [Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service – Microsoft Learn]【3:7†source】\n\nIf you need deep-dive deployment templates or specific compliance mapping for your industry, please specify!","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":1159,"end_index":1171,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure managed virtual network for Microsoft Foundry projects","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/how-to/managed-virtual-network"}},{"text":"","start_index":1171,"end_index":1183,"url_citation":{"title":"ai-foundry-infra-networking/README.md at main - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/strudel0209/ai-foundry-infra-networking/blob/main/README.md"}},{"text":"","start_index":5540,"end_index":5552,"url_citation":{"title":"Configure managed virtual network for Microsoft Foundry projects","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/how-to/managed-virtual-network"}},{"text":"","start_index":5608,"end_index":5620,"url_citation":{"title":"Securing Microsoft Foundry with Bring Your Own VNET · Cloud & AI ...","url":"https://nirmalt.com/posts/securingmicrosoftfoundrywithbyovnet/"}},{"text":"","start_index":5724,"end_index":5736,"url_citation":{"title":"ai-foundry-infra-networking/README.md at main - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/strudel0209/ai-foundry-infra-networking/blob/main/README.md"}},{"text":"","start_index":5894,"end_index":5906,"url_citation":{"title":"Built-in Enterprise Readiness with Azure AI Agent Service","url":"https://argonsys.com/microsoft-cloud/library/built-in-enterprise-readiness-with-azure-ai-agent-service/"}},{"text":"","start_index":5980,"end_index":5992,"url_citation":{"title":"Set up private networking for Foundry Agent Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks"}}]},"bing_searches":[{"text":"Azure AI Foundry networking options BYO VNet vs Managed VNet comparison requirements 2025","url":"https://www.bing.com/search?q=Azure+AI+Foundry+networking+options+BYO+VNet+vs+Managed+VNet+comparison+requirements+2025"}],"annotations":null}

{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Here are the **2025 subnet and address space requirements and restrictions for the Azure AI Foundry Agent Service** (including the latest Microsoft Foundry/AI Foundry releases):\n\n### 1. Supported Address Spaces & Subnet Classes\n- **Class A (10.0.0.0/8):** Supported and GA (generally available) in select regions including East US, West Europe, etc. (see official documentation for full list of supported regions).\n- **Class B (172.16.0.0/12) and Class C (192.168.0.0/16):** Supported and GA in all Azure AI Foundry Agent Service supported regions.\n- *Deployment templates and setup steps are the same for all subnet classes.*【3:1†source】\n\n### 2. Subnet Sizing Restrictions\n- **Agent Subnet Minimum Size:** /26 or larger (provides at least 64 IP addresses). This is to handle scaling and service delegation requirements.\n- **Private Endpoints Subnet:** Recommended to be /27 or larger, separate from the Agent subnet. No delegation is required for the Private Endpoints subnet.【3:2†source】\n\n### 3. Reserved IP Ranges and Address Conflicts\n- Azure reserves certain IP ranges for internal networking and platform management. Common reserved/private IP ranges include:\n    - 168.63.129.16/32 (Azure DNS and management traffic)\n    - 169.254.0.0/16 (Link-local addresses)\n    - Avoid address range overlaps with existing VNets, peered VNets, ExpressRoute, or on-premises networks.\n- **Ensure your chosen subnet does not overlap with other subnets or reserved spaces to avoid deployment failures.**【3:2†source】\n\n### 4. Delegation Requirements\n- **The Agent subnet must be delegated to `Microsoft.App/environments`**, enabling VNet injection for agent compute and isolation.\n- Private Endpoints subnet requires no delegation.\n\n### 5. Network Security Best Practices\n- Use private endpoints and network rules (deny-by-default).\n- No public egress is allowed by default—traffic routes through private endpoints for all core Azure resources (Storage, Cosmos DB, etc.).\n- Leverage private DNS zones to resolve endpoints within your VNet.【3:4†source】【3:1†source】\n\n### 6. Documentation Reference & Sample Templates\n- Microsoft provides official IaC (Bicep/Terraform) templates reflecting these requirements.\n- Consult the [Azure AI Foundry Agent Service documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/foundry/set-private-network-agent-service) and [GitHub samples](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup) for up-to-date setup patterns.\n\n---\n\n**Summary:**  \nFor a successful and compliant Azure AI Foundry Agent Service deployment in 2025, use a /26 or larger dedicated subnet, select Class A/B/C address ranges appropriate for your region, avoid address overlaps with any reserved or conflicting ranges, and always delegate the Agent subnet as required. Reserved platform ranges (such as 168.63.129.16/32) must not be assigned to your subnets.\n\nFor the most up-to-date requirements and supported regions, always consult Microsoft's official Learn documentation and release notes.【3:1†source】【3:2†source】【3:4†source】","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":626,"end_index":638,"url_citation":{"title":"Azure AI Agent Service: Standard Agent Setup with E2E Network ... - 1. # 🏗️ Workshop: Microsoft Foundry dla klientów enterprise
2. ## Wdrożenie AI Foundry z BYO VNet — krok po kroku
3. 
4. ---
5. 
6. ## 📋 O warsztacie
7. 
8. | | |
9. |---|---|
10. | **Czas trwania** | ~2-3 godziny |
11. | **Poziom** | Intermediate |
12. | **Wymagania** | Azure CLI, subskrypcja Azure, uprawnienia Owner |
13. | **Rezultat** | Działające środowisko AI Foundry w prywatnej sieci VNet z pełną izolacją |
14. 
15. ### Czego się nauczysz
16. 
17. 1. Jak wdrożyć Microsoft AI Foundry w prywatnej sieci klienta (BYO VNet)
18. 2. Jak skonfigurować Private Endpoints dla pełnej izolacji sieciowej
19. 3. Jak przetestować, że dane nigdy nie opuszczają sieci prywatnej
20. 4. Jak uzyskać dostęp do portalu ai.azure.com z wnętrza VNet
21. 5. Jak wystawić modele przez API Management w sieci wewnętrznej
22. 6. Jakie role RBAC są wymagane i dlaczego
23. 
24. ### Architektura docelowa
25. 
26. ```
27. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
28. │  Twoja sieć VNet (192.168.0.0/16)                                │
29. │                                                                  │
30. │  ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
31. │  │ snet-agent   │    │ snet-pe (Private Endpoints)             │  │
32. │  │ /24          │    │ /24                                     │  │
33. │  │              │    │ PE → AI Foundry (CognitiveServices)     │  │
34. │  │ Delegacja:   │    │ PE → Storage Account (blob)             │  │
35. │  │ Microsoft.   │    │ PE → CosmosDB (documents)               │  │
36. │  │ App/envs     │    │ PE → AI Search                          │  │
37. │  │              │    │                                         │  │
38. │  │ Agent        │    │ Cały ruch AI zostaje w Twojej sieci     │  │
39. │  │ Runtime      │    │                                         │  │
40. │  └─────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
41. │                                                                  │
42. │  ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐        │
43. │  │ Bastion     │    │ Jumpbox VM   │    │ VPN Gateway   │        │
44. │  │ /26         │    │ Windows      │    │ P2S           │        │
45. │  │ Bezpieczny  │    │ Dostęp do    │    │ Zdalny dostęp │        │
46. │  │ RDP/SSH     │    │ ai.azure.com │    │ dla zespołu   │        │
47. │  └─────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └───────────────┘        │
48. │                                                                  │
49. │  Private DNS Zones (6 stref) → rozwiązują nazwy na prywatne IP  │
50. └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
````

</details>

260. .\scripts\01-deploy-foundry.ps1
261. ```
262. 
263. ### Co się tworzy?
264. 
265. | Zasób | Cel | Czas |
266. |-------|-----|------|
267. | VNet + 5 subnetów | Sieć prywatna z izolacją | ~1 min |
268. | AI Services Account | Konto Foundry z networkInjections | ~3 min |
269. | Storage Account | Pliki agentów (blob) | ~1 min |
270. | AI Search | Vector store dla agentów | ~2 min |
271. | CosmosDB | Thread storage (konwersacje) | ~3 min |
272. | 4× Private Endpoints | Izolacja sieciowa | ~2 min |
273. | 6× Private DNS Zones | Rozwiązywanie nazw wewnątrz VNet | ~1 min |
274. | Model gpt-4.1 | Deployment modelu GlobalStandard | ~1 min |
275. | Project + Connections | Projekt z 3 połączeniami | ~2 min |
276. | Capability Host | Agent runtime | ~2 min |
277. | 9× RBAC Assignments | Tożsamości zarządzane | ~1 min |
278. | Bastion + Jumpbox VM | Dostęp do portalu | ~5 min |
279. | VPN Gateway P2S | Zdalny dostęp | ~25 min |
280. 
281. > ⏱️ **Łączny czas: ~25-35 min** (VPN Gateway jest najdłuższy)
282. 
283. > 💡 Skrypt ma wbudowany retry logic — jeśli konto AI potrzebuje więcej czasu na provisioning, automatycznie poczeka i ponowi próbę.
284. 
285. ### Oczekiwany wynik
286. 
287. ```
288. ✅ Deployment SUCCEEDED
289. ```
290.
````

</details>

424. ## 🖥️ Faza 7: Test portalu przez Bastion (10 min)

618. ## 📚 Materiały dodatkowe
619. 
620. - [Oficjalny template Microsoft](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)
621. - [Azure AI Foundry — prywatne sieci](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/environment-setup)
622. - [BYO VNet — wymagania subnetów](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/concepts/networking)
623. 
624. ---
625. 
626. ## 📝 Notatki dla prowadzącego
627. 
628. ### Role wymagane dla uczestników warsztatu
629. 
630. Na **Account AI Services** (lub Project):
631. - `Azure AI Developer` — tworzenie agentów i deploymentów
632. - `Cognitive Services OpenAI User` — dostęp do modeli
633. 
634. Na **zasobach** (Storage, Search, CosmosDB):
635. - `Storage Blob Data Contributor`
636. - `Search Index Data Contributor`  
637. - `Cosmos DB Operator` (control plane) + `Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor` (data plane)
638. 
639. ### Koszty szacunkowe
640. 
641. | Zasób | Koszt/h (est.) |
642. |-------|---------------|

**Azure AI Foundry project-level RBAC isolation multiple projects per-user access control CosmosDB Search Storage**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry project-level RBAC isolation multiple projects per-user access control CosmosDB Search Storage"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"You’re asking about how to implement **project-level RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) isolation** in an **Azure AI Foundry** scenario where:\n\n- Multiple projects might exist.\n- Each user can have different access rights per project.\n- Data sources include **Cosmos DB**, **Azure Search (Cognitive Search)**, and **Azure Storage**.\n\nHere’s an overview of **best practices and options** for handling **per-project, per-user access control** and resource isolation.\n\n---\n\n## 1. **Azure RBAC for Resource Isolation**\n\n### **At Azure Resource Level**\n- **Resource Groups per Project:** Place each project’s resources (Cosmos DB, Storage, Search) into its **own resource group**.\n- Assign users or groups only to the resource group for their project using built-in or custom Azure roles.\n\n#### **Pros:**\n- **Clear isolation**, easy to audit.\n- Works well for strict, organization-level separation.\n\n#### **Cons:**\n- Can be cumbersome with many small projects.\n- Access granularity is at resource/container, not row/document/file.\n\n---\n\n## 2. **Data Plane Security (within resources)**\n\n### **CosmosDB**\n- **Partition data by project:** Use a `projectId` property in your documents/collections.\n- **Managed Identity or AAD Authentication:** Use [Azure AD identities](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-setup-rbac) to access Cosmos DB with RBAC. You can assign **built-in roles** (`Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader`, etc.) at the database/container level.\n- For more granularity (per-document), **you must enforce project-level access in your application code**, i.e., users can only query/view documents for projects they have access to.\n\n### **Azure Search (Cognitive Search)**\n- No native \"project\" access isolation within a single index.\n- Solution: **Create one index per project** or incorporate a `projectId`/`projectKey` in each document and filter queries in your application based on user rights.\n- Secure Search API keys using Azure Key Vault and only provide keys with proper permissions.\n\n### **Azure Storage**\n- **Separate containers per project** is the cleanest solution.\n- Leverage Azure AD–based access to containers or use [Access Policies](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-access-policy-manage).\n\n---\n\n## 3. **Application-Level RBAC**\n\n**Critical for per-user, per-project access!**\n\n- Store project memberships and user roles in a central database/service.\n- All data service calls (Cosmos, Search, Storage) are proxied through your app/API, which checks the user’s rights before performing any operations.\n- Consider using managed identities or user-assigned identities for the app service itself.\n\n---\n\n## 4. **Best Practices**:\n\n- **Minimize Data Exposure:** Always scope queries based on user’s project list.\n- **Log and Audit:** Monitor resource and data access for compliance.\n- **Automate Resource Provisioning:** Use Infrastructure-as-Code tools for consistent, auditable deployment.\n- For large-scale scenarios, consider a [multi-tenant architecture](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/solution-ideas/articles/saas-multitenant-app-architecture).\n\n---\n\n## Example Workflow\n\n1. **User logs in via Azure AD**.\n2. **App looks up user’s allowed projects** (project-user matrix).\n3. For **Cosmos DB**, query only where `projectId in user.projects`.\n4. For **Search**, query with filter: `projectId eq 'projectX'` or use index per project.\n5. For **Storage**, access only containers mapped to user’s projects.\n\n---\n\n## **References & Docs**\n\n- [Azure Cosmos DB RBAC](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-setup-rbac)\n- [Azure Cognitive Search Security](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-security-overview)\n- [Azure Storage RBAC](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-auth-aad-rbac-portal)\n- [RBAC patterns for SaaS/Projects](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/solution-ideas/articles/saas-multitenant-app-architecture)\n\n---\n\nIf you elaborate on your architecture—e.g., “We want each data source shared across projects but with row-level isolation,” or “Each project gets a separate Cosmos DB container”—I can offer more tailored guidance or code samples!","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

**Azure AI Foundry RBAC scope project level vs hub level role assignment isolation between projects documentation 2025**

{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"Here’s a summary of how Azure AI Foundry RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) works at the hub and project level, specifically regarding scope and the isolation of role assignments between projects, as described in the latest documentation for 2025:\n\n### Core Structure of Azure AI Foundry\n\n- **Hub:** This is the central management and governance boundary. Hubs manage shared infrastructure, networking, centralized security policies, managed identities, and overall enforcement of governance (e.g., compliance, networking, key management). Role assignments at the hub level control who can manage infrastructure and create projects within that hub.\n- **Project:** Projects exist within a hub and represent isolated workspaces for specific AI applications or workloads. Each project inherits security and governance policies from the hub but enforces isolation for its own resources, identity, and RBAC assignments. Projects are where development, model training, deployment, and experimentation actually occur.\n\n### RBAC Scope: Hub vs. Project\n\n- **Hub-Level Role Assignments:** Users granted roles at the hub level have access to create and manage projects, configure infrastructure, and control global policies (e.g., managed networking, encryption, access to shared resources). These roles *do not* grant detailed project-level access unless explicitly propagated or assigned at the project level. Hub-level roles include Owner, Contributor, Azure AI Administrator, etc. Only users with appropriate hub permissions can assign roles at the project level for projects within that hub【3:4†source】【3:9†source】.\n- **Project-Level Role Assignments:** These roles control access within a specific project and do not cross project boundaries. For example, a user assigned as a Contributor or Manager in ProjectA has no access to ProjectB unless also explicitly assigned there. Project-level RBAC includes fine-grained roles like Azure AI Project Manager, Model Contributor, Data Scientist, etc., limited in scope to the resources of the respective project【3:4†source】【3:5†source】【3:7†source】.\n\n### Isolation Between Projects\n\n- **RBAC Isolation:** Project-level role assignments are isolated; a role assignment in one project does not grant any permissions in another. Each project acts as a container with its own set of resources, identities, and role assignments, enforcing strong multi-tenancy and separation between teams or workloads.\n- **Inheritance:** Projects inherit hub policies regarding governance and security defaults (such as networking controls), but RBAC assignments for users and managed identities do *not* flow down unless specifically assigned at the project level.\n- **Best Practices:** Enterprises typically set broad access controls and governance at the hub level, while teams or developers are then given scoped, minimal access at the project level to ensure least-privilege and isolation. Only the required users and service principals are assigned to each project.\n\n### Summary Table\n\n| Scope    | Typical Roles Assigned                    | What Access Controls | Isolation?         |\n|----------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|\n| Hub      | Owner, Contributor, Administrator         | Hub infra & project creation | RBAC limited to hub management, not project assets  |\n| Project  | Project Manager, Model Contributor, etc.  | Resources in the specific project | Completely isolated between projects                 |\n\n### References for 2025\n\n- Microsoft official documentation on RBAC scopes for Foundry, including side-by-side explanation of hub and project level access and their isolation【3:4†source】【3:9†source】.\n- Details on Foundry’s layered resource hierarchy and security-driven separation of concerns【3:5†source】.\n- Conceptual breakdown from industry blog posts on how isolation and inheritance work between hubs and projects【3:1†source】【3:6†source】.\n\nFor the most authoritative and up-to-date details, always refer to Microsoft’s official Learn documentation on [Role-based access control for Microsoft Foundry (Hubs and Projects)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/hub-rbac-foundry) and [RBAC for Projects](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry)【3:4†source】【3:9†source】.","annotations":[{"text":"","start_index":1582,"end_index":1594,"url_citation":{"title":"Role-based <sub>⏱️ 325m 57s</sub>

### ✅ `web_search`

**Azure AI Foundry project level RBAC CosmosDB Search Storage shared resources isolation how to scope per project data plane access**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "query": "Azure AI Foundry project level RBAC CosmosDB Search Storage shared resources isolation how to scope per project data plane access"
}
```

</details>

```
{"type":"output_text","text":{"value":"You're asking how to **scope data plane access (like queries and writes) to CosmosDB, Azure AI Search, and Storage in a shared Azure AI Foundry environment**, so that **each project** has isolated access even when the resources are shared at a higher level.\n\nThis scenario is common in multi-project environments where compute or management is shared, but each project must only access its own data in shared services.\n\n## Main Challenge\n\n**Azure RBAC** controls **management-plane** operations (resource creation, configuration, etc.).  \n**Data plane access** (e.g., read/write/query on CosmosDB, Search, Storage) is often scoped via:\n- Resource-level keys/secrets or\n- Azure AD Identity-based access (using RBAC or ABAC where supported).\n\n**Key goal:**  \n**Projects** must only see **their own data** in shared resources—no cross-project queries.\n\n---\n\n## Approaches by Service\n\n### **1. CosmosDB**\n- **Data Plane RBAC (Preview) or Resource Tokens:**  \n   - **Azure AD-based data plane RBAC**: Restrict access by assigning permissions to specific CosmosDB containers/collections matching project boundaries.  \n   - **Resource tokens** (for programmatic restriction): Can generate per-project tokens scoped to their container/partition key.\n   - **Partitioning:** Use one database but separate containers for each project, or strict partition key management.\n\n- **Isolation pattern:**  \n   - Create one **container per project** (ideal for strong isolation; possible in multi-tenant scenarios).\n   - Use **RBAC/role assignments for data plane** (`Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader/Writer`) at the container level, mapping user/service identity to the right scope.\n\n> **Reference:** [CosmosDB Data Plane RBAC](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-setup-rbac) (for overview and role granularity) .\n\n---\n\n### **2. Azure AI Search**\n- **Index-level API Keys or Azure AD:**  \n   - AI Search supports **admin/query API keys** (not project-scoped) and now also supports **Azure AD** authentication for data plane.\n   - Create **one index per project** and use an Azure AD app identity with allowed access only to the project's index.\n   - If you use API keys, you must create a custom service layer to multiplex keys per index/project, as the keys aren’t per-index.\n\n- **Isolation pattern:**  \n   - **Per-project index** with Azure AD Role Assignment on that index (where supported).\n   - Or handle routing/isolation at the application/API level.\n\n---\n\n### **3. Azure Storage (Blob/File/Table)**\n- **Container-level RBAC/ACLs:**  \n   - Use **one storage account** but create a **container per project** (Blob/Queue/Table).\n   - Use **Azure RBAC for Data Actions** to assign scoped access (e.g., Storage Blob Data Reader/Contributor) at the container level.\n   - Azure AD identities (users/apps) mapped to **container-level** permissions.\n\n- **Isolation pattern:**\n    - Assign identities with rights only to their own project's containers.\n\n---\n\n## General Pattern: **Scoped Identity + Per-Project Resource**\n\n1. **Create per-project containers/collections/indexes within the shared resource.**\n2. **Assign Azure AD roles (RBAC) for the data plane** at the correct scope (container, collection, index) to project service principals or managed identities.\n3. **Applications use managed identities** (or client credentials) tied to the project so they naturally get the right scope when authenticating with these resources.\n\n---\n\n## Example (Cosmos DB Per-Container RBAC):\n\n1. **Resource structure**\n   - CosmosDB Account: `my-ai-foundry-cosmos`\n   - Containers: `projectA-data`, `projectB-data`\n\n2. **Identity assignment**\n   - Assign `Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader/Writer` to `projectA-spn` on `projectA-data` container only.\n\n3. **Application flow**\n   - The code for ProjectA uses its own identity, and Azure authorization ensures it can only read/write its container.\n\n---\n\n## Limitations/Notes\n\n- **Not all services support data plane RBAC at every granularity**. E.g., some may only support at the resource, not nested objects (e.g., Storage supports at container level, CosmosDB at the container not at partition).\n- Some require **custom proxy/service layer** for fine-grained access control if this isn't natively supported per resource.\n- Always monitor for **feature updates** in Azure, as data plane RBAC scopes are expanding.\n\n---\n\n## References\n\n- [Cosmos DB Data Plane RBAC](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-setup-rbac) \n- [Azure Search Security Overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-security-overview) \n- [Azure Storage Data Access RBAC](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-auth-aad-rbac) \n\nIf you need **specific ARM role assignment examples** or a sample **Terraform or Bicep** configuration for this pattern, let me know your preferred tool and target service.","annotations":null},"bing_searches":null,"annotations":null}
```

### ✅ `web_fetch`

**https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/rbac-azure-ai-foundry?pivots=hub-project**

<details>

  "url": "https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/rbac-azure-ai-foundry?pivots=hub-project",
  "max_length": 15000

<details>
<summary>182 lines</summary>

````
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/rbac-azure-ai-foundry?pivots=hub-project:
In this article, you learn core role-based access control (RBAC) concepts for Microsoft Foundry, including scopes, built-in roles, and common enterprise assignment patterns.

Tip

RBAC roles apply when you authenticate using Microsoft Entra ID. If you use key-based authentication instead, the key grants full access without role restrictions. Microsoft recommends using Entra ID authentication for improved security and granular access control.

For more information about authentication and authorization in Microsoft Foundry, see [Authentication and Authorization](authentication-authorization-foundry).

## Minimum role assignments to get started

For new users to Azure and Microsoft Foundry, start with these minimum assignments so both your user principal and project managed identity can access Foundry features.

You can verify current assignments by using [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).

*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **user principal**.
*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **project's managed identity**.

If the user who created the project can assign roles (for example, by having the Azure **Owner** role at subscription or resource group scope), both assignments are added automatically.

To assign these roles manually, use the following quick steps.

### Assign a role to your user principal

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry resource and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **User, group, or service principal**, select your user principal, and then select **Review + assign**.

### Assign a role to your project's managed identity

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry project and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **Managed identity**, select your project's managed identity, and then select **Review + assign**.

## Terminology for role-based access control in Foundry

To understand role-based access control in Microsoft Foundry, consider two questions for your enterprise.

*   What permissions do I want my team to have when building in Microsoft Foundry?
*   At what scope do I want to assign permissions to my team?

To help answer these questions, here are descriptions of some terminology used throughout this article.

*   **Permissions**: Allowed or denied actions that an identity can perform on a resource, such as reading, writing, deleting, or managing both control plane and data plane operations.
*   **Scope**: The set of Azure resources to which a role assignment applies. Typical scopes include subscription, resource group, Foundry resource, or Foundry project.
*   **Role**: A named collection of permissions that defines which actions can be performed on Azure resources at a given scope.

An identity gets a _role_ with specific _permissions_ at a selected _scope_ based on your enterprise requirements.

In Microsoft Foundry, consider two scopes when completing role assignments.

*   **Foundry resource**: The top-level scope that defines the administrative, security, and monitoring boundary for a Microsoft Foundry environment.
*   **Foundry project**: A sub-scope within a Foundry resource used to organize work and enforce access control for Foundry APIs, tools, and developer workflows.

## Built-in roles

A **built-in role** in Foundry is a role created by Microsoft that covers common access scenarios that you can assign to your team members. Key built-in roles used across Azure include Owner, Contributor, and Reader. These roles aren't specific to Foundry resource permissions.

For Foundry resources, use additional built-in roles to follow least-privilege access principles. The following table lists key built-in roles for Foundry and links to the exact role definitions in [AI + Machine Learning built-in roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles/ai-machine-learning).

Role

Description

**Azure AI User**

Grants reader access to Foundry project, Foundry resource, and data actions for your Foundry project. If you can assign roles, this role is assigned to you automatically. Otherwise, your subscription Owner or a user with role assignment permissions grants it. Least privilege access role in Foundry.

**Azure AI Project Manager**

Lets you perform management actions on Foundry projects, build and develop with projects, and conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Account Owner**

Grants full access to manage projects and resources, and lets you conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Owner**

Grants full access to managed projects and resources and build and develop with projects. Highly privileged self-serve role designed for digital natives.

### Permissions for each built-in role

Use the following table to see the permissions allowed for each built-in role in Microsoft Foundry.

Built-in role

Create Foundry projects

Create Foundry accounts

Build and develop in a project (data actions)

Complete role assignments

Reader access to projects and accounts

Manage models

Publish agents

**Azure AI User**

✔

✔

**Azure AI Project Manager**

✔

✔ (only assign Azure AI User role)

✔

✔

**Azure AI Account Owner**

✔

✔

✔ (only assign Azure AI User role)

✔

✔

**Azure AI Owner**

✔

✔

✔

✔

✔

✔

Use the following table to see the permissions allowed for each key Azure built-in roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader).

Built-in role

Create Foundry projects

Create Foundry accounts

Build and develop in a project (data actions)

Complete role assignments

Reader access to projects and accounts

Manage models

Publish agents

**Owner**

✔

✔

✔ (assign any role to any user)

✔

✔

✔

**Contributor**

✔

✔

✔

✔

**Reader**

✔

To publish agents, you need the **Azure AI Project Manager** role (minimum) on the Foundry resource scope. For more information, see [Agent applications in Microsoft Foundry](../agents/how-to/agent-applications).

[![Diagram of the built-in roles in Foundry.](../media/how-to/network/detailed-rbac-diagram.png)](../media/how-to/network/detailed-rbac-diagram.png#lightbox)

For more on built-in roles in Azure and Foundry, see [Azure built-in roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles). To learn more about conditional delegation used in the Azure AI Account Owner and Azure AI Project Manager role, see [Delegate Azure role assignment management to others with conditions](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/delegate-role-assignments-portal).

## Sample enterprise RBAC mappings for projects

Here's an example of how to implement role-based access control (RBAC) for an enterprise Foundry resource.

Persona

Role and Scope

Purpose

IT admin

Owner on subscription scope

The IT admin ensures the Foundry resource meets enterprise standards. Assign managers the **Azure AI Account Owner** role on the resource to let them create new Foundry accounts. Assign managers the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource to let them create projects within an account.

Managers

Azure AI Account Owner on Foundry resource scope

Managers manage the Foundry resource, deploy models, audit compute resources, audit connections, and create shared connections. They can't build in projects, but they can assign the **Azure AI User** role to themselves and others to start building.

Team lead or lead developer

Azure AI Project Manager on Foundry resource scope

Lead developers create projects for their team and start building in those projects. After you create a project, project owners invite other members and assign the **Azure AI User** role.

Team members or developers

Azure AI User on Foundry project scope and Reader on the Foundry resource scope

Developers build agents in a project with pre-deployed Foundry models and pre-built connections.

## Manage role assignments

To manage roles in Foundry, you must have permission to assign and remove roles in Azure. The Azure built-in **Owner** role includes that permission. You can assign roles through the Foundry portal (Admin page), Azure portal IAM, or Azure CLI. You can remove roles by using Azure portal IAM or Azure CLI.

In the Foundry portal, manage permissions by:

1.  Open the **Admin** page in [Foundry](https://ai.azure.com), then select **Operate** > **Admin**.
2.  Select your project name.
3.  Select **Add user** to manage project access. This action is available only if you have role-assignment permissions.
4.  Apply the same flow for Foundry resource-level access.

You can manage permissions in the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com) under **Access Control (IAM)** or by using Azure CLI.

For example, the following command assigns the Azure AI User role to `joe@contoso.com` for resource group `this-rg` in subscription `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`:

```
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" --assignee "joe@contoso.com" --scope /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/this-rg 
```

## Create custom roles for projects

If the built-in roles don't meet your enterprise requirements, create a custom role that allows for precise control over allowed actions and scopes. Here's an example subscription-level custom role definition:

```
{
  "properties": {
    "roleName": "My Enterprise Foundry User",
    "description": "Custom role for Foundry at my enterprise to only allow building Agents. Assign at subscription level.",
    "assignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<your-subscription-id>"],
    "permissions": [ { 
        "actions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/*/read", "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read", "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/listkeys/action","Microsoft.Resources/deployments/*"], 
        "notActions": [], 
        "dataActions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/AIServices/agents/*"], 
        "notDataActions": []     
    } ]
  }
}
```

For more information on creating a custom role, see the following articles.

*   [Azure portal](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-portal)
*   [Azure CLI](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-cli)
*   [Azure PowerShell](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-powershell)
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features). This article provides more details on specific permissions in Foundry across control and data plane which you can utilize when building custom roles.

## Notes and limitations

*   To view and purge deleted Foundry accounts, you must have the Contributor role assigned at the subscription scope.
*   Users with the Contributor role can deploy models in Foundry.
*   You need the Owner role on a resource's scope to create custom roles in the resource.
*   If you have permissions to role assign in Azure (for example, the Owner role assigned on the account scope) to your user principal, and you deploy a Foundry resource from the Azure portal or Foundry portal UI, then the Azure AI User role gets automatically assigned to your user principal. This assignment doesn't apply when deploying Foundry from SDK or CLI.
*   When you create a Foundry resource, the built-in role-based access control (RBAC) permissions give you access to the resource. To use resources created outside Foundry, ensure the resource has permissions that let you access it. Here are some examples:
    *   To use a new Azure Blob Storage account, add the Foundry account resource's managed identity to the Storage Blob Data Reader role on that storage account.
    *   To use a new Azure AI Search source, add Foundry to the Azure AI Search role assignments.
*   To fine-tune a model in Foundry, you need both data plane and control plane permissions. Deploying a fine-tuned model is a control plane permission. Therefore, the only built-in role with both data plane and control plane permissions is the **Azure AI Owner** role. Or, if you prefer, you can also assign the **Azure AI User** role for data plane permissions and the **Azure AI Account Owner** role for control plane permissions.

## Related content

*   [Create a project](../how-to/create-projects).
*   [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).
*   [Authentication and Authorization in Foundry](authentication-authorization-foundry).
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features).
*   [Hosted agent permissions reference](../agents/concepts/hosted-agent-permissions).

## Appendix

### Access Isolation Examples

Each organization may have different access isolation requirements depending on the user personas in their enterprise. Access isolation refers to which users in your enterprise are given what role assignments for either a separation of permissions using our built-in roles or a unified, highly permissive role. There are three access isolation options for Foundry that you can select for your organization depending on your access isolation requirements.

**No access isolation.** This means in your enterprise, you don't have any requirements separating permissions between a developer, project manager, or an admin. The permissions for these roles can be assigned across teams.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant all users in your enterprise the **Azure AI Owner** role on the resource scope

**Partial access isolation.** This means the project manager in your enterprise should be able to develop within projects as well as create projects. But your admins shouldn't be able to develop within Foundry, only create Foundry projects and accounts.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant your admin with **Azure AI Account Owner** on the resource scope
*   Grant your developer and project managers with **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource

**Full access isolation.** This means your admins, project managers, and developers have clear permissions assigned that don't overlap for their different functions within an enterprise.

Therefore you should...

*   Grant your admin the **Azure AI Account Owner** on resource scope
*   Grant your developer the **Reader** role on Foundry resource scope and **Azure AI User** on project scope
*   Grant your project manager the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on resource scope

### Use Microsoft Entra groups with Foundry

Microsoft Entra ID provides several ways to manage access to resources, applications, and tasks. By using Microsoft Entra groups, you can grant access and permissions to a group of users instead of to each individual user. Enterprise IT admins can create Microsoft Entra groups in the Azure portal to simplify the role assignment process for developers. When you create a Microsoft Entra group, you can minimize the number of role assignments required for new developers working on F

<note>Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of 15000 to get more content.</note>
````

</details>

### 💬 Copilot

Teraz mam pełny obraz. Oto odpowiedź:

Świetne pytanie! Oto jak to działa:

## Izolacja per-projekt w Foundry

### Scope RBAC — dwa poziomy

| Scope | Co kontroluje | Przykład |
|-------|---------------|---------|
| **Foundry Resource** (Account) | Kto tworzy projekty, zarządza infrastrukturą | Admin, Project Manager |
| **Foundry Project** | Kto pracuje W konkretnym projekcie | Developer, Data Scientist |

**Role nadane na Project NIE dają dostępu do innego Project** — izolacja jest wbudowana.

### Jak nadać dostęp per-projekt

```powershell
# User A → tylko Projekt 1
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" \
  --assignee "userA@firma.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/{account}/projects/{projekt1}"

# User B → tylko Projekt 2
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" \
  --assignee "userB@firma.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/{account}/projects/{projekt2}"
```

### A co z CosmosDB, Storage, Search?

Tu jest **kluczowa informacja** — te zasoby są **współdzielone** na poziomie Account:

| Zasób | Izolacja per-projekt? | Jak to działa |
|-------|----------------------|---------------|
| **CosmosDB** | ⚠️ Dane oddzielone logicznie | Foundry sam separuje dane per-projekt (kontenery/partycje). Managed Identity projektu ma dostęp, ale **data plane RBAC** (Cosmos DB Built-in roles) scopuje się na cały account, nie per-container |
| **Storage** | ⚠️ Osobne kontenery per-projekt | Foundry tworzy osobne kontenery. Ale RBAC jest na poziomie Storage Account |
| **AI Search** | ⚠️ Osobne indeksy per-projekt | Foundry separuje indeksy, ale RBAC jest na poziomie Search Service |

### Rekomendacja dla ścisłej izolacji

Jeśli regulacje **wymagają** pełnej izolacji danych:

1. **Osobne zasoby per-projekt** — każdy projekt z własnym Storage/CosmosDB/Search (droższe, ale pełna izolacja)
2. **Lub polegaj na Foundry** — Foundry logicznie separuje dane, a dostęp kontrolujesz rolami na Project scope

### Minimalna konfiguracja (2 projekty, 2 userów)

```
Account (Foundry Resource):
├── Admin → Azure AI Account Owner (scope: Account)
│
├── Projekt-1:
│   └── UserA → Azure AI User (scope: Projekt-1)
│   └── UserA → Reader (scope: Account) ← widzi Account ale nie inne projekty
│
└── Projekt-2:
    └── UserB → Azure AI User (scope: Projekt-2)
    └── UserB → Reader (scope: Account)
```

\> ⚠️ **NIE nadawaj** ról na Account scope (np. `Azure AI User` na Account) — to da dostęp do WSZYSTKICH projektów!

📎 Dokumentacja: [RBAC for Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/concepts/rbac-azure-ai-foundry?pivots=hub-project)

Czy chcesz żebym dodał to do WORKSHOP.md jako dodatkową sekcję o multi-tenant?

<sub>⏱️ 328m 51s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Dobre pytanie — i masz rację, że to trzeba wyjaśnić!

## Co było potrzebne, a co było "na zapas"

Kiedy rozwiązywaliśmy problem z portalem, zrobiłem trochę **shotgun approach** — nadawałem role "na wszelki wypadek". Teraz po przeczytaniu dokumentacji widzę, że:

### Dla **Managed Identity projektu** (te w Bicep) — ✅ WSZYSTKIE potrzebne
Te role są **wymagane** — bez nich Agent Service nie działa:
- Storage Blob Data Owner → agent potrzebuje dostępu do plików
- Search Index Data Contributor → agent potrzebuje vector store
- Cosmos DB Operator + Data Contributor → agent przechowuje wątki

### Dla **admina** (human user) — ⚠️ nadałem za dużo

| Rola nadana userowi | Potrzebna? | Dlaczego |
|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| **Azure AI Developer** | ✅ TAK | Bez tego nie stworzysz agenta |
| **Cognitive Services OpenAI User** | ⚠️ Pewnie NIE | Azure AI Developer powinien wystarczyć |
| **Storage Blob Data Contributor** | ❌ NIE | Portal używa MI projektu, nie Twojej tożsamości |
| **Search Index Data Contributor** | ❌ NIE | j.w. — portal idzie przez MI |
| **Cosmos DB Operator** | ❌ NIE | j.w. |

Błąd CosmosDB który widziałeś (`principal [836b9920...]`) — to był **Project MI**, nie Twój user! Naprawiłem go nadając `Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor` do **MI projektu**, a nie do Ciebie.

### Wniosek

Dla human usera wystarczy:
```
Azure AI User       → odczyt, przeglądanie
Azure AI Developer  → tworzenie agentów, praca z modelem
```
...nadane na scope **projektu** (nie account!).

Reszta (Storage, Search, CosmosDB) jest potrzebna tylko dla **Managed Identity projektu**, nie dla użytkowników.

Powinienem poprawić to w Bicep i WORKSHOP.md — usunąć niepotrzebne role dla usera. Chcesz żebym to teraz posprzątał?

<sub>⏱️ 334m 26s</sub>

### ✅ `powershell`

**Add Bastion/VM access roles for userlab01**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "command": "# Check if userlab01 needs any additional permissions for Bastion/VM access\n# Bastion requires: Reader on VM, Reader on NIC, Reader on Bastion resource or RG\n\n$sub = \"38b8b5d4-b3a1-4892-b2a7-3f5bcfdad59f\"\n$rg = \"rg-foundry-byovnet\"\n$userPrincipal = \"userlab01@MngEnvMCAP833611.onmicrosoft.com\"\n$rgScope = \"/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/$rg\"\n\n# User needs at minimum Reader on the RG to see VM and Bastion\nWrite-Host \"=== Current roles for userlab01 ===\"\naz role assignment list --assignee $userPrincipal --all --query \"[].{role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}\" -o table 2>&1\n\nWrite-Host \"\"\nWrite-Host \"=== Adding Reader on RG (needed for Bastion + VM access) ===\"\naz role assignment create --role \"Reader\" --assignee $userPrincipal --scope $rgScope -o none 2>&1\n\n# Also needs Virtual Machine User Login for AAD-based login, OR we use local credentials\nWrite-Host \"Adding Virtual Machine User Login on RG...\"\naz role assignment create --role \"Virtual Machine User Login\" --assignee $userPrincipal --scope $rgScope -o none 2>&1\n\nWrite-Host \"\"\nWrite-Host \"=== Updated roles ===\"\naz role assignment list --assignee $userPrincipal --all --query \"[].{role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}\" -o table 2>&1",
  "description": "Add Bastion/VM access roles for userlab01",
  "initial_wait": 30,
  "shellId": "multi-tenant"
}

<sub>⏱️ 335m 54s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Problem jasny. Masz **5 aktywnych Conditional Access policies**, w tym jedną krytyczną:

| Policy | Efekt |
|--------|-------|
| **"Security info registration..."** | ⛔ **BLOCK** — blokuje rejestrację MFA |
| **"Multifactor authentication..."** (×2) | Wymaga MFA |

To jest **catch-22**: MFA jest wymagane, ale rejestracja MFA jest zablokowana. Nowy user nie może ani zarejestrować MFA ani się zalogować.

Masz dwie opcje:

1. **Wykluczyć userlab01 z polityk MFA** (szybkie, na czas testu)
2. **Zalogować się jako admin na Bastion**, i tam w tej samej przeglądarce zalogować się jako userlab01 na ai.azure.com

Chcesz żebym wykluczył userlab01 z tych CA policies? To jest managed environment, więc powinno być bezpieczne na czas testów.

<sub>⏱️ 340m 54s</sub>

### ✅ `powershell`

**Add Azure AI User on Account scope**

<details>

{
  "command": "$sub = \"38b8b5d4-b3a1-4892-b2a7-3f5bcfdad59f\"\n$rg = \"rg-foundry-byovnet\"\n$account = \"ai-foundry-byovnet-unrkke4ibdxcm\"\n$userPrincipal = \"userlab01@MngEnvMCAP833611.onmicrosoft.com\"\n$accountScope = \"/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/$rg/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/$account\"\n\n# Portal explicitly asks for \"Azure AI User\" on the ACCOUNT resource\nWrite-Host \"Adding Azure AI User on Account scope...\"\naz role assignment create --role \"Azure AI User\" --assignee $userPrincipal --scope $accountScope -o none 2>&1\n\nWrite-Host \"\"\nWrite-Host \"=== Updated roles ===\"\naz role assignment list --assignee $userPrincipal --all --query \"[].{role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}\" -o table 2>&1",
  "description": "Add Azure AI User on Account scope",
  "initial_wait": 20,
  "shellId": "multi-tenant"
}
```

<sub>⏱️ 341m 3s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Dodałem. Odśwież portal i spróbuj ponownie.

### 🔍 Ważne odkrycia z tego testu:

**1. Agent Service wymaga `Azure AI User` na ACCOUNT** — rola na samym projekcie nie wystarczy. To oznacza że pełna izolacja per-projekt ma ograniczenia dla Agent Service.

**2. User widzi OBA projekty** — `Reader` na Account daje widoczność wszystkich projektów. Nie da się tego uniknąć — nawigacja portalu tego wymaga.

To są super cenne wnioski do warsztatu! Odśwież stronę i daj znać czy teraz `project-lab01` działa. A potem sprawdź czy możesz wejść do **oryginalnego projektu** i tam coś zrobić (to pokaże poziom izolacji).

---

<sub>⏱️ 342m 0s</sub>

### 👤 User

wszedłęm sobie teraz do project-agent-test i widze tam agenta test i mogę z nim gadac

---

<sub>⏱️ 342m 21s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

To jest **krytyczne odkrycie** dla warsztatu! 🚨

### Wynik testu izolacji:

| Test | Oczekiwanie | Rezultat |
|------|-------------|----------|
| Widzi project-lab01 | ✅ TAK | ✅ TAK |
| Widzi project-agent-test | ❌ NIE | ⚠️ **TAK — WIDZI!** |
| Może gadać z agentem w cudzym projekcie | ❌ NIE | ⚠️ **TAK — MOŻE!** |

### Wniosek:

**`Azure AI User` na Account = dostęp do WSZYSTKICH projektów**. Nie ma izolacji per-projekt, bo Agent Service **wymaga** tej roli na Account scope.

To jest **ograniczenie platformy** — nie da się mieć jednocześnie:
- Działającego Agent Service
- Izolacji między projektami w tym samym Account

### Dla klientów enterprise: dwie opcje

| Opcja | Izolacja | Koszt | Złożoność |
|-------|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Osobny Account per zespół** | ✅ Pełna | Wyższy (osobne zasoby) | Średnia |
| **Jeden Account, wiele projektów** | ❌ Brak dla agentów | Niższy | Niska |

To jest **super wartościowa informacja** dla klientów! Zapisuję to jako lesson learned. Chcesz żebym:
1. Dodał to do WORKSHOP.md jako ważną notatkę?
2. Kontynuował z testami APIM?

---

<sub>⏱️ 346m 31s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

🎉 **TO JEST MEGA ODKRYCIE!** 

### Wynik testu izolacji — FINAL:

| Test | Rezultat |
|------|----------|
| Widzi **tylko** project-lab01 | ✅ **TAK!** |
| NIE widzi project-agent-test | ✅ **Izolacja działa!** |
| Stworzył agenta (test10agent) | ✅ **Działa!** |
| Agent odpowiada | ✅ **Działa!** |
| Bez ról na CosmosDB/Storage/Search | ✅ **Nie potrzeba!** |

### Wnioski — to zmienia wszystko:

| Rola | Scope | Potrzebna? |
|------|-------|-----------|
| **Azure AI User** | Project | ✅ TAK |
| **Azure AI Developer** | Project | ✅ TAK (tworzenie agentów) |
| Reader na Account | Account | ❌ NIE (i nie dawać — bo widzi inne projekty!) |
| Storage/CosmosDB/Search | Zasoby | ❌ NIE (portal używa MI projektu) |

### Recepta dla enterprise multi-tenant:

```
Account (Foundry Resource):
├── Projekt-1: UserA → Azure AI User + Azure AI Developer (scope: Projekt-1)
└── Projekt-2: UserB → Azure AI User + Azure AI Developer (scope: Projekt-2)

Wynik: UserA widzi TYLKO Projekt-1, UserB widzi TYLKO Projekt-2 ✅
```

Błąd portalu który wcześniej widzieliśmy ("assign Azure AI User for the resource") był **mylący** — sugerował Account scope, ale Project scope wystarczy!

Dodaję to do WORKSHOP.md jako kluczowy finding. Chcesz kontynuować z APIM?

654. ## 📝 Notatki dla prowadzącego
655. 
656. ### Role wymagane dla uczestników warsztatu
657. 
658. Na **Account AI Services** (lub Project):
659. - `Azure AI Developer` — tworzenie agentów i deploymentów
660. - `Cognitive Services OpenAI User` — dostęp do modeli
661. 
662. Na **zasobach** (Storage, Search, CosmosDB):
663. - `Storage Blob Data Contributor`
664. - `Search Index Data Contributor`  
665. - `Cosmos DB Operator` (control plane) + `Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor` (data plane)
666. 
667. ### Koszty szacunkowe
668. 
669. | Zasób | Koszt/h (est.) |
670. |-------|---------------|
671. | AI Services (gpt-4.1 GlobalStandard) | Pay-per-token |
672. | Storage Account | ~$0.02/GB/mies. |
673. | AI Search (Basic) | ~$1/h |
674. | CosmosDB (Serverless) | Pay-per-RU |
675. | Bastion (Basic) | ~$0.19/h |
676. | VPN Gateway (VpnGw1AZ) | ~$0.19/h |
677. | Jumpbox VM (B2s) | ~$0.05/h |
678. | **APIM Developer** | ~$0.07/h |
679. 
680. > 💡 **Tip**: Po warsztacie natychmiast uruchom Cleanup aby uniknąć kosztów.
681. 
682. ### Timeline warsztatu
683. 
684. | Czas | Faza | Czynność |
685. |------|------|----------|
686. | 0:00 | Przygotowanie | Konfiguracja, az login |
687. | 0:10 | Faza 1 | Pre-flight |
688. | 0:15 | Faza 2 | Deploy (czekamy ~30 min) |
689. | 0:15-0:45 | Teoria | Omówienie architektury BYO VNet, PE, DNS |
690. | 0:45 | Faza 3-6 | Testy walidacyjne (sieć, PE, agent, RBAC) |
691. | 1:00 | Faza 7 | **⭐ Test portalu przez Bastion** |
692. | 1:15 | Faza 7.4 | Tworzenie agenta na VM |
693. | 1:30 | Faza 8 | APIM deploy (opcja, 45 min) |
694. | 2:00 | Faza 9 | Raport GO/NO-GO |
695. | 2:15 | Cleanup | Usunięcie zasobów |
696. | 2:30 | Q&A | Pytania i odpowiedzi |
697.
```
