No

Yes

Use Entra ID for managed identity tool access.

Evaluations

No

Yes

Use Entra ID.

Content safety analyze calls

Yes

Yes

Use RBAC to limit high-risk operations.

Batch analysis jobs (Content Understanding)

Yes

Yes

Entra ID recommended for scale.

Portal playground usage

Yes

Yes

Playground uses project connection mode.

Network isolation with Private Link

Yes

Yes

Entra ID adds conditional access.

Least privilege with built-in and custom roles

No

Yes

Keys are all-or-nothing per resource.

Managed identity (system or user-assigned)

No

Yes

Enables secret-less auth.

Per-request user attribution

No

Yes

Token contains tenant and object IDs.

Revocation (immediate)

Rotate key

Remove role or disable principal

Short token lifetime applies.

Support in automation pipelines

Yes (secret)

Yes (service principal or managed identity)

Entra ID reduces secret rotation.

Assistants API

Yes

Yes

Recommended to use Entra ID.

Batch inferencing

Yes

Yes

Toolbox

No

Yes

Use Entra ID for managed identity tool access.

## Identity types

Azure resources and applications authenticate by using different identity types, each designed for specific scenarios. User principals represent human users, service principals represent applications or automated processes, and managed identities provide a secure, credential-free way for Azure resources to access other services. Understanding these distinctions helps you choose the right identity for interactive sign-ins, app-to-app communication, or workload automation.

Azure supports the following identity types.

Identity type

Description

User principal

Individual user in Microsoft Entra ID

[Service principal](/en-us/entra/identity-platform/app-objects-and-service-principals) (app registration)

Application identity that uses a client secret or certificate

[Managed identity](/en-us/entra/identity/managed-identities-azure-resources/overview) (system-assigned)

Azure resource-bound identity automatically managed by the platform.

Managed identity (user-assigned)

Standalone identity that attaches to multiple resources.

## Built-in roles overview

In Foundry, use the built-in roles to separate the allowed actions for a user. Most enterprises want a separation of control and data plane actions for their built-in roles. Others expect a combined data and control plane role to minimize the number of role assignments required. The following table lists scenarios and the corresponding built-in Foundry roles that best fit each scenario.

Scenario

Typical built-in roles

Notes

Build agents with pre-deployed models

Azure AI User

Data plane usage only; no management writes.

Manage deployments or fine-tune models

Azure AI Project Manager

Includes model deployment creation and update.

Rotate keys or manage resource

Azure AI Account Owner

High privilege; consider custom role for least privilege.

Manage resource, manage deployments, build agents

Azure AI Owner

Highly privileged self-serve role for users who need both control plane and data plane access. Combine with Azure Monitor Reader if observability required.

Observability, tracing, monitoring

Azure AI User (minimum)

Add Azure Monitor Reader on Application Insights.

To understand the breakdown of built-in roles and the control and data plane actions, review the following diagram.

[![Diagram mapping built-in roles to control plane actions and data plane actions in Foundry.](../media/authentication-authorization/role-mapping-diagram.png)](../media/authentication-authorization/role-mapping-diagram.png#lightbox)

Tip

Create a custom role if a built-in role grants excess permissions for your use case.

## Set up Microsoft Entra ID

For high-level guidance on setting up Entra ID authentication in Foundry, see [Configure key-less authentication](../foundry-models/how-to/configure-entra-id).

1.  Ensure your Microsoft Foundry resource has a custom subdomain configured. See [Custom subdomains](/en-us/azure/ai-services/cognitive-services-custom-subdomains). A custom subdomain is required for token-based authentication.
    
2.  Assign the needed built-in or custom role to each principal. You need the **Owner** or **User Access Administrator** role at the target scope to assign roles. Common role assignments:
    
    *   **Azure AI User**: For developers who need to build and test with pre-deployed models.
    *   **Azure AI Project Manager**: For team leads who need to create projects and manage deployments.
    *   **Azure AI Account Owner**: For administrators who need full resource management and can conditionally assign Azure AI User for data plane access.
    *   **Azure AI Owner**: For users who need both full resource management and data plane access. Example CLI command to assign the Azure AI User role:
    
    ```
    az role assignment create \
      --assignee <principal-id> \
      --role "Azure AI User" \
      --scope /subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/<resource-group>/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/<resource-name>
    ```
    
    To verify the role assignment, run `az role assignment list --assignee <principal-id> --scope <resource-scope>` and confirm the role appears in the output.
    
3.  (Optional) For a service principal, create an app registration, add a client secret or certificate, and note the tenant ID, client ID, and secret or certificate.
    
4.  (Optional) For a managed identity, enable the system-assigned identity on the calling service or attach a user-assigned identity, then assign a role to it on the Foundry resource.
    
5.  Remove key-based authentication after all callers use token authentication. Optionally disable local authentication in deployment templates.
    

**Reference**: [Assign Azure roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/role-assignments-portal) | [Role-based access control for Foundry](rbac-foundry)

## Troubleshoot common authentication errors

Error

Cause

Resolution

401 Unauthorized

Missing or expired token; invalid API key

Verify the token acquisition scope is `https://ai.azure.com/.default`. Regenerate the API key if you use key-based auth.

403 Forbidden

Missing RBAC role assignment

Assign the appropriate built-in role (for example, Azure AI User) at the resource or project scope.

AADSTS700016

Application not found in tenant

Verify the app registration exists in the correct tenant and the client ID is correct.

Custom subdomain required

Resource uses a regional endpoint instead of a custom subdomain

Configure a [custom subdomain](/en-us/azure/ai-services/cognitive-services-custom-subdomains) on the Foundry resource. Token-based auth requires a custom subdomain.

## Related content

*   [Role-based access control for Foundry](rbac-foundry)
*   [Configure key-less authentication with Microsoft Entra ID](../foundry-models/how-to/configure-entra-id)
*   [Rotate API access keys](../../ai-services/rotate-keys?context=/azure/ai-foundry/context/context)
*   [Azure built-in roles (AI + machine learning)](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles#ai-+-machine-learning)
*   [Authentication vs. authorization (Microsoft Entra ID)](/en-us/entra/identity-platform/authentication-vs-authorization)
*   [Identity fundamentals](/en-us/entra/fundamentals/identity-fundamental-concepts)
````

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#sample-enterprise-rbac-mappings-for-projects:
In this article, you learn core role-based access control (RBAC) concepts for Microsoft Foundry, including scopes, built-in roles, and common enterprise assignment patterns.

Tip

RBAC roles apply when you authenticate using Microsoft Entra ID. If you use key-based authentication instead, the key grants full access without role restrictions. Microsoft recommends using Entra ID authentication for improved security and granular access control.

For more information about authentication and authorization in Microsoft Foundry, see [Authentication and Authorization](authentication-authorization-foundry).

## Minimum role assignments to get started

For new users to Azure and Microsoft Foundry, start with these minimum assignments so both your user principal and project managed identity can access Foundry features.

You can verify current assignments by using [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).

*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **user principal**.
*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **project's managed identity**.

If the user who created the project can assign roles (for example, by having the Azure **Owner** role at subscription or resource group scope), both assignments are added automatically.

To assign these roles manually, use the following quick steps.

### Assign a role to your user principal

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry resource and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **User, group, or service principal**, select your user principal, and then select **Review + assign**.

### Assign a role to your project's managed identity

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry project and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **Managed identity**, select your project's managed identity, and then select **Review + assign**.

## Terminology for role-based access control in Foundry

To understand role-based access control in Microsoft Foundry, consider two questions for your enterprise.

*   What permissions do I want my team to have when building in Microsoft Foundry?
*   At what scope do I want to assign permissions to my team?

To help answer these questions, here are descriptions of some terminology used throughout this article.

*   **Permissions**: Allowed or denied actions that an identity can perform on a resource, such as reading, writing, deleting, or managing both control plane and data plane operations.
*   **Scope**: The set of Azure resources to which a role assignment applies. Typical scopes include subscription, resource group, Foundry resource, or Foundry project.
*   **Role**: A named collection of permissions that defines which actions can be performed on Azure resources at a given scope.

An identity gets a _role_ with specific _permissions_ at a selected _scope_ based on your enterprise requirements.

In Microsoft Foundry, consider two scopes when completing role assignments.

*   **Foundry resource**: The top-level scope that defines the administrative, security, and monitoring boundary for a Microsoft Foundry environment.
*   **Foundry project**: A sub-scope within a Foundry resource used to organize work and enforce access control for Foundry APIs, tools, and developer workflows.

## Built-in roles

A **built-in role** in Foundry is a role created by Microsoft that covers common access scenarios that you can assign to your team members. Key built-in roles used across Azure include Owner, Contributor, and Reader. These roles aren't specific to Foundry resource permissions.

For Foundry resources, use additional built-in roles to follow least-privilege access principles. The following table lists key built-in roles for Foundry and links to the exact role definitions in [AI + Machine Learning built-in roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles/ai-machine-learning).

Role

Description

**Azure AI User**

Grants reader access to Foundry project, Foundry resource, and data actions for your Foundry project. If you can assign roles, this role is assigned to you automatically. Otherwise, your subscription Owner or a user with role assignment permissions grants it. Least privilege access role in Foundry.

**Azure AI Project Manager**

Lets you perform management actions on Foundry projects, build and develop with projects, and conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Account Owner**

Grants full access to manage projects and resources, and lets you conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Owner**

Grants full access to managed projects and resources and build and develop with projects. Highly privileged self-serve role designed for digital natives.

### Permissions for each built-in role

Use the following table to see the

<note>Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of 5000 to get more content.</note>
```

</details>

### 💬 Copilot

Znalazłem! To jest w dwóch miejscach:

### 1. Sekcja "Full access isolation" (Appendix):

\> **Full access isolation** — Grant your developer the **Reader** role on Foundry resource scope and **Azure AI User** on project scope

### 2. Sekcja "Sample enterprise RBAC mappings":

\> **Team members or developers** — `Azure AI User` on Foundry **project** scope and `Reader` on the Foundry **resource** scope

Oba cytaty z: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry

Konkretnie:
- Tabela "Sample enterprise RBAC mappings for projects" → wiersz "Team members or developers"
- Appendix → "Access Isolation Examples" → "Full access isolation"

Czyli `Reader` na Account **jest w dokumentacji** — ale nie jest wyjaśnione *dlaczego*. Z naszych testów wiemy: bez niego user nie widzi modeli i nie może tworzyć agentów (bo nie widzi deploymentów). To jest control plane read potrzebny do listowania modeli w UI.

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples:
In this article, you learn core role-based access control (RBAC) concepts for Microsoft Foundry, including scopes, built-in roles, and common enterprise assignment patterns.

Tip

RBAC roles apply when you authenticate using Microsoft Entra ID. If you use key-based authentication instead, the key grants full access without role restrictions. Microsoft recommends using Entra ID authentication for improved security and granular access control.

For more information about authentication and authorization in Microsoft Foundry, see [Authentication and Authorization](authentication-authorization-foundry).

## Minimum role assignments to get started

For new users to Azure and Microsoft Foundry, start with these minimum assignments so both your user principal and project managed identity can access Foundry features.

You can verify current assignments by using [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).

*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **user principal**.
*   Assign the **Azure AI User** role on your Foundry resource to your **project's managed identity**.

If the user who created the project can assign roles (for example, by having the Azure **Owner** role at subscription or resource group scope), both assignments are added automatically.

To assign these roles manually, use the following quick steps.

### Assign a role to your user principal

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry resource and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **User, group, or service principal**, select your user principal, and then select **Review + assign**.

### Assign a role to your project's managed identity

In the Azure portal, open your Foundry project and go to **Access control (IAM)**. Create a role assignment for **Azure AI User**, set **Members** to **Managed identity**, select your project's managed identity, and then select **Review + assign**.

## Terminology for role-based access control in Foundry

To understand role-based access control in Microsoft Foundry, consider two questions for your enterprise.

*   What permissions do I want my team to have when building in Microsoft Foundry?
*   At what scope do I want to assign permissions to my team?

To help answer these questions, here are descriptions of some terminology used throughout this article.

*   **Permissions**: Allowed or denied actions that an identity can perform on a resource, such as reading, writing, deleting, or managing both control plane and data plane operations.
*   **Scope**: The set of Azure resources to which a role assignment applies. Typical scopes include subscription, resource group, Foundry resource, or Foundry project.
*   **Role**: A named collection of permissions that defines which actions can be performed on Azure resources at a given scope.

An identity gets a _role_ with specific _permissions_ at a selected _scope_ based on your enterprise requirements.

In Microsoft Foundry, consider two scopes when completing role assignments.

*   **Foundry resource**: The top-level scope that defines the administrative, security, and monitoring boundary for a Microsoft Foundry environment.
*   **Foundry project**: A sub-scope within a Foundry resource used to organize work and enforce access control for Foundry APIs, tools, and developer workflows.

## Built-in roles

A **built-in role** in Foundry is a role created by Microsoft that covers common access scenarios that you can assign to your team members. Key built-in roles used across Azure include Owner, Contributor, and Reader. These roles aren't specific to Foundry resource permissions.

For Foundry resources, use additional built-in roles to follow least-privilege access principles. The following table lists key built-in roles for Foundry and links to the exact role definitions in [AI + Machine Learning built-in roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles/ai-machine-learning).

Role

Description

**Azure AI User**

Grants reader access to Foundry project, Foundry resource, and data actions for your Foundry project. If you can assign roles, this role is assigned to you automatically. Otherwise, your subscription Owner or a user with role assignment permissions grants it. Least privilege access role in Foundry.

**Azure AI Project Manager**

Lets you perform management actions on Foundry projects, build and develop with projects, and conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Account Owner**

Grants full access to manage projects and resources, and lets you conditionally assign the Azure AI User role to other user principals.

**Azure AI Owner**

Grants full access to managed projects and resources and build and develop with projects. Highly privileged self-serve role designed for digital natives.

### Permissions for each built-in role

Use the following table to see the permissions allowed for each built-in role in Microsoft Foundry.

Built-in role

Create Foundry projects

Create Foundry accounts

Build and develop in a project (data actions)

Complete role assignments

Reader access to projects and accounts

Manage models

Publish agents

To publish agents, you need the **Azure AI Project Manager** role (minimum) on the Foundry resource scope. For more information, see [Agent applications in Microsoft Foundry](../agents/how-to/agent-applications).

To learn more about conditional delegation used in the Azure AI Account Owner and Azure AI Project Manager role, see [Delegate Azure role assignment management to others with conditions](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/delegate-role-assignments-portal).

## Sample enterprise RBAC mappings for projects

Here's an example of how to implement role-based access control (RBAC) for an enterprise Foundry resource.

Persona

Role and Scope

Purpose

IT admin

Owner on subscription scope

The IT admin ensures the Foundry resource meets enterprise standards. Assign managers the **Azure AI Account Owner** role on the resource to let them create new Foundry accounts. Assign managers the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource to let them create projects within an account.

Managers

Azure AI Account Owner on Foundry resource scope

Managers manage the Foundry resource, deploy models, audit compute resources, audit connections, and create shared connections. They can't build in projects, but they can assign the **Azure AI User** role to themselves and others to start building.

Team lead or lead developer

Azure AI Project Manager on Foundry resource scope

Lead developers create projects for their team and start building in those projects. After you create a project, project owners invite other members and assign the **Azure AI User** role.

Team members or developers

Azure AI User on Foundry project scope and Reader on the Foundry resource scope

Developers build agents in a project with pre-deployed Foundry models and pre-built connections.

## Manage role assignments

To manage roles in Foundry, you must have permission to assign and remove roles in Azure. The Azure built-in **Owner** role includes that permission. You can assign roles through the Foundry portal (Admin page), Azure portal IAM, or Azure CLI. You can remove roles by using Azure portal IAM or Azure CLI.

In the Foundry portal, manage permissions by:

1.  Open the **Admin** page in [Foundry](https://ai.azure.com), then select **Operate** > **Admin**.
2.  Select your project name.
3.  Select **Add user** to manage project access. This action is available only if you have role-assignment permissions.
4.  Apply the same flow for Foundry resource-level access.

You can manage permissions in the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com) under **Access Control (IAM)** or by using Azure CLI.

For example, the following command assigns the Azure AI User role to `joe@contoso.com` for resource group `this-rg` in subscription `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`:

```
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" --assignee "joe@contoso.com" --scope /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/this-rg 
```

## Create custom roles for projects

If the built-in roles don't meet your enterprise requirements, create a custom role that allows for precise control over allowed actions and scopes. Here's an example subscription-level custom role definition:

```
{
  "properties": {
    "roleName": "My Enterprise Foundry User",
    "description": "Custom role for Foundry at my enterprise to only allow building Agents. Assign at subscription level.",
    "assignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<your-subscription-id>"],
    "permissions": [ { 
        "actions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/*/read", "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read", "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/listkeys/action","Microsoft.Resources/deployments/*"], 
        "notActions": [], 
        "dataActions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/AIServices/agents/*"], 
        "notDataActions": []     
    } ]
  }
}
```

For more information on creating a custom role, see the following articles.

*   [Azure portal](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-portal)
*   [Azure CLI](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-cli)
*   [Azure PowerShell](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-powershell)
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features). This article provides more details on specific permissions in Foundry across control and data plane which you can utilize when building custom roles.

## Notes and limitations

*   To view and purge deleted Foundry accounts, you must have the Contributor role assigned at the subscription scope.
*   Users with the Contributor role can deploy models in Foundry.
*   You need the Owner role on a resource's scope to create custom roles in the resource.
*   If you have permissions to role assign in Azure (for example, the Owner role assigned on the account scope) to your user principal, and you deploy a Foundry resource from the Azure portal or Foundry portal UI, then the Azure AI User role gets automatically assigned to your user principal. This assignment doesn't apply when deploying Foundry from SDK or CLI.
*   When you create a Foundry resource, the built-in role-based access control (RBAC) permissions give you access to the resource. To use resources created outside Foundry, ensure the resource has permissions that let you access it. Here are some examples:
    *   To use a new Azure Blob Storage account, add the Foundry account resource's managed identity to the Storage Blob Data Reader role on that storage account.
    *   To use a new Azure AI Search source, add Foundry to the Azure AI Search role assignments.
*   To fine-tune a model in Foundry, you need both data plane and control plane permissions. Deploying a fine-tuned model is a control plane permission. Therefore, the only built-in role with both data plane and control plane permissions is the **Azure AI Owner** role. Or, if you prefer, you can also assign the **Azure AI User** role for data plane permissions and the **Azure AI Account Owner** role for control plane permissions.

## Related content

*   [Create a project](../how-to/create-projects).
*   [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).
*   [Authentication and Authorization in Foundry](authentication-authorization-foundry).
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features).
*   [Hosted agent permissions reference](../agents/concepts/hosted-agent-permissions).

## Appendix

### Access Isolation Examples

Each organization may have different access isolation requirements depending on the user personas in their enterprise. Access isolation refers to which users in your enterprise are given what role assignments for either a separation of permissions using our built-in roles or a unified, highly permissive role. There are three access isolation options for Foundry that you can select for your organization depending on your access isolation requirements.

**No access isolation.** This means in your enterprise, you don't have any requirements separating permissions between a developer, project manager, or an admin. The permissions for these roles can be assigned across teams.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant all users in your enterprise the **Azure AI Owner** role on the resource scope

**Partial access isolation.** This means the project manager in your enterprise should be able to develop within projects as well as create projects. But your admins shouldn't be able to develop within Foundry, only create Foundry projects and accounts.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant your admin with **Azure AI Account Owner** on the resource scope
*   Grant your developer and project managers with **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource

**Full access isolation.** This means your admins, project managers, and developers have clear permissions assigned that don't overlap for their different functions within an enterprise.

Therefore you should...

*   Grant your admin the **Azure AI Account Owner** on resource scope
*   Grant your developer the **Reader** role on Foundry resource scope and **Azure AI User** on project scope
*   Grant your project manager the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on resource scope

### Use Microsoft Entra groups with Foundry

Microsoft Entra ID provides several ways to manage access to resources, applications, and tasks. By using Microsoft Entra groups, you can grant access and permissions to a group of users instead of to each individual user. Enterprise IT admins can create Microsoft Entra groups in the Azure portal to simplify the role assignment process for developers. When you create a Microsoft Entra group, you can minimize the number of role assignments required for new developers working on F

## [Task manager automatically opening](/en-us/answers/questions/5881974/task-manager-automatically-opening)

Whenever I wake up my Windows 11 PC from sleep, task manager automatically opens even if i end it’s task before putting PC to sleep.

Windows for home | Windows 11 | Sleep and Power on, off

![](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/media/logos/logo_windows.svg)

### ✅ `web_fetch`

**https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples**

<details>

{

<summary>80 lines</summary>

````
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples:
ns.

## Manage role assignments

To manage roles in Foundry, you must have permission to assign and remove roles in Azure. The Azure built-in **Owner** role includes that permission. You can assign roles through the Foundry portal (Admin page), Azure portal IAM, or Azure CLI. You can remove roles by using Azure portal IAM or Azure CLI.

In the Foundry portal, manage permissions by:

1.  Open the **Admin** page in [Foundry](https://ai.azure.com), then select **Operate** > **Admin**.
2.  Select your project name.
3.  Select **Add user** to manage project access. This action is available only if you have role-assignment permissions.
4.  Apply the same flow for Foundry resource-level access.

You can manage permissions in the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com) under **Access Control (IAM)** or by using Azure CLI.

For example, the following command assigns the Azure AI User role to `joe@contoso.com` for resource group `this-rg` in subscription `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`:

```
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" --assignee "joe@contoso.com" --scope /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/this-rg 
```

## Create custom roles for projects

If the built-in roles don't meet your enterprise requirements, create a custom role that allows for precise control over allowed actions and scopes. Here's an example subscription-level custom role definition:

```
{
  "properties": {
    "roleName": "My Enterprise Foundry User",
    "description": "Custom role for Foundry at my enterprise to only allow building Agents. Assign at subscription level.",
    "assignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<your-subscription-id>"],
    "permissions": [ { 
        "actions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/*/read", "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read", "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/listkeys/action","Microsoft.Resources/deployments/*"], 
        "notActions": [], 
        "dataActions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/AIServices/agents/*"], 
        "notDataActions": []     
    } ]
  }
}
```

For more information on creating a custom role, see the following articles.

*   [Azure portal](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-portal)
*   [Azure CLI](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-cli)
*   [Azure PowerShell](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-powershell)
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features). This article provides more details on specific permissions in Foundry across control and data plane which you can utilize when building custom roles.

## Notes and limitations

*   To view and purge deleted Foundry accounts, you must have the Contributor role assigned at the subscription scope.
*   Users with the Contributor role can deploy models in Foundry.
*   You need the Owner role on a resource's scope to create custom roles in the resource.
*   If you have permissions to role assign in Azure (for example, the Owner role assigned on the account scope) to your user principal, and you deploy a Foundry resource from the Azure portal or Foundry portal UI, then the Azure AI User role gets automatically assigned to your user principal. This assignment doesn't apply when deploying Foundry from SDK or CLI.
*   When you create a Foundry resource, the built-in role-based access control (RBAC) permissions give you access to the resource. To use resources created outside Foundry, ensure the resource has permissions that let you access it. Here are some examples:
    *   To use a new Azure Blob Storage account, add the Foundry account resource's managed identity to the Storage Blob Data Reader role on that storage account.
    *   To use a new Azure AI Search source, add Foundry to the Azure AI Search role assignments.
*   To fine-tune a model in Foundry, you need both data plane and control plane permissions. Deploying a fine-tuned model is a control plane permission. Therefore, the only built-in role with both data plane and control plane permissions is the **Azure AI Owner** role. Or, if you prefer, you can also assign the **Azure AI User** role for data plane permissions and the **Azure AI Account Owner** role for control plane permissions.

## Related content

*   [Create a project](../how-to/create-projects).
*   [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).
*   [Authentication and Authorization in Foundry](authentication-authorization-foundry).
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features).
*   [Hosted agent permissions reference](../agents/concepts/hosted-agent-permissions).

## Appendix

### Access Isolation Examples

Each organization may have different access isolation requirements depending on the user personas in their enterprise. Access isolation refers to which users in your enterprise are given what role assignments for either a separation of permissions using our built-in roles or a unified, highly permissive role. There are three access isolation options for Foundry that you can select for your organization depending on your access isolation requirements.

**No access isolation.** This means in your enterprise, you don't have any requirements separating permissions between a developer, project manager, or an admin. The permissions for these roles can be assigned across teams.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant all users in your enterprise the **Azure AI Owner** role on the resource scope

**Partial access isolation.** This means the project manager in your enterprise should be able to develop within projects as well as create projects. But your admins shouldn't be able to develop within Foundry, only create Foundry projects and accounts.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant your admin with **Azure AI Account Owner** on the resource scope
*   Grant your developer and project managers with **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource

**Full access isolation.** This means your admins, project managers, and developers have clear permissions assigned that don't overlap for their different functions within an enterprise.

Therefore you should...

*   Grant your admin the **Azure AI Account Owner** on resource scope
*   Grant your developer the **Reader** role on Foundry resource scope and **Azure AI User** on project scope
*   Grant your project manager the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on resource scope

### Use Microsoft Entra groups with Foundry

Microsoft Entra ID provides several ways to manage access to resources, applications, and tasks. By using Microsoft Entra groups, you can grant access and permissions to a group of users instead of to each individual user. Enterprise IT admins can create Microsoft Entra groups in the Azure portal to simplify the role assignment process for developers. When you create a Microsoft Entra group, you can minimize the number of role assignments required for new developers working on Foundry projects by assigning the group the required role assignment on the necessary resource.

Complete the following steps to use Microsoft Entra ID groups with Foundry:

1.  Create a **Security** group in **Groups** in the Azure portal.
2.  Add an owner and the user principals in your organization who need shared access.
3.  Open the target resource and go to **Access control (IAM)**.
4.  Assign the required role to **User, group, or service principal**, and select the new security group.
5.  Select **Review + assign** so the role assignment applies to all members of the group.

Common examples:

*   To build agents, run traces, and use core Foundry capabilities, assign **Azure AI User** to the Microsoft Entra group.
*   To use Tracing and Monitoring features, assign **Reader** on the connected Application Insights resource to the same group.

To learn more about Microsoft Entra ID groups, prerequisites, and limitations, refer to:

*   [Learn about groups, group membership, and access in Microsoft Entra](/en-us/entra/fundamentals/concept-learn-about-groups).
*   [How to manage groups in Microsoft Entra](/en-us/entra/fundamentals/how-to-manage-groups).
````

</details>

Use the following table to see the permissions allowed for each key Azure built-in roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader).

Built-in role

To publish agents, you need the **Azure AI Project Manager** role (minimum) on the Foundry resource scope. For more information, see [Agent applications in Microsoft Foundry](../agents/how-to/agent-applications).

[![Diagram of the built-in roles in Foundry.](../media/how-to/network/detailed-rbac-diagram.png)](../media/how-to/network/detailed-rbac-diagram.png#lightbox)

For more on built-in roles in Azure and Foundry, see [Azure built-in roles](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles). To learn more about conditional delegation used in the Azure AI Account Owner and Azure AI Project Manager role, see [Delegate Azure role assignment management to others with conditions](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/delegate-role-assignments-portal).

## Sample enterprise RBAC mappings for projects

Here's an example of how to implement role-based access control (RBAC) for an enterprise Foundry resource.

Persona

Role and Scope

Purpose

IT admin

Owner on subscription scope

The IT admin ensures the Foundry resource meets enterprise standards. Assign managers the **Azure AI Account Owner** role on the resource to let them create new Foundry accounts. Assign managers the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource to let them create projects within an account.

Managers

Azure AI Account Owner on Foundry resource scope

Managers manage the Foundry resource, deploy models, audit compute resources, audit connections, and create shared connections. They can't build in projects, but they can assign the **Azure AI User** role to themselves and others to start building.

Team lead or lead developer

Azure AI Project Manager on Foundry resource scope

Lead developers create projects for their team and start building in those projects. After you create a project, project owners invite other members and assign the **Azure AI User** role.

Team members or developers

Azure AI User on Foundry project scope and Reader on the Foundry resource scope

Developers build agents in a project with pre-deployed Foundry models and pre-built connections.

## Manage role assignments

To manage roles in Foundry, you must have permission to assign and remove roles in Azure. The Azure built-in **Owner** role includes that permission. You can assign roles through the Foundry portal (Admin page), Azure portal IAM, or Azure CLI. You can remove roles by using Azure portal IAM or Azure CLI.

In the Foundry portal, manage permissions by:

1.  Open the **Admin** page in [Foundry](https://ai.azure.com), then select **Operate** > **Admin**.
2.  Select your project name.
3.  Select **Add user** to manage project access. This action is available only if you have role-assignment permissions.
4.  Apply the same flow for Foundry resource-level access.

You can manage permissions in the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com) under **Access Control (IAM)** or by using Azure CLI.

For example, the following command assigns the Azure AI User role to `joe@contoso.com` for resource group `this-rg` in subscription `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`:

```
az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" --assignee "joe@contoso.com" --scope /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/this-rg 
```

## Create custom roles for projects

If the built-in roles don't meet your enterprise requirements, create a custom role that allows for precise control over allowed actions and scopes. Here's an example subscription-level custom role definition:

```
{
  "properties": {
    "roleName": "My Enterprise Foundry User",
    "description": "Custom role for Foundry at my enterprise to only allow building Agents. Assign at subscription level.",
    "assignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<your-subscription-id>"],
    "permissions": [ { 
        "actions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/*/read", "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read", "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/listkeys/action","Microsoft.Resources/deployments/*"], 
        "notActions": [], 
        "dataActions": ["Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/AIServices/agents/*"], 
        "notDataActions": []     
    } ]
  }
}
```

For more information on creating a custom role, see the following articles.

*   [Azure portal](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-portal)
*   [Azure CLI](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-cli)
*   [Azure PowerShell](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/custom-roles-powershell)
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features). This article provides more details on specific permissions in Foundry across control and data plane which you can utilize when building custom roles.

## Notes and limitations

*   To view and purge deleted Foundry accounts, you must have the Contributor role assigned at the subscription scope.
*   Users with the Contributor role can deploy models in Foundry.
*   You need the Owner role on a resource's scope to create custom roles in the resource.
*   If you have permissions to role assign in Azure (for example, the Owner role assigned on the account scope) to your user principal, and you deploy a Foundry resource from the Azure portal or Foundry portal UI, then the Azure AI User role gets automatically assigned to your user principal. This assignment doesn't apply when deploying Foundry from SDK or CLI.
*   When you create a Foundry resource, the built-in role-based access control (RBAC) permissions give you access to the resource. To use resources created outside Foundry, ensure the resource has permissions that let you access it. Here are some examples:
    *   To use a new Azure Blob Storage account, add the Foundry account resource's managed identity to the Storage Blob Data Reader role on that storage account.
    *   To use a new Azure AI Search source, add Foundry to the Azure AI Search role assignments.
*   To fine-tune a model in Foundry, you need both data plane and control plane permissions. Deploying a fine-tuned model is a control plane permission. Therefore, the only built-in role with both data plane and control plane permissions is the **Azure AI Owner** role. Or, if you prefer, you can also assign the **Azure AI User** role for data plane permissions and the **Azure AI Account Owner** role for control plane permissions.

## Related content

*   [Create a project](../how-to/create-projects).
*   [Check access for a user to a single Azure resource](/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/check-access?tabs=default).
*   [Authentication and Authorization in Foundry](authentication-authorization-foundry).
*   [Disable preview features in Microsoft Foundry](../how-to/disable-preview-features).
*   [Hosted agent permissions reference](../agents/concepts/hosted-agent-permissions).

## Appendix

### Access Isolation Examples

Each organization may have different access isolation requirements depending on the user personas in their enterprise. Access isolation refers to which users in your enterprise are given what role assignments for either a separation of permissions using our built-in roles or a unified, highly permissive role. There are three access isolation options for Foundry that you can select for your organization depending on your access isolation requirements.

**No access isolation.** This means in your enterprise, you don't have any requirements separating permissions between a developer, project manager, or an admin. The permissions for these roles can be assigned across teams.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant all users in your enterprise the **Azure AI Owner** role on the resource scope

**Partial access isolation.** This means the project manager in your enterprise should be able to develop within projects as well as create projects. But your admins shouldn't be able to develop within Foundry, only create Foundry projects and accounts.

Therefore, you should...

*   Grant your admin with **Azure AI Account Owner** on the resource scope
*   Grant your developer and project managers with **Azure AI Project Manager** role on the resource

**Full access isolation.** This means your admins, project managers, and developers have clear permissions assigned that don't overlap for their different functions within an enterprise.

Therefore you should...

*   Grant your admin the **Azure AI Account Owner** on resource scope
*   Grant your developer the **Reader** role on Foundry resource scope and **Azure AI User** on project scope
*   Grant your project manager the **Azure AI Project Manager** role on resource scope

### Use Microsoft Entra groups with Foundry

Microsoft Entra ID provides several ways to manage access to resources, applications, and tasks. By using Microsoft Entra groups, you can grant access and permissions to a group of users instead of to each individual user. Enterprise IT admins can create Microsoft Entra groups in the Azure portal to simplify the role assignment process for developers. When you create a Microsoft Entra group, you can minimize the number of role assignments required for new developers working on F

<sub>⏱️ 1456m 54s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Przeszukałem dokumentację. Nie znalazłem żadnych publicznych zgłoszeń (issues) ani feedbacku o tym, że widoczność nazw projektów jest problemem. Oto co **wynika z dokumentacji**:

---

### Co mówi Microsoft:

Dokumentacja definiuje **3 poziomy izolacji** i **żaden z nich nie ukrywa nazw projektów**:

| Poziom | Konfiguracja | Nazwy widoczne? |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **No isolation** | AI Owner na Account | Tak + pełny dostęp |
| **Partial isolation** | AI Project Manager na Account | Tak + tworzenie projektów |
| **Full access isolation** | Reader na Account + AI User na Project | **Tak, ale BEZ data access** |

### Dlaczego tak jest?

`Reader` na Account to **standardowa rola Azure** — daje `*/read` na zasobie i jego child resources (projekty). Nie da się dać `Reader` na Account BEZ listowania child resources — tak działa ARM.

### Jeśli klient wymaga ukrycia nazw:

Dokumentacja **nie oferuje** takiej opcji w ramach jednego Account. Z naszych testów:
- **Bez Reader** → nie widać modeli, nie można tworzyć agentów
- **Z Reader** → widać nazwy, ale brak data access ✅

**Jedyne rozwiązanie na pełne ukrycie** → **osobne Foundry Account** per zespół/projekt.

Nie znalazłem żadnych issue na GitHubie ani na MS Q&A z prośbą o taką funkcjonalność. To sugeruje, że MS traktuje widoczność nazw jako **akceptowalną** w modelu "Full access isolation" — bo reader to control plane read, a dane są chronione na data plane.

---

<sub>⏱️ 1460m 3s</sub>

### 👤 User

oki robimy taki własnie przykład na workshop dodaj to prosze

<sub>⏱️ 1460m 11s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md.**

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md (lines 1-50)

<details>
<summary>50 lines</summary>

```
1. # 🏗️ Workshop: Microsoft Foundry dla klientów enterprise
2. ## Wdrożenie AI Foundry z BYO VNet — krok po kroku
3. 
4. ---
5. 
6. ## 📋 O warsztacie
7. 
8. | | |
9. |---|---|
10. | **Czas trwania** | ~2-3 godziny |
11. | **Poziom** | Intermediate |
12. | **Wymagania** | Azure CLI, subskrypcja Azure, uprawnienia Owner |
13. | **Rezultat** | Działające środowisko AI Foundry w prywatnej sieci VNet z pełną izolacją |
14. 
15. ### Czego się nauczysz
16. 
17. 1. Jak wdrożyć Microsoft AI Foundry w prywatnej sieci klienta (BYO VNet)
18. 2. Jak skonfigurować Private Endpoints dla pełnej izolacji sieciowej
19. 3. Jak przetestować, że dane nigdy nie opuszczają sieci prywatnej
20. 4. Jak uzyskać dostęp do portalu ai.azure.com z wnętrza VNet
21. 5. Jak wystawić modele przez API Management w sieci wewnętrznej
22. 6. Jakie role RBAC są wymagane i dlaczego
23. 7. Jak zbudować izolację multi-tenant per-projekt
24. 
25. ### 💰 Szacunkowe koszty warsztatu
26. 
27. | Zasób | Koszt/h (est.) | Uwagi |
28. |-------|---------------|-------|
29. | AI Services (gpt-4.1 GlobalStandard) | Pay-per-token | ~$0.01 per 1K tokens |
30. | Storage Account | ~$0.02/GB/mies. | Minimalny ruch na warsztacie |
31. | AI Search (Basic) | ~$1/h | Wymagany tier Basic minimum |
32. | CosmosDB (Serverless) | Pay-per-RU | Minimalny ruch na warsztacie |
33. | Bastion (Basic) | ~$0.19/h | Dostęp do portalu |
34. | VPN Gateway (VpnGw1AZ) | ~$0.19/h | Opcjonalny, zdalny dostęp |
35. | Jumpbox VM (B2s) | ~$0.05/h | Windows Server |
36. | APIM Developer (opcja) | ~$0.07/h | Faza 8 |
37. 
38. > 💡 **Szacowany koszt: ~$2-4/h** za całe środowisko. Po warsztacie **natychmiast uruchom Cleanup!**
39. 
40. ### 🕐 Timeline
41. 
42. | Czas | Faza | Opis |
43. |------|------|------|
44. | 0:00 | Przygotowanie | Konfiguracja, `az login` |
45. | 0:10 | Faza 1 | Pre-flight checks |
46. | 0:15 | Faza 2 | Deploy infrastruktury (~30 min, można omawiać teorię) |
47. | 0:45 | Faza 3-6 | Testy walidacyjne (sieć, PE, agent, RBAC) |
48. | 1:00 | Faza 7 | **⭐ Test portalu przez Bastion** |
49. | 1:15 | Faza 7 | Tworzenie agenta, test izolacji |
50. | 1:30 | Faza 8 | APIM deploy (opcja, 45 min) |
```

100. 
101. ### Wymagania sieciowe BYO VNet
102. 
103. #### Wspierane zakresy adresów IP
104. 
105. | Klasa | Zakres | Status |
106. |-------|--------|--------|
107. | **Class A** | `10.0.0.0/8` | ✅ GA (wybrane regiony) |
108. | **Class B** | `172.16.0.0/12` | ✅ GA (wszystkie regiony) |
109. | **Class C** | `192.168.0.0/16` | ✅ GA (wszystkie regiony) |
110. 
111. > ⚠️ **Zarezerwowane zakresy — NIE UŻYWAJ:**
112. > - `169.254.0.0/16` — Link-local (Azure internal)
113. > - `172.30.0.0/16` — Azure internal
114. > - `172.31.0.0/16` — Azure internal
115. > - `100.64.0.0/10` — Shared address space
116. > - `168.63.129.16/32` — Azure DNS / wireserver
117. 
118. #### Wymagania subnetów
119. 
120. | Subnet | Min. rozmiar | Delegacja | Uwagi |
121. |--------|-------------|-----------|-------|
122. | **Agent** (snet-agent) | /26 (min), /24 (rekomendowany) | `Microsoft.App/environments` | **Wyłączny** — żadne inne zasoby! |
123. | **PE** (snet-pe) | /27 (min), /24 (rekomendowany) | **Brak** (żadna delegacja!) | Private Endpoints |
124. | **AzureBastionSubnet** | /26 | - | Nazwa MUSI być dokładnie `AzureBastionSubnet` |
125. | **GatewaySubnet** | /27 | - | Nazwa MUSI być dokładnie `GatewaySubnet` |
126. | **snet-apim** | /27 | - | Opcjonalnie, dla API Management |
127. 
128. > ⚠️ **Krytyczne:**
129. > - Subnet agentów MUSI mieć delegację do `Microsoft.App/environments`
130. > - Subnet PE **NIE MOŻE** mieć żadnej delegacji
131. > - **Wszystkie zasoby MUSZĄ być w tym samym regionie** — Foundry nie wspiera cross-region
132. 
133. #### Wymagane Private DNS Zones
134. 
135. Do poprawnego rozwiązywania nazw wewnątrz VNet potrzeba **6 stref DNS**:
136. 
137. | Strefa DNS | Dla jakiego zasobu |
138. |------------|-------------------|
139. | `privatelink.cognitiveservices.azure.com` | AI Services (control plane) |
140. | `privatelink.openai.azure.com` | OpenAI endpoints |
141. | `privatelink.services.ai.azure.com` | Agent Service endpoints |
142. | `privatelink.blob.core.windows.net` | Storage Account |
143. | `privatelink.documents.azure.com` | CosmosDB |
144. | `privatelink.search.windows.net` | AI Search |
145. 
146. > 📎 Każda strefa musi być **zlinkowana z VNetem** aby DNS działał.
147. 
148. #### Model RBAC — kto potrzebuje jakich ról?
149. 
150. Microsoft Foundry używa trzech typów tożsamości, każdy wymaga innych ról:
151. 
152. ##### 1. Managed Identity konta (Account MI)
153. 
154. Tożsamość systemowa konta AI Services — używana przez platformę do zarządzania zasobami.
155. 
156. | Rola | Scope | Cel |
157. |------|-------|-----|
158. | **Storage Blob Data Owner** | Storage Account | Zarządzanie plikami agentów |
159. | **Storage Queue Data Contributor** | Storage Account | Kolejki komunikatów |
160. | **Search Index Data Contributor** | AI Search | Indeksowanie danych |
161. 
162. ##### 2. Managed Identity projektu (Project MI)
163. 
164. Tożsamość systemowa projektu — używana przez Agent Runtime do operacji data plane.
165. 
166. | Rola | Scope | Cel |
167. |------|-------|-----|
168. | **Storage Blob Data Owner** | Storage Account | Pliki agentów (blob) |
169. | **Storage Queue Data Contributor** | Storage Account | Kolejki komunikatów |
170. | **Search Index Data Contributor** | AI Search | Vector store (RAG) |
171. | **Search Service Contributor** | AI Search | Zarządzanie indeksami |
172. | **Cosmos DB Operator** | CosmosDB | Control plane |
173. | **Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor** | CosmosDB (data plane!) | Odczyt/zapis wątków |
174. 
175. > ⚠️ **Pułapka: CosmosDB data plane RBAC**
176. >
177. > Gdy `disableLocalAuth: true` (co jest wymagane dla bezpieczeństwa), samo nadanie "Cosmos DB Operator" **NIE WYSTARCZY**.
178. > Trzeba dodać **Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor** przez osobne API:
179. > ```
180. > az cosmosdb sql role assignment create \
181. >   --role-definition-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002 \
182. >   --principal-id <MI-principal-id> \
183. >   --scope <cosmos-account-id>
184. > ```
185. > Jest to częsty błąd — w portalu widać "Request blocked by Auth cosmos".
186. 
187. ##### 3. Użytkownicy portalu (human users)
188. 
189. > 🔬 **PRZETESTOWANE! Wyniki naszych testów izolacji per-projekt:**
190. 
191. Dla użytkowników portalu ai.azure.com wystarczą **TYLKO role Foundry** nadane na scope **projektu**:
192. 
193. | Rola | Scope | Cel | Wymagana? |
194. |------|-------|-----|-----------|
195. | **Azure AI User** | **Project** | Odczyt, przeglądanie projektu | ✅ TAK |
196. | **Azure AI Developer** | **Project** | Tworzenie agentów, praca z modelem | ✅ TAK (dla deweloperów) |
197. 
198. **NIE potrzeba** (portal używa MI projektu, nie tożsamości usera):
199. 
200. | Rola | Potrzebna? | Dlaczego NIE |
````

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 1460m 11s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md.**

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md (lines 200-350)

<details>
<summary>151 lines</summary>

````
200. | Rola | Potrzebna? | Dlaczego NIE |
201. |------|-----------|--------------|
202. | Storage Blob Data Contributor | ❌ | Portal → MI projektu → Storage |
203. | Search Index Data Contributor | ❌ | Portal → MI projektu → Search |
204. | Cosmos DB Operator | ❌ | Portal → MI projektu → CosmosDB |
205. | Cognitive Services OpenAI User | ❌ | Azure AI Developer wystarczy |
206. 
207. ##### Izolacja per-projekt — jak to działa
208. 
209. ```
210. az role assignment create --role "Azure AI User" \
211.   --assignee "userA@firma.com" \
212.   --scope ".../accounts/{account}/projects/{projekt-A}"
213. 
214. az role assignment create --role "Azure AI Developer" \
215.   --assignee "userA@firma.com" \
216.   --scope ".../accounts/{account}/projects/{projekt-A}"
217. ```
218. 
219. > ✅ **Przetestowano:** UserA widzi TYLKO projekt-A, nie widzi innych projektów
220. > ✅ **Przetestowano:** UserA może tworzyć agentów i rozmawiać z nimi
221. > ✅ **Przetestowano:** Bez ról na CosmosDB/Storage/Search — wszystko działa
222. >
223. > ⚠️ **UWAGA:** NIE nadawaj `Azure AI User` na scope **Account** — to daje dostęp do WSZYSTKICH projektów!
224. > Portal wyświetla mylący komunikat sugerujący Account scope — **ignoruj go**, Project scope wystarczy.
225. 
226. ##### Przykład: 2 zespoły — izolacja data plane (rekomendowane)
227. 
228. ```
229. Foundry Account: ai-foundry-enterprise
230. │
231. ├── projekt-zespol-A:
232. │   ├── UserA1 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-A)
233. │   └── UserA2 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-A)
234. │
235. └── projekt-zespol-B:
236.     ├── UserB1 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-B)
237.     └── UserB2 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-B)
238. 
239. Wszyscy: Reader (scope: Account) ← potrzebne do widoczności modeli
240. 
241. Wynik:
242. • Zespół A widzi nazwy obu projektów, ale NIE MA dostępu do danych zespołu B ✅
243. • Agent Service, Agents, pliki — izolacja per-projekt ✅
244. • Dane w CosmosDB/Storage/Search oddzielone przez MI projektu ✅
245. ```
246. 
247. > 📘 **Poziomy izolacji — porównanie:**
248. >
249. > | Metoda | Widzi nazwy projektów? | Data plane izolacja? | Kiedy stosować |
250. > |--------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
251. > | **Reader + AI User** (powyżej) | ⚠️ Tak | ✅ Pełna | Większość przypadków enterprise |
252. > | **Osobne Foundry Accounts** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Regulacje wymagające ukrycia nazw |
253. > | **Osobne Resource Groups** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Osobne zarządzanie per-zespół |
254. >
255. > Jeśli regulacje wymagają **pełnego ukrycia** istnienia innych projektów → użyj **osobnych Accounts**
256. > w osobnych Resource Groups. Każdy Account z własnym deployment modeli.
257. 
258. ##### Autentykacja — Entra ID vs API keys
259. 
260. | Funkcja | API key | Entra ID |
261. |---------|---------|----------|
262. | Agent Service | ❌ NIE działa | ✅ Wymagany |
263. | Evaluations | ❌ | ✅ Wymagany |
264. | Model inference | ✅ | ✅ |
265. | Per-user auditing | ❌ | ✅ |
266. | Managed Identity | ❌ | ✅ |
267. | Least privilege RBAC | ❌ (all-or-nothing) | ✅ |
268. 
269. > ⚠️ **Agent Service wymaga Entra ID** — API keys nie działają z agentami!
270. > W BYO VNet z `disableLocalAuth: true` API keys są w ogóle wyłączone.
271. >
272. > Token scope: `https://ai.azure.com/.default`
273. 
274. > 📎 [Autentykacja i autoryzacja w Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/authentication-authorization-foundry)
275. > 📎 [RBAC dla Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry)
276. > 📎 [Built-in roles](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles/ai-machine-learning)
277. 
278. ### Architektura docelowa warsztatu
279. 
280. ```
281. ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
282. │  Twoja sieć VNet (192.168.0.0/16)                                │
283. │                                                                  │
284. │  ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
285. │  │ snet-agent   │    │ snet-pe (Private Endpoints)             │  │
286. │  │ /24          │    │ /24                                     │  │
287. │  │              │    │ PE → AI Foundry (CognitiveServices)     │  │
288. │  │ Delegacja:   │    │ PE → Storage Account (blob)             │  │
289. │  │ Microsoft.   │    │ PE → CosmosDB (documents)               │  │
290. │  │ App/envs     │    │ PE → AI Search                          │  │
291. │  │              │    │                                         │  │
292. │  │ Agent        │    │ Cały ruch AI zostaje w Twojej sieci     │  │
293. │  │ Runtime      │    │                                         │  │
294. │  └─────────────┘    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
295. │                                                                  │
296. │  ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐        │
297. │  │ Bastion     │    │ Jumpbox VM   │    │ VPN Gateway   │        │
298. │  │ /26         │    │ Windows      │    │ P2S           │        │
299. │  │ Bezpieczny  │    │ Dostęp do    │    │ Zdalny dostęp │        │
300. │  │ RDP/SSH     │    │ ai.azure.com │    │ dla zespołu   │        │
301. │  └─────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └───────────────┘        │
302. │                                                                  │
303. │  Private DNS Zones (6 stref) → rozwiązują nazwy na prywatne IP  │
304. └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
305. ```
306. 
307. ---
308. 
309. ## 🔧 Przygotowanie (10 min)
310. 
311. ### Krok 1: Sklonuj repozytorium
312. 
313. ```powershell
314. git clone <repo-url>
315. cd foundry-vnet-test
316. ```
317. 
318. ### Krok 2: Zaloguj się do Azure
319. 
320. ```powershell
321. az login
322. az account set --subscription "TWOJA-SUBSKRYPCJA-ID"
323. az account show --query "{name:name, id:id}" -o table
324. ```
325. 
326. ### Krok 3: Skonfiguruj parametry
327. 
328. Otwórz `config/test-config.json` i dostosuj:
329. 
330. ```json
331. {
332.   "subscription_id": "TWOJA-SUBSKRYPCJA-ID",
333.   "resource_group": "rg-foundry-byovnet",
334.   "location": "swedencentral"
335. }
336. ```
337. 
338. > 💡 **Wspierane regiony**: Sweden Central, East US, East US 2, West Europe, France Central, Australia East
339. 
340. ### Krok 4: Ustaw hasło dla maszyny wirtualnej
341. 
342. ```powershell
343. $env:VM_ADMIN_PASSWORD = "WybierzSilneHaslo123!"
344. ```
345. 
346. > ⚠️ Hasło musi spełniać wymagania Azure: min. 12 znaków, wielkie i małe litery, cyfra, znak specjalny.
347. 
348. ---
349.

<sub>⏱️ 1460m 15s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md.**

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md (lines 350-520)

<details>
<summary>171 lines</summary>

````
350. ## ✅ Faza 1: Pre-flight (2 min)
351. 
352. Sprawdzamy czy Twoje środowisko jest gotowe do wdrożenia.
353. 
354. ```powershell
355. .\scripts\00-preflight.ps1
356. ```
357. 
358. ### Co sprawdza pre-flight?
359. 
360. > 📘 **Dlaczego pre-flight?** Azure wymaga rejestracji "providerów zasobów" w subskrypcji zanim można tworzyć zasoby.
361. > Np. `Microsoft.CognitiveServices` musi być zarejestrowany aby stworzyć konto AI Foundry.
362. > Pre-flight sprawdza 10 wymaganych providerów i powie które trzeba zarejestrować.
363. 
364. | Test | Opis |
365. |------|------|
366. | Azure CLI | Czy jest zainstalowane i zalogowane |
367. | Subskrypcja | Czy zgadza się z konfiguracją |
368. | Providery | Czy 10 wymaganych providerów jest zarejestrowanych |
369. | Region | Czy Foundry jest dostępny w wybranym regionie |
370. | Uprawnienia | Czy masz prawa do tworzenia zasobów |
371. | Bicep | Czy szablon się kompiluje |
372. 
373. ### Oczekiwany wynik
374. 
375. ```
376. ✅ 15/15 Pre-flight checks PASSED
377. ```
378. 
379. > ❌ Jeśli coś nie przejdzie — skrypt powie co naprawić (np. `az provider register --namespace Microsoft.CognitiveServices`)
380. 
381. ---
382. 
383. ## 🚀 Faza 2: Wdrożenie infrastruktury (25-35 min)
384. 
385. Teraz deployujemy wszystkie zasoby Azure jednym poleceniem.
386. 
387. ```powershell
388. .\scripts\01-deploy-foundry.ps1
389. ```
390. 
391. ### Co się tworzy?
392. 
393. > 📘 **Jak to działa pod spodem?**
394. > Deployment używa **Bicep** (deklaratywny język IaC od Microsoft). ARM (Azure Resource Manager)
395. > analizuje szablon, buduje graf zależności i tworzy zasoby równolegle tam gdzie to możliwe.
396. > Jedyny zasób który wymaga sekwencyjnego tworzenia to: Account → Model → Project → Connections → RBAC → Capability Host.
397. >
398. > 📘 **Co to jest networkInjections?**
399. > Parametr `networkInjections` na koncie AI Services mówi Azure: "wstrzyknij agent runtime do MOJEGO subnetu".
400. > To jest kluczowa różnica między BYO VNet a Managed VNet — ruch agenta nigdy nie opuszcza Twojej sieci.
401. 
402. | Zasób | Cel | Czas |
403. |-------|-----|------|
404. | VNet + 5 subnetów | Sieć prywatna z izolacją | ~1 min |
405. | AI Services Account | Konto Foundry z networkInjections | ~3 min |
406. | Storage Account | Pliki agentów (blob) | ~1 min |
407. | AI Search | Vector store dla agentów | ~2 min |
408. | CosmosDB | Thread storage (konwersacje) | ~3 min |
409. | 4× Private Endpoints | Izolacja sieciowa | ~2 min |
410. | 6× Private DNS Zones | Rozwiązywanie nazw wewnątrz VNet | ~1 min |
411. | Model gpt-4.1 | Deployment modelu GlobalStandard | ~1 min |
412. | Project + Connections | Projekt z 3 połączeniami | ~2 min |
413. | Capability Host | Agent runtime | ~2 min |
414. | 11× RBAC Assignments | Tożsamości zarządzane (control + data plane) | ~1 min |
415. | Bastion + Jumpbox VM | Dostęp do portalu | ~5 min |
416. | VPN Gateway P2S | Zdalny dostęp | ~25 min |
417. 
418. > ⏱️ **Łączny czas: ~25-35 min** (VPN Gateway jest najdłuższy)
419. 
420. > 💡 Skrypt ma wbudowany retry logic — jeśli konto AI potrzebuje więcej czasu na provisioning (networkInjections), automatycznie poczeka na stan `Succeeded` i ponowi próbę.
421. 
422. > 📘 **Dlaczego Storage, CosmosDB i AI Search?**
423. > Agent Service potrzebuje tych trzech usług:
424. > - **Storage Account** — przechowuje pliki uploadowane do agenta (blob) i kolejki komunikatów
425. > - **CosmosDB** — przechowuje wątki konwersacji (thread storage) i stany agentów
426. > - **AI Search** — vector store dla RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), file search
427. >
428. > W trybie BYO VNet wszystkie trzy są w Twojej sieci za Private Endpoints.
429. 
430. ### Oczekiwany wynik
431. 
432. ```
433. ✅ Deployment SUCCEEDED
434. ```
435. 
436. ---
437. 
438. ## 🔍 Faza 3: Walidacja sieci (3 min)
439. 
440. Sprawdzamy czy sieć jest poprawnie skonfigurowana.
441. 
442. > 📎 **Dokumentacja:** [BYO VNet — wymagania sieciowe](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks)
443. 
444. ```powershell
445. .\scripts\04-test-network.ps1
446. ```
447. 
448. ### Na co patrzymy?
449. 
450. - ✅ VNet z prawidłową przestrzenią adresową
451. - ✅ Subnet agentów z delegacją `Microsoft.App/environments`
452. - ✅ Subnet PE **bez** delegacji
453. - ✅ AzureBastionSubnet z prawidłowym CIDR
454. - ✅ GatewaySubnet z prawidłowym CIDR
455. - ✅ 6 Private DNS Zones zlinkowanych z VNetem
456. - ✅ Wszystkie zasoby w tym samym regionie
457. 
458. ### Oczekiwany wynik
459. 
460. ```
461. --- Summary: 14 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN ---
462. ```
463. 
464. > ℹ️ 1 WARN o DNS zone `azure-api.net` jest OK — to zona APIM, którą dodamy później.
465. 
466. ---
467. 
468. ## 🔒 Faza 4: Walidacja Private Endpoints (3 min)
469. 
470. Sprawdzamy czy wszystkie Private Endpoints działają i public access jest wyłączony.
471. 
472. > 📘 **Co to jest Private Endpoint?**
473. > Private Endpoint tworzy prywatny interfejs sieciowy (NIC) w Twoim VNecie, który "mapuje" publiczny
474. > endpoint usługi Azure na prywatny adres IP. Np. zamiast łączyć się z `mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net`
475. > przez publiczny Internet (np. 52.239.x.x), ruch idzie na prywatny IP (np. 192.168.1.4) wewnątrz Twojej sieci.
476. >
477. > W połączeniu z **Private DNS Zones**, aplikacje nawet nie wiedzą że używają prywatnego adresu —
478. > DNS automatycznie rozwiązuje nazwę na prywatny IP.
479. 
480. ```powershell
481. .\scripts\05-test-pe.ps1
482. ```
483. 
484. ### Na co patrzymy?
485. 
486. - ✅ 4 Private Endpoints ze statusem **Approved**
487. - ✅ Każdy PE ma prywatny adres IP z zakresu snet-pe
488. - ✅ **Public access: Disabled** na wszystkich zasobach
489. - ✅ **SharedKey disabled** na Storage (tylko AAD)
490. - ✅ **Local auth disabled** na CosmosDB (tylko AAD)
491. - ✅ DNS A-records rozwiązują się na prywatne IP
492. 
493. ### Oczekiwany wynik
494. 
495. ```
496. --- Summary: 22 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN ---
497. ```
498. 
499. > 🔐 To jest kluczowy test — potwierdza, że żaden ruch nie wychodzi poza Twoją sieć.
500. 
501. ---
502.

508. > [Network injection concepts](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/concepts/networking)
509. 
510. ```powershell
511. .\scripts\06-test-agent.ps1
512. ```
513.

560. ## 🖥️ Faza 7: Test portalu przez Bastion (10 min)
561. 
562. To jest kluczowy moment warsztatu — sprawdzamy dostęp do portalu AI Foundry z wnętrza sieci.
563. 
564. > 📘 **Jak działa portal ai.azure.com z BYO VNet?**
565. >
566. > Portal AI Foundry to aplikacja webowa hostowana przez Microsoft na publicznym Internecie.
567. > **UI się załaduje** — ale wszystkie **API calls** (tworzenie agentów, odczyt zasobów, chat)
568. > idą bezpośrednio do Twoich endpointów Azure, które są za Private Endpoints.
569. >
570. > Dlatego:
571. > - **Z Internetu**: UI się ładuje, ale API zwraca 403/404 → nie da się pracować
572. > - **Z wnętrza VNet**: UI + API działają → pełny dostęp do agentów
573. >
574. > 📘 **Opcje dostępu do portalu:**
575. >
576. > | Metoda | Opis | Kiedy używać |
577. > |--------|------|-------------|
578. > | **Azure Bastion** | RDP/SSH przez przeglądarkę, bez publicznego IP | Testy, szybki dostęp, workshop |
579. > | **VPN Point-to-Site** | VPN klient na laptopie, Azure AD auth | Zespół deweloperski, codzienne użycie |
580. > | **ExpressRoute** | Dedykowane łącze do Azure | Produkcja, duże firmy |
581. > | **Azure Virtual Desktop** | Wirtualne desktopy w chmurze | Wielu użytkowników, thin clients |
582. >
583. > 📎 [Portal access z prywatnej sieci](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/how-to/managed-virtual-network#access-the-portal)
584. 
585. ### Krok 7.1: Najpierw przetestuj z Internetu (powinno NIE działać)
586. 
587. Otwórz przeglądarkę na swoim komputerze:
588. 1. Wejdź na **https://ai.azure.com**
589. 2. Zaloguj się
590. 3. Wybierz projekt → **Agents**
591. 
592. **Oczekiwany wynik:** ❌ Błąd dostępu / "nie można załadować"
593. 
594. > ✅ To potwierdza, że portal jest niedostępny z Internetu!
595. 
596. ### Krok 7.2: Połącz się przez Bastion
597. 
598. 1. Otwórz **Azure Portal** → Resource Group `rg-foundry-byovnet`
599. 2. Kliknij na **vm-jumpbox** → **Connect** → **Connect via Bastion**
600. 3. Zaloguj się:
601.    - Username: `azureadmin`
602.    - Password: hasło które ustawiłeś w `$env:VM_ADMIN_PASSWORD`
603. 4. Otworzy się sesja RDP w przeglądarce
604. 
605. ### Krok 7.3: Otwórz portal AI Foundry na VM
606. 
607. Na Jumpbox VM:
608. 1. Otwórz **Microsoft Edge**
609. 2. Wejdź na **https://ai.azure.com**
610. 3. Zaloguj się swoim kontem Azure
611. 4. Wybierz projekt `project-agent-test`
612. 5. Przejdź do **Agents**
613. 
614. **Oczekiwany wynik:** ✅ Portal ładuje się, widzisz projekt, możesz tworzyć agentów!
615. 
616. ### Krok 7.4: Stwórz testowego agenta
617. 
618. 1. Kliknij **+ New Agent**
619. 2. Wybierz model: `gpt-4.1`
620. 3. Nazwa: `test-agent-workshop`
621. 4. Instructions: `Jesteś pomocnym asystentem. Odpowiadaj po polsku.`
622. 5. Kliknij **Create**
623. 6. W panelu czatu napisz: `Cześć, czy działasz?`
624. 
625. **Oczekiwany wynik:** ✅ Agent odpowiada — cały ruch przechodzi przez Private Endpoints!
626. 
627. > 🎉 **Gratulacje!** Masz działającego agenta AI w pełni izolowanym środowisku sieciowym.
628. 
629. ### Krok 7.5: Opcjonalnie — test z CLI na VM
630. 
631. Na Jumpbox VM otwórz PowerShell:
632. 
633. ```powershell
634. # Zaloguj się
635. az login
636. 
637. # Pobierz token
638. $token = az account get-access-token --resource "https://cognitiveservices.azure.com" --query accessToken -o tsv
639. 
640. # Test OpenAI (powinien zwrócić odpowiedź)
641. curl -X POST "https://ai-foundry-byovnet-UNIQUEID.cognitiveservices.azure.com/openai/deployments/gpt-4.1/chat/completions?api-version=2024-12-01-preview" `
642.   -H "Authorization: Bearer $token" `
643.   -H "Content-Type: application/json" `
644.   -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Powiedz cześć"}],"max_tokens":50}'
645. 
646. # Test Agent API (powinien zwrócić listę agentów)
647. curl "https://ai-foundry-byovnet-UNIQUEID.services.ai.azure.com/agents?api-version=2025-05-01-preview" `
648.   -H "Authorization: Bearer $token"
649. ```
650. 
651. **Oczekiwany wynik:** ✅ Odpowiedzi zwracane poprawnie — z wnętrza VNet API działa!
652. 
653. ---
654. 
655. ## 🌐 Faza 8: APIM — API Gateway w sieci prywatnej (45 min)
656. 
657. > ℹ️ Ta faza jest opcjonalna — dodaje API Management jako bramkę do modeli AI.
658. 
659. > 📘 **Po co APIM?** API Management pozwala wystawiać modele AI jako standardowe API (OpenAPI/REST)
660. > z dodatkową warstwą kontroli: rate limiting, autentykacja, monitoring, transformacje.
661. > W trybie **Internal VNet** APIM jest wstrzykiwany bezpośrednio do Twojego VNetu —
662. > endpoint jest dostępny TYLKO z wnętrza sieci.
663. >
664. > 📎 **Dokumentacja:** [APIM Internal VNet](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-internal-vnet) |
665. > [APIM + Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-authenticate-authorize-azure-openai)
666. 
667. ### Krok 8.1: Deploy APIM
668. 
669. ```powershell
670. .\scripts\02-deploy-apim.ps1
671. ```
672. 
673. > ⏱️ APIM Developer tier: **30-45 minut**. To dobry moment na przerwę ☕
674. 
675. ### Krok 8.2: Import API do APIM
676. 
677. ```powershell
678. .\scripts\03-import-openapi-mcp.ps1
679. ```
680. 
681. ### Krok 8.3: Test APIM
682. 
683. ```powershell
684. .\scripts\07-test-apim.ps1
685. ```

721. ## 🧹 Cleanup (5 min)
722. 
723. Po zakończeniu warsztatu — usuń zasoby żeby nie generować kosztów.
724. 
725. ```powershell
726. # Krok 1: Usuń Capability Host (wymagane przed usunięciem konta AI)
727. $sub = (Get-Content config/test-config.json | ConvertFrom-Json).subscription_id
728. $rg = (Get-Content config/test-config.json | ConvertFrom-Json).resource_group
729. $aiName = az cognitiveservices account list --resource-group $rg --query "[0].name" -o tsv
730. 
731. az rest --method DELETE `
732.   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$sub/resourceGroups/$rg/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/$aiName/projects/project-agent-test/capabilityHosts/default?api-version=2025-04-01-preview"
733. 
734. # Krok 2: Poczekaj 2 minuty
735. Start-Sleep -Seconds 120
736. 
737. # Krok 3: Usuń resource group (wszystkie zasoby)
738. az group delete --name $rg --yes --no-wait
739. Write-Host "Usuwanie w toku. Zajmie ~10 min."
740. 
741. # Krok 4: Po usunięciu RG — purguj konto AI (soft-delete protection)
742. # Poczekaj aż RG się usunie, potem:
743. az cognitiveservices account purge --name $aiName --resource-group $rg --location swedencentral
744. ```
745. 
746. ---
747. 
748. ## ⚠️ Znane pułapki i rozwiązania
749. 
750. | Problem | Przyczyna | Rozwiązanie |
751. |---------|-----------|-------------|
752. | "Account in state Accepted" | networkInjections provisionuje się asynchronicznie | Skrypt ma retry logic — poczeka i ponowi |
753. | "You don't have permission to build agents" | Rola Azure AI User = read-only | Nadaj **Azure AI Developer** na Account lub Project |
754. | "Request blocked by Auth cosmos" | Brak CosmosDB data plane RBAC | Dodaj `Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor` przez `az cosmosdb sql role assignment create` |
755. | "Connection is AzureBlob not AzureStorageAccount" | Zła kategoria storage connection | Kategoria MUSI być `AzureStorageAccount` |
756. | Portal nie ładuje się z Internetu | Public access = Disabled | ✅ To poprawne zachowanie! Użyj Bastion lub VPN |
757. | VPN Gateway: "must have zones" | AZ SKU wymaga zone-redundant PIP | Public IP musi mieć `zones: ['1','2','3']` |
758. | Deploy trwa >30 min | VPN Gateway jest powolny | To normalne — Gateway tworzy się 25 min |
759. 
760. ---
761.

763. 
764. ### Oficjalna dokumentacja Microsoft
765. 
766. | Temat | Link |
767. |-------|------|
768. | **AI Foundry — przegląd** | [learn.microsoft.com/azure/foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/) |
769. | **Agent Service — BYO VNet** | [Prywatna sieć dla agentów](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/how-to/virtual-networks) |
770. | **Agent Service — Managed VNet** | [Zarządzana sieć wirtualna](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/how-to/managed-virtual-network) |
771. | **Opcje sieciowe — porównanie** | [Networking concepts](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/concepts/networking) |
772. | **Konfiguracja środowiska agentów** | [Environment setup](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/agents/environment-setup) |
773. | **RBAC dla AI Foundry** | [Role-based access control](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/role-based-access-control) |
774. | **Private Endpoints — ogólnie** | [Azure Private Link](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview) |
775. | **Private DNS Zones** | [DNS configuration](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-dns) |
776. | **Azure Bastion** | [Bastion overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bastion/bastion-overview) |
777. | **VPN P2S — konfiguracja** | [Point-to-Site VPN](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/vpn-gateway-howto-point-to-site-resource-manager-portal) |
778. | **APIM Internal VNet** | [VNet integration](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-internal-vnet) |
779. | **CosmosDB data plane RBAC** | [RBAC for Cosmos DB](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cosmos-db/how-to-setup-rbac) |
780. 
781. ### Oficjalny template Bicep
782. 
783. - [foundry-samples / 15-private-network-standard-agent-setup](https://github.com/microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/tree/main/infrastructure/infrastructure-setup-bicep/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup)
784. 
785. ### Znane problemy i ograniczenia (stan: maj 2025)
786. 
787. | Problem | Opis | Obejście |
788. |---------|------|----------|
789. | **Race condition przy deploy** | Account z `networkInjections` przechodzi przez stan `Accepted` — child resources fail | Retry logic w skrypcie deploy |
790. | **CosmosDB data plane** | `disableLocalAuth: true` + brak RBAC data plane = 403 w portalu | `az cosmosdb sql role assignment create` |
791. | **VpnGw1 deprecated** | SKU VpnGw1 zastąpiony przez VpnGw1AZ | Użyj VpnGw1AZ + PIP z zones |
792. | **Storage connection** | Kategoria musi być `AzureStorageAccount` (nie `AzureBlob`) | Sprawdź Bicep template |
793. | **subnetArmId** | Dynamiczne `subnet[0].id` odrzucane | Użyj `resourceId()` |
794. | **Class A addresses** | `10.0.0.0/8` może nie działać we wszystkich regionach | Użyj `192.168.0.0/16` lub `172.16.0.0/12` |
795. 
796. ---
797. 
798. ---
799. 
800. *Warsztat opracowany na podstawie testów przeprowadzonych w maju 2025. Sprawdź dokumentację Microsoft pod kątem zmian.*
801.
