<sub>⏱️ 1466m 45s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Oto gotowe pytanie do Product Group:

---

**Subject: Project name visibility with "Full access isolation" RBAC — is hiding project names on the roadmap?**

Hi team,

We're running enterprise customer workshops for AI Foundry with BYO VNet and testing multi-tenant isolation patterns as documented in [RBAC for Foundry — Access Isolation Examples](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples).

We implemented the recommended **"Full access isolation"** configuration:
- `Azure AI User` scoped to **Project**
- `Reader` scoped to **Foundry resource (Account)**

Our testing confirms that **data plane isolation works correctly** — a user with these roles can create and use agents in their assigned project, but **cannot access the Agents tab or data in other projects**. This is great.

However, we have a concern that several enterprise customers have raised:

**The `Reader` role on the Account scope allows users to see the names of ALL projects under that Account**, even projects they have no data access to. This is expected ARM behavior (`*/read` on parent = list child resources), but some customers consider project names themselves to be sensitive information (e.g., project names may reveal business initiatives, acquisitions, or internal codenames).

**Questions:**

1. **Is there a way to grant the equivalent of `Reader` on Account (specifically: list model deployments for the dropdown) without also granting visibility to other project names?** We tested without `Reader` and the user cannot see models in the dropdown, effectively breaking agent creation.

2. **Is there any plan to introduce a more granular role or permission that allows listing model deployments without listing sibling projects?** Something like a `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments/read` without `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/projects/read`.

3. **For customers who require full project name hiding, is the only supported pattern to use separate Foundry Accounts (one per team)?** If so, is there any guidance on managing model deployments across multiple accounts efficiently?

4. **Would a custom role with selective `actions` work with the Foundry portal?** For example: `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/read` + `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments/read` but NOT `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/projects/read`. Or does the portal require the full `Reader` role?

**What we tested (for reference):**

| Configuration | Models visible? | Can create agents? | Sees other project names? |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI User on Project only | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| AI User on Project + Reader on Account | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Yes (names only, no data) |
| AI User on Account | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (all projects!) | ✅ Full access to all |

Environment: BYO VNet, Sweden Central, `disableLocalAuth: true`, Entra ID only.

Thanks for any guidance!

---

Skopiuj i wyślij. Pytania są precyzyjne i poparte naszymi testami — PG powinno docenić konkretne dane.

---

<sub>⏱️ 1473m 18s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot



OK, bardziej ogólna wersja:

---

**Subject: Full access isolation — can project names be hidden from users with Reader on Account?**

Hi team,

While testing multi-tenant isolation patterns as documented in [RBAC for Foundry — Access Isolation Examples](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples), we noticed a potential gap in the **"Full access isolation"** model.

The recommended setup is:
- `Azure AI User` on **Project** scope
- `Reader` on **Foundry resource (Account)** scope

Data plane isolation works perfectly — users can only access Agents/data in their assigned project. However, the `Reader` role on Account also exposes **names of all sibling projects** to every user (standard ARM `*/read` behavior). In some regulated environments, project names themselves may be considered sensitive (e.g., revealing business initiatives or codenames).

**What we tested:**

| Configuration | Models visible? | Can create agents? | Sees other project names? |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI User on Project only | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| AI User on Project + Reader on Account | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Yes (names only, no data) |
| AI User on Account | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (all projects!) | ✅ Full access to all |

Without `Reader` on Account, users cannot see model deployments in the portal dropdown, which effectively blocks agent creation.

**Questions:**

1. Is there a way to allow listing model deployments without also listing sibling projects? (e.g., a more granular role or permission split)

2. Would a custom role with selective actions (e.g., `accounts/read` + `accounts/deployments/read` but NOT `accounts/projects/read`) work with the Foundry portal?

3. For environments requiring full project name hiding, is the only supported path to use **separate Foundry Accounts** per team?

Environment: BYO VNet, Sweden Central, `disableLocalAuth: true`, Entra ID auth only.

Thanks!

---

Krócej, bardziej ogólnie, bez kontekstu warsztatowego.

---

{
  "command": "$sub = \"38b8b5d4-b3a1-4892-b2a7-3f5bcfdad59f\"\n\n# Custom role: can read deployments/models but NOT projects\n$roleDef = @{\n    Name = \"Foundry Model Reader\"\n    Description = \"Allows reading AI Foundry account, model deployments, and available models - without listing projects. Use instead of Reader for project-level isolation.\"\n    Actions = @(\n        \"Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/read\"\n        \"Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments/read\"\n        \"Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/models/read\"\n        \"Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/connections/read\"\n        \"Microsoft.Authorization/*/read\"\n    )\n    NotActions = @()\n    DataActions = @()\n    NotDataActions = @()\n    AssignableScopes = @(\"/subscriptions/$sub\")\n} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3\n\n$roleDef | Out-File -FilePath C:\\repos\\FoundryVnet\\custom-role.json -Encoding utf8\nWrite-Host \"Role definition saved\"\nGet-Content C:\\repos\\FoundryVnet\\custom-role.json",
  "description": "Create custom role definition",
  "initial_wait": 15,
  "shellId": "rbac-research"
}

<sub>⏱️ 1517m 26s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Gotowe! Konfiguracja userlab01 teraz:

| Rola | Scope |
|------|-------|
| **Azure AI User** | Project (project-lab01) |
| **Foundry Model Reader** (custom) | Account |

Custom role ma:
- ✅ `accounts/read` — odczyt konta
- ✅ `accounts/deployments/read` — lista modeli
- ✅ `accounts/models/read` — katalog modeli
- ✅ `accounts/connections/read` — połączenia
- ❌ **NIE MA** `accounts/projects/read` — nie powinien widzieć projektów!

**Teraz przetestuj na Bastion** (InPrivate, zaloguj się jako userlab01):
1. Czy widzisz nazwy innych projektów?
2. Czy widzisz modele w dropdown?
3. Czy możesz tworzyć agentów w project-lab01?

\> ⚠️ RBAC propaguje się ~5 minut — poczekaj chwilę przed testem.

C:\repos\FoundryVnet\foundry-vnet-test\WORKSHOP.md (lines 840-870)

<details>
<summary>31 lines</summary>

````
840. │  │ ✅ Agents: DOSTĘP    │  │ ❌ Agents: ZABLOKOW. │         │
841. │  │ ✅ Modele: WIDOCZNE  │  │ ❌ Data plane: BRAK  │         │
842. │  └──────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘         │

845. 
846. > 📘 **Poziomy izolacji — porównanie (przetestowane):**
847. >
848. > | Metoda | Widzi nazwy projektów? | Data plane izolacja? | Kiedy stosować |
849. > |--------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
850. > | **Reader + AI User na Project** | ⚠️ Tak (nazwy widoczne) | ✅ Pełna | Większość scenariuszy enterprise |
851. > | **Tylko AI User na Project** (bez Reader) | ❌ Nie widzi | ✅ Pełna, ale ⚠️ nie widzi modeli! | Nie zalecane — nie można tworzyć agentów |
852. > | **AI User na Account** | ✅ Widzi wszystko | ❌ BRAK izolacji! | NIE UŻYWAJ w multi-tenant! |
853. > | **Osobne Foundry Accounts** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Regulacje wymagające ukrycia nazw |
854. > | **Osobne Resource Groups** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Osobne zarządzanie per-zespół |
855. >
856. > ⚠️ **Jeśli regulacje wymagają ukrycia nazw projektów** → jedynym rozwiązaniem są **osobne Foundry Accounts**
857. > w osobnych Resource Groups. Każdy Account z własnym deployment modeli.
858. >
859. > 📎 [Access Isolation Examples](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry#access-isolation-examples)
860.

1009. ## 🌐 Faza 8: APIM — API Gateway w sieci prywatnej (45 min)
1010. 
1011. > ℹ️ Ta faza jest opcjonalna — dodaje API Management jako bramkę do modeli AI.
1012. 
1013. > 📘 **Po co APIM?** API Management pozwala wystawiać modele AI jako standardowe API (OpenAPI/REST)
1014. > z dodatkową warstwą kontroli: rate limiting, autentykacja, monitoring, transformacje.
1015. > W trybie **Internal VNet** APIM jest wstrzykiwany bezpośrednio do Twojego VNetu —
1016. > endpoint jest dostępny TYLKO z wnętrza sieci.
1017. >
1018. > 📎 **Dokumentacja:** [APIM Internal VNet](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-using-with-internal-vnet) |
1019. > [APIM + Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/api-management/api-management-authenticate-authorize-azure-openai)
1020.
````

</details>

````
240. > To jest ograniczenie ARM — rola Reader daje `*/read` na zasobie i child resources.
241. > Jeśli regulacje wymagają ukrycia nazw → użyj **osobnych Foundry Accounts** (patrz tabela poniżej).
242. 
243. ##### Przykład: 2 zespoły — izolacja data plane (rekomendowane)
244. 
245. ```
246. Foundry Account: ai-foundry-enterprise
247. │
248. ├── projekt-zespol-A:
249. │   ├── UserA1 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-A)
250. │   └── UserA2 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-A)
251. │
252. └── projekt-zespol-B:
253.     ├── UserB1 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-B)
254.     └── UserB2 → Azure AI User (scope: projekt-zespol-B)
255. 
256. Wszyscy: Reader (scope: Account) ← potrzebne do widoczności modeli
257. 
258. Wynik:
259. • Zespół A widzi nazwy obu projektów, ale NIE MA dostępu do danych zespołu B ✅
260. • Agent Service, Agents, pliki — izolacja per-projekt ✅
261. • Dane w CosmosDB/Storage/Search oddzielone przez MI projektu ✅
262. ```
263. 
264. > 📘 **Poziomy izolacji — porównanie:**
265. >
266. > | Metoda | Widzi nazwy projektów? | Data plane izolacja? | Kiedy stosować |
267. > |--------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
268. > | **Reader + AI User** (powyżej) | ⚠️ Tak | ✅ Pełna | Większość przypadków enterprise |
269. > | **Osobne Foundry Accounts** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Regulacje wymagające ukrycia nazw |
270. > | **Osobne Resource Groups** | ✅ Nie widzi nic | ✅ Pełna | Osobne zarządzanie per-zespół |
271. >
272. > Jeśli regulacje wymagają **pełnego ukrycia** istnienia innych projektów → użyj **osobnych Accounts**
273. > w osobnych Resource Groups. Każdy Account z własnym deployment modeli.
274. 
275. ##### Autentykacja — Entra ID vs API keys
276. 
277. | Funkcja | API key | Entra ID |
278. |---------|---------|----------|
279. | Agent Service | ❌ NIE działa | ✅ Wymagany |
280. | Evaluations | ❌ | ✅ Wymagany |
281. | Model inference | ✅ | ✅ |
282. | Per-user auditing | ❌ | ✅ |
283. | Managed Identity | ❌ | ✅ |
284. | Least privilege RBAC | ❌ (all-or-nothing) | ✅ |
285. 
286. > ⚠️ **Agent Service wymaga Entra ID** — API keys nie działają z agentami!
287. > W BYO VNet z `disableLocalAuth: true` API keys są w ogóle wyłączone.
288. >
289. > Token scope: `https://ai.azure.com/.default`
290.
````

</details>

### 💬 Copilot

Nie muszę szukać pliku — znam strukturę dokumentacji z naszych wcześniejszych pobrań. Oto gotowa propozycja:

---

## PR Proposal for `rbac-foundry.md`

**Title:** Add custom role example for hiding project names in "Full access isolation"

**File:** `articles/ai-services/foundry/concepts/rbac-foundry.md`

**Description:**

The current "Full access isolation" pattern recommends `Reader` on Foundry resource scope, which grants `*/read` and exposes all project names to every user. In regulated environments, project names themselves may be sensitive (e.g., revealing business initiatives or codenames). This PR adds a custom role example that provides the same model visibility without exposing sibling project names.

---

### Proposed change — add after "Full access isolation" section in Appendix:

```markdown
**Full access isolation with project name hiding.** In some regulated environments, 
even project names may be considered sensitive. The `Reader` role grants `*/read` at 
the Foundry resource scope, which includes `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/projects/read` 
— allowing users to see the names of all sibling projects.

To hide project names while preserving model visibility, create a custom role 
that replaces `Reader`:

```json
{
  "properties": {
    "roleName": "Foundry Model Reader",
    "description": "Read AI Foundry account and model deployments without listing projects.",
    "assignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/\<your-subscription-id\>"],
    "permissions": [{
      "actions": [
        "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/read",
        "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments/read",
        "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/models/read",
        "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/connections/read",
        "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read"
      ],
      "notActions": [],
      "dataActions": [],
      "notDataActions": []
    }]
  }
}
```

Then assign:

- Grant your developer the **Foundry Model Reader** custom role on Foundry resource scope 
  and **Azure AI User** on project scope.

This configuration provides the same functionality as "Full access isolation" — users can 
see model deployments in the portal dropdown and build agents in their assigned project — 
but cannot see the names of other projects under the same Foundry resource.

\> [!NOTE]
\> The key difference from `Reader` is the omission of 
\> `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/projects/read`. Without this permission, 
\> the Foundry portal does not display sibling project names in the project list.

| Configuration | See model deployments | Create agents | See other project names |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reader + Azure AI User on project | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Yes (names only, no data access) |
| Foundry Model Reader + Azure AI User on project | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Hidden |
| Azure AI User on project only (no account role) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
```

---

### Proposed change — add row to "Sample enterprise RBAC mappings" table:

```markdown
| Team members or developers (with project name hiding) | Foundry Model Reader (custom) on Foundry resource scope and Azure AI User on Foundry project scope | Developers build agents in a project with pre-deployed models. Other project names are hidden. See [custom role example](#full-access-isolation-with-project-name-hiding). |
```

---

### Proposed change — add note to "Notes and limitations" section:

```markdown
* The `Reader` role on Foundry resource scope grants `*/read`, which includes listing 
  all projects under that resource. If your organization requires hiding project names 
  from users who should not know about other teams' projects, consider using a 
  [custom role](#full-access-isolation-with-project-name-hiding) instead of `Reader`.
```

---

```
1. <#
2. .SYNOPSIS
3.     Pre-flight checks for Foundry BYO VNet deployment.
4. .DESCRIPTION
5.     Validates: az CLI login, subscription, provider registration, permissions, region.
6.     Run this BEFORE deploying any Bicep templates.
7. #>
8. param(
9.     [Parameter(Mandatory=$false)]
10.     [string]$ConfigPath = "$PSScriptRoot\..\config\test-config.json"
11. )
12. 
13. $ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"
14. 
15. # --- Load config ---
16. $config = Get-Content $ConfigPath -Raw | ConvertFrom-Json
17. $subscriptionId = $config.subscription_id
18. $location = $config.location
19. $providers = $config.required_providers
20. 
21. $results = @()
22. $pass = 0; $fail = 0; $warn = 0
23. 
24. function Add-Result($test, $status, $detail) {
25.     $icon = switch ($status) { "PASS" { [char]0x2705 } "FAIL" { [char]0x274C } "WARN" { [char]0x26A0  } }
```

<details>
<summary>30 lines</summary>

```
1. <#
2. .SYNOPSIS
3.     Master orchestrator — runs full Foundry BYO VNet test suite.
4. .DESCRIPTION
5.     Executes all phases in order:
6.       Phase 0: Pre-flight checks
7.       Phase 1: Deploy Foundry infrastructure (Bicep)
8.       Phase 2: Deploy APIM (Bicep)
9.       Phase 3: Import APIs into APIM
10.       Phase 4: Run all validation tests
11.       Phase 5: Generate E2E report
12. 
13.     Use -SkipDeploy to run only tests (if infrastructure already exists).
14.     Use -SkipApim to skip APIM deployment and tests.
15.     Use -WhatIf to validate Bicep without deploying.
16. #>
17. param(
18.     [Parameter(Mandatory=$false)]
19.     [string]$ConfigPath = "$PSScriptRoot\config\test-config.json",
20.     [switch]$SkipDeploy,
21.     [switch]$SkipApim,
22.     [switch]$WhatIf
23. )
24. 
25. $ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"
26. $scriptDir = "$PSScriptRoot\scripts"
27. $startTime = Get-Date
28. 
29. Write-Host @"
30.
```

</details>

329. ## 🔧 Przygotowanie (10 min)
330. 
331. ### Krok 1: Sklonuj repozytorium
332. 
333. ```powershell
334. git clone <repo-url>
335. cd foundry-vnet-test
336. ```
337. 
338. ### Krok 2: Zaloguj się do Azure
339. 
340. ```powershell
341. az login
342. az account set --subscription "TWOJA-SUBSKRYPCJA-ID"
343. az account show --query "{name:name, id:id}" -o table
344. ```
345. 
346. ### Krok 3: Skonfiguruj parametry
347. 
348. Otwórz `config/test-config.json` i dostosuj:
349. 
350. ```json
351. {
352.   "subscription_id": "TWOJA-SUBSKRYPCJA-ID",
353.   "resource_group": "rg-foundry-byovnet",
354.   "location": "swedencentral"
355. }
356. ```
357. 
358. > 💡 **Wspierane regiony**: Sweden Central, East US, East US 2, West Europe, France Central, Australia East
359. 
360. ### Krok 4: Ustaw hasło dla maszyny wirtualnej
361. 
362. ```powershell
363. $env:VM_ADMIN_PASSWORD = "WybierzSilneHaslo123!"
364. ```
365.

<sub>⏱️ 1720m 13s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Gotowe! ✅ Oto co dodałem:

| Plik | Opis |
|------|------|
| **`.env.example`** | Szablon — klient kopiuje na `.env` i uzupełnia |
| **`.env`** | W `.gitignore` — nigdy nie trafi do repo |
| **`scripts/load-env.ps1`** | Ładuje `.env`, ustawia zmienne, aktualizuje `test-config.json` |
| **`WORKSHOP.md`** | Sekcja "Przygotowanie" zaktualizowana o flow `.env` |

**Workflow klienta:**
```powershell
cp .env.example .env    # kopiuj szablon
# edytuj .env — wpisz hasła i subscription ID
. .\scripts\load-env.ps1  # załaduj
```

Hasła i subscription ID **nigdy nie trafią do repo** 🔒

<sub>⏱️ 1726m 46s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Folder `bicep\modules` jest pusty bo **main.bicep jest monolityczny** — cała infrastruktura (~780 linii) jest w jednym pliku. Folder `modules` został utworzony na początku "na zapas" ale nigdy nie rozbiliśmy Bicep na moduły.

Dwie opcje:

1. **Usunąć pusty folder** — Git i tak go nie śledzi, po prostu go wyczyścić
2. **Rozbić main.bicep na moduły** — lepsze dla warsztatu (czytelność), np. `networking.bicep`, `foundry.bicep`, `security.bicep`, `vpn.bicep`

Chcesz żebym usunął pusty folder czy rozbił Bicep na moduły?

1. // ============================================================================
2. // Foundry BYO VNet - Main Bicep Template
3. // Deploys: VNet, Subnets, Foundry Account, Project, PE, DNS, RBAC
4. // Based on: microsoft-foundry/foundry-samples/15-private-network-standard-agent-setup
5. // ============================================================================
6. 
7. @description('Azure region for all resources')
8. param location string = 'swedencentral'
9. 
10. @description('Foundry account name prefix')
11. param aiServicesName string = 'ai-foundry-byovnet'
12. 
13. @description('Model deployment configuration')

17. param modelSkuName string = 'GlobalStandard'

23.

171. }

```
700. 
701. resource jumpboxVm 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines@2024-07-01' = if (deployBastion) {
702.   name: 'vm-jumpbox'
703.   location: location
704.   properties: {
705.     hardwareProfile: { vmSize: 'Standard_B2s' }
706.     osProfile: {
707.       computerName: 'jumpbox'
708.       adminUsername: vmAdminUsername
709.       adminPassword: vmAdminPassword
710.     }
711.     storageProfile: {
712.       imageReference: {
713.         publisher: 'MicrosoftWindowsDesktop'
714.         offer: 'windows-11'
715.         sku: 'win11-24h2-pro'
716.         version: 'latest'
717.       }
718.       osDisk: {
719.         createOption: 'FromImage'
720.         managedDisk: { storageAccountType: 'Standard_LRS' }
721.       }
722.     }
723.     networkProfile: {
724.       networkInterfaces: [{ id: jumpboxNic.id }]
725.     }
726.   }
727. }
728. 
729. // ============================================================================
730. // VPN Gateway P2S (for client remote access)
731. // ============================================================================
732. resource vpnGatewayPip 'Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses@2024-05-01' = if (deployVpnGateway) {
733.   name: 'pip-vpn-gateway'
734.   location: location
735.   sku: { name: 'Standard' }
736.   zones: ['1', '2', '3']
737.   properties: {
738.     publicIPAllocationMethod: 'Static'
739.   }
740. }
741. 
742. resource vpnGateway 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworkGateways@2024-05-01' = if (deployVpnGateway) {
743.   name: 'vpngw-foundry-test'
744.   location: location
745.   properties: {
746.     gatewayType: 'Vpn'
747.     vpnType: 'RouteBased'
748.     sku: { name: 'VpnGw1AZ', tier: 'VpnGw1AZ' }
749.     ipConfigurations: [
750.       {
751.         name: 'default'
752.         properties: {
753.           publicIPAddress: { id: vpnGatewayPip.id }
754.           subnet: { id: vnet.properties.subnets[4].id } // GatewaySubnet
755.         }
756.       }
757.     ]
758.     vpnClientConfiguration: {
759.       vpnClientAddressPool: {
760.         addressPrefixes: ['172.16.0.0/24']
761.       }
762.       vpnClientProtocols: ['OpenVPN']
763.       vpnAuthenticationTypes: ['AAD']
764.       aadTenant: 'https://login.microsoftonline.com/${subscription().tenantId}'
765.       aadAudience: 'c632b3df-fb67-4d84-bdcf-b95ad541b5c8'
766.       aadIssuer: 'https://sts.windows.net/${subscription().tenantId}/'
767.     }
768.   }
769. }
770. 
771. // Additional outputs for portal access
772. output bastionId string = deployBastion ? bastion.id : ''
773. output jumpboxPrivateIp string = deployBastion ? jumpboxNic.properties.ipConfigurations[0].properties.privateIPAddress : ''
774. output vpnGatewayId string = deployVpnGateway ? vpnGateway.id : ''
775. output vpnGatewayPublicIp string = deployVpnGateway ? vpnGatewayPip.properties.ipAddress : ''
776.
```

</details>

### ✅ `task`

**Review Bicep modularization plan**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

````json
{
  "name": "bicep-modular-review",
  "agent_type": "rubber-duck",
  "description": "Review Bicep modularization plan",
  "prompt": "I'm refactoring a monolithic Bicep template (~776 lines) for Azure AI Foundry BYO VNet deployment into modules. The user wants the `bicep/modules/` folder populated.\n\n## Current monolithic main.bicep structure:\n\n1. **Lines 1-60**: Parameters + variables (6 subnets, 6 DNS zone names, resource name variables)\n2. **Lines 64-120**: VNet with 6 subnets (agent, PE, Bastion, mgmt, Gateway, APIM)\n3. **Lines 122-144**: 6 Private DNS Zones + VNet Links\n4. **Lines 148-219**: Storage Account, AI Search, CosmosDB (BYO dependencies)\n5. **Lines 222-269**: AI Services Account (with networkInjections BYO VNet) + Model Deployment\n6. **Lines 274-332**: Foundry Project + 3 Connections (Storage, Search, Cosmos)\n7. **Lines 335-470**: RBAC — 9 ARM role assignments + 2 CosmosDB data plane assignments\n   - Project MI → Search (2 roles), Storage (3 roles), CosmosDB (1 ARM + 1 data plane)\n   - Account MI → Search (1 role), Storage (2 roles), CosmosDB (1 ARM + 1 data plane)\n8. **Lines 475-497**: Capability Host (depends on ALL RBAC being done + connections)\n9. **Lines 498-635**: 4 Private Endpoints + 4 DNS Zone Groups\n10. **Lines 640-655**: Outputs\n11. **Lines 658-727**: Bastion + PIP + NIC + Jumpbox VM (conditional: deployBastion)\n12. **Lines 730-775**: VPN Gateway + PIP (conditional: deployVpnGateway)\n\n## Key dependency chain:\n- AI Services Account needs VNet subnet for `networkInjections.subnetArmId`\n- Private Endpoints need AI Services ID + dependency IDs + networking\n- Project has `dependsOn: [modelDeployment, peAiServices]` — must wait for PE\n- RBAC needs Project MI principal ID + Account MI principal ID\n- Capability Host `dependsOn` all 11 RBAC resources + uses connection names\n\n## My proposed modular split (9 modules):\n\n1. `modules/networking.bicep` — VNet + 6 subnets + 6 DNS zones + VNet links\n2. `modules/dependencies.bicep` — Storage Account + AI Search + CosmosDB\n3. `modules/ai-services.bicep` — AI Services Account + Model Deployment\n4. `modules/private-endpoints.bicep` — 4 PEs + 4 DNS Zone Groups\n5. `modules/project.bicep` — Project + 3 Connections\n6. `modules/rbac.bicep` — All 11 RBAC assignments (uses `existing` references for scope)\n7. `modules/capability-host.bicep` — Capability Host\n8. `modules/bastion.bicep` — Bastion + PIP + NIC + VM\n9. `modules/vpn-gateway.bicep` — VPN Gateway + PIP\n\n## main.bicep orchestrator dependency flow:\n```\nPhase 1 (parallel): networking, dependencies\nPhase 2: ai-services (needs networking.agentSubnetId)\nPhase 2 (parallel): bastion (needs networking.bastionSubnetId), vpn-gateway (needs networking.gatewaySubnetId)\nPhase 3: private-endpoints (needs networking, dependencies, ai-services outputs)\nPhase 4: project (dependsOn: privateEndpoints; needs ai-services.name, dependencies outputs)\nPhase 5: rbac (needs project.principalId, ai-services.principalId, dependencies names)\nPhase 6: capability-host (dependsOn: rbac; needs ai-services.name, project.name, connection names)\n```\n\n## Key concerns I want validated:\n\n1. **guid() consistency for RBAC**: Original uses `guid(searchService.id, project.id, roleId)`. In modular rbac.bicep, I'd use `existing` resource references so `searchService.id` resolves the same. But for `project.id` I need to pass the full resource ID (not just principalId) to keep guid() deterministic. I plan to pass both `projectId` (resource ID) and `projectPrincipalId` to rbac.bicep.\n\n2. **DNS zone IDs as array output**: networking.bicep outputs `dnsZoneIds` as an array using `[for ...]`. private-endpoints.bicep receives this and indexes by position (e.g., `dnsZoneIds[0]` = cognitiveservices). This is fragile if order changes. Is there a better pattern?\n\n3. **existing resource references in rbac.bicep**: I use `resource searchService ... existing = { name: searchServiceName }` to scope role assignments. This should work within the same resource group deployment.\n\n4. **Conditional modules**: bastion and vpn-gateway are wrapped in `if (deployBastion)` / `if (deployVpnGateway)`. Outputs from conditional modules need `?` ternary in main.bicep outputs.\n\n5. **Is 9 modules too granular or about right for a workshop?**\n\nPlease critique this plan and flag any issues before I implement.\n",
  "mode": "background"
}
````

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 1734m 30s</sub>

### ℹ️ Notification

Background agent "Review Bicep modularization plan" (rubber-duck) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>

I'm refactoring a monolithic Bicep template (~776 lines) for Azure AI Foundry BYO VNet deployment into modules. The user wants the `bicep/modules/` folder populated.

## Current monolithic main.bicep structure:

1. **Lines 1-60**: Parameters + variables (6 subnets, 6 DNS zone names, resource name variables)
2. **Lines 64-120**: VNet with 6 subnets (agent, PE, Bastion, mgmt, Gateway, APIM)
3. **Lines 122-144**: 6 Private DNS Zones + VNet Links
4. **Lines 148-219**: Storage Account, AI Search, CosmosDB (BYO dependencies)
5. **Lines 222-269**: AI Services Account (with networkInjections BYO VNet) + Model Deployment
6. **Lines 274-332**: Foundry Project + 3 Connections (Storage, Search, Cosmos)
7. **Lines 335-470**: RBAC — 9 ARM role assignments + 2 CosmosDB data plane assignments
   - Project MI → Search (2 roles), Storage (3 roles), CosmosDB (1 ARM + 1 data plane)
   - Account MI → Search (1 role), Storage (2 roles), CosmosDB (1 ARM + 1 data plane)
8. **Lines 475-497**: Capability Host (depends on ALL RBAC being done + connections)
9. **Lines 498-635**: 4 Private Endpoints + 4 DNS Zone Groups
10. **Lines 640-655**: Outputs
11. **Lines 658-727**: Bastion + PIP + NIC + Jumpbox VM (conditional: deployBastion)
12. **Lines 730-775**: VPN Gateway + PIP (conditional: deployVpnGateway)

## Key dependency chain:
- AI Services Account needs VNet subnet for `networkInjections.subnetArmId`
- Private Endpoints need AI Services ID + dependency IDs + networking
- Project has `dependsOn: [modelDeployment, peAiServices]` — must wait for PE
- RBAC needs Project MI principal ID + Account MI principal ID
- Capability Host `dependsOn` all 11 RBAC resources + uses connection names

## My proposed modular split (9 modules):

1. `modules/networking.bicep` — VNet + 6 subnets + 6 DNS zones + VNet links
2. `modules/dependencies.bicep` — Storage Account + AI Search + CosmosDB
3. `modules/ai-services.bicep` — AI Services Account + Model Deployment
4. `modules/private-endpoints.bicep` — 4 PEs + 4 DNS Zone Groups
5. `modules/project.bicep` — Project + 3 Connections
6. `modules/rbac.bicep` — All 11 RBAC assignments (uses `existing` references for scope)
7. `modules/capability-host.bicep` — Capability Host
8. `modules/bastion.bicep` — Bastion + PIP + NIC + VM
9. `modules/vpn-gateway.bicep` — VPN Gateway + PIP

## main.bicep orchestrator dependency flow:
```
Phase 1 (parallel): networking, dependencies
Phase 2: ai-services (needs networking.agentSubnetId)
Phase 2 (parallel): bastion (needs networking.bastionSubnetId), vpn-gateway (needs networking.gatewaySubnetId)
Phase 3: private-endpoints (needs networking, dependencies, ai-services outputs)
Phase 4: project (dependsOn: privateEndpoints; needs ai-services.name, dependencies outputs)
Phase 5: rbac (needs project.principalId, ai-services.principalId, dependencies names)
Phase 6: capability-host (dependsOn: rbac; needs ai-services.name, project.name, connection names)
```

## Key concerns I want validated:

1. **guid() consistency for RBAC**: Original uses `guid(searchService.id, project.id, roleId)`. In modular rbac.bicep, I'd use `existing` resource references so `searchService.id` resolves the same. But for `project.id` I need to pass the full resource ID (not just principalId) to keep guid() deterministic. I plan to pass both `projectId` (resource ID) and `projectPrincipalId` to rbac.bicep.

2. **DNS zone IDs as array output**: networking.bicep outputs `dnsZoneIds` as an array using `[for ...]`. private-endpoints.bicep receives this and indexes by position (e.g., `dnsZoneIds[0]` = cognitiveservices). This is fragile if order changes. Is there a better pattern?

3. **existing resource references in rbac.bicep**: I use `resource searchService ... existing = { name: searchServiceName }` to scope role assignments. This should work within the same resource group deployment.

4. **Conditional modules**: bastion and vpn-gateway are wrapped in `if (deployBastion)` / `if (deployVpnGateway)`. Outputs from conditional modules need `?` ternary in main.bicep outputs.

5. **Is 9 modules too granular or about right for a workshop?**

Please critique this plan and flag any issues before I implement.


</details>

---

<sub>⏱️ 1734m 34s</sub>

### ✅ `read_agent`

**Rubber-duck agent (Review Bicep modularization plan)**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "agent_id": "bicep-modular-review",
  "wait": true
}
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>63 lines</summary>

```
Agent completed. agent_id: bicep-modular-review, agent_type: rubber-duck, status: completed, description: Review Bicep modularization plan, elapsed: 163s, total_turns: 0, model: gpt-5.4, duration: 160s

Overall, the split is sound. The main risks are around **RBAC completeness** and **dependency inference**, not the module boundaries.

## Blocking Issues

### 1. RBAC inventory/count is already off
**Impact:** Easy to drop a permission during refactor and end up with a partially working deployment.  
**Severity:** Blocking  
**Why:** The current template has **12** RBAC assignments, not 11:
- Project MI: 2 Search + 3 Storage + 1 Cosmos ARM + 1 Cosmos data = **7**
- Account MI: 1 Search + 2 Storage + 1 Cosmos ARM + 1 Cosmos data = **5**

Also, the current `capabilityHost.dependsOn` is already missing `storageQueueRbacAccount`, so the monolith itself is inconsistent.

**Fix:**  
- Build the `rbac.bicep` module from an explicit matrix/list so you can verify all 12 assignments are preserved.
- Make `capability-host` depend on the **rbac module call**, not hand-listed assignments.

---

### 2. `existing` references do not create deployment ordering
**Impact:** Role assignments can race ahead of Search/Storage/Cosmos creation and fail with `NotFound`.  
**Severity:** Blocking  
**Why:** `existing` is fine for scoping, but it does **not** establish a dependency. If `rbac.bicep` only gets resource names, ARM can still schedule it before `dependencies.bicep` finishes.

**Fix:**  
- Pass **resource IDs** from producer modules (`searchServiceId`, `storageAccountId`, `cosmosAccountId`, `projectId`, `aiServicesId`) and/or
- Add explicit `dependsOn` on the `rbac` module call for `dependencies`, `project`, and `ai-services`.

This also helps preserve deterministic `guid()` values.

---

## Non-Blocking Issues

### 3. DNS zone ID array by position is too fragile
**Impact:** Wrong zone-to-PE wiring if ordering changes; hard-to-spot runtime breakage.  
**Severity:** Non-Blocking  
**Fix:** Output a named object, e.g.
- `dnsZoneIds.cognitiveservices`
- `dnsZoneIds.openai`
- `dnsZoneIds.servicesai`
- `dnsZoneIds.search`
- `dnsZoneIds.blob`
- `dnsZoneIds.cosmos`

Same advice for subnets: prefer `subnetIds.agent`, `subnetIds.pe`, etc. over index-based access.

---

### 4. `guid()` consistency plan is correct, but you need both principal IDs and resource IDs
**Impact:** Recreated role assignment names on refactor if inputs drift.  
**Severity:** Non-Blocking  
**Fix:** Your plan to pass `projectId` + `projectPrincipalId` is correct. Do the same for account-scoped assignments: pass `aiServicesId` + `aiServicesPrincipalId`. Don’t derive GUID seeds from names.

---

### 5. `existing` in `rbac.bicep` is fine only under same-scope assumptions
**Impact:** Refactor becomes brittle if later moved cross-RG/subscription.  
**Severity:** Non-Blocking  
**Fix:** For current same-RG workshop deployment, `existing { name: ... }` is fine. If you expect scope changes later, switch to explicit `scope`/ID-based patterns.

---

## Suggestions

### 6. Nine modules is about right
**Impact:** Good balance of teachability vs separation.  
**Severity:** Suggestion  
**Take:** Not too granular. The only “small” module is `capability-host`, but keeping it separate is justified because it must run **after RBAC**.

### 7. Keep `project` and `capability-host` separate
**Impact:** Cleaner orchestration.  
**Severity:** Suggestion  
**Why:** If you merge them, you reintroduce awkward post-RBAC sequencing inside one module.

### 8. Conditional modules approach is fine
**Impact:** No real issue.  
**Severity:** Suggestion  
**Fix:** Your `if (...)` + ternary output plan is the right pattern.

## Bottom line
The modular shape is good. The two things most likely to bite you are:
1. **Dropping/miscounting RBAC assignments**, and  
2. **Assuming `existing` creates ordering when it doesn’t**.
```

</details>


---

# GitHub Copilot CLI Documentation

## README
# GitHub Copilot CLI

The power of GitHub Copilot, now in your terminal.

GitHub Copilot CLI brings AI-powered coding assistance directly to your command line, enabling you to build, debug, and understand code through natural language conversations. Powered by the same agentic harness as GitHub's Copilot coding agent, it provides intelligent assistance while staying deeply integrated with your GitHub workflow.

See [our official documentation](https://docs.github.com/copilot/concepts/agents/about-copilot-cli) for more information.

![Image of the splash screen for the Copilot CLI](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f40aa23d-09dd-499e-9457-1d57d3368887)


## 🚀 Introduction and Overview

We're bringing the power of GitHub Copilot coding agent directly to your terminal. With GitHub Copilot CLI, you can work locally and synchronously with an AI agent that understands your code and GitHub context.

- **Terminal-native development:** Work with Copilot coding agent directly in your command line — no context switching required.
- **GitHub integration out of the box:** Access your repositories, issues, and pull requests using natural language, all authenticated with your existing GitHub account.
- **Agentic capabilities:** Build, edit, debug, and refactor code with an AI collaborator that can plan and execute complex tasks.
- **MCP-powered extensibility:** Take advantage of the fact that the coding agent ships with GitHub's MCP server by default and supports custom MCP servers to extend capabilities.
- **Full control:** Preview every action before execution — nothing happens without your explicit approval.

We're still early in our journey, but with your feedback, we're rapidly iterating to make the GitHub Copilot CLI the best possible companion in your terminal.

## 📦 Getting Started

### Supported Platforms

- **Linux**
- **macOS**
- **Windows**

### Prerequisites

- (On Windows) **PowerShell** v6 or higher
- An **active Copilot subscription**. See [Copilot plans](https://github.com/features/copilot/plans?ref_cta=Copilot+plans+signup&ref_loc=install-copilot-cli&ref_page=docs).

If you have access to GitHub Copilot via your organization or enterprise, you cannot use GitHub Copilot CLI if your organization owner or enterprise administrator has disabled it in the organization or enterprise settings. See [Managing policies and features for GitHub Copilot in your organization](http://docs.github.com/copilot/managing-copilot/managing-github-copilot-in-your-organization/managing-github-copilot-features-in-your-organization/managing-policies-for-copilot-in-your-organization) for more information.

### Installation

Install with the install script (macOS and Linux):

```bash
curl -fsSL https://gh.io/copilot-install | bash
```

Or

```bash
wget -qO- https://gh.io/copilot-install | bash
```

Use `| sudo bash` to run as root and install to `/usr/local/bin`.

Set `PREFIX` to install to `$PREFIX/bin/` directory. Defaults to `/usr/local`
when run as root or `$HOME/.local` when run as a non-root user.

Set `VERSION` to install a specific version. Defaults to the latest version.

For example, to install version `v0.0.369` to a custom directory:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://gh.io/copilot-install | VERSION="v0.0.369" PREFIX="$HOME/custom" bash
```

Install with [Homebrew](https://formulae.brew.sh/cask/copilot-cli) (macOS and Linux):

```bash
brew install copilot-cli
```

```bash
brew install copilot-cli@prerelease
```


Install with [WinGet](https://github.com/microsoft/winget-cli) (Windows):

```bash
winget install GitHub.Copilot
```

```bash
winget install GitHub.Copilot.Prerelease
```


Install with [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@github/copilot) (macOS, Linux, and Windows):

```bash
npm install -g @github/copilot
```

```bash
npm install -g @github/copilot@prerelease
```


### Launching the CLI

```bash
copilot
```

On first launch, you'll be greeted with our adorable animated banner! If you'd like to see this banner again, launch `copilot` with the `--banner` flag.

If you're not currently logged in to GitHub, you'll be prompted to use the `/login` slash command. Enter this command and follow the on-screen instructions to authenticate.

#### Authenticate with a Personal Access Token (PAT)

You can also authenticate using a fine-grained PAT with the "Copilot Requests" permission enabled.

1. Visit https://github.com/settings/personal-access-tokens/new
2. Under "Permissions," click "add permissions" and select "Copilot Requests"
3. Generate your token
4. Add the token to your environment via the environment variable `GH_TOKEN` or `GITHUB_TOKEN` (in order of precedence)

### Using the CLI

Launch `copilot` in a folder that contains code you want to work with.

By default, `copilot` utilizes Claude Sonnet 4.5. Run the `/model` slash command to choose from other available models, including Claude Sonnet 4 and GPT-5.

### Experimental Mode

Experimental mode enables access to new features that are still in development. You can activate experimental mode by:

- Launching with the `--experimental` flag: `copilot --experimental`
- Using the `/experimental` slash command from within the CLI

Once activated, the setting is persisted in your config, so the `--experimental` flag is no longer needed on subsequent launches.

#### Experimental Features

- **Autopilot mode:** Autopilot is a new mode (press `Shift+Tab` to cycle through modes), which encourages the agent to continue working until a task is completed.

Each time you submit a prompt to GitHub Copilot CLI, your monthly quota of premium requests is reduced by one. For information about premium requests, see [About premium requests](https://docs.github.com/copilot/managing-copilot/monitoring-usage-and-entitlements/about-premium-requests).

For more information about how to use the GitHub Copilot CLI, see [our official documentation](https://docs.github.com/copilot/concepts/agents/about-copilot-cli).

## 🔧 Configuring LSP Servers

GitHub Copilot CLI supports Language Server Protocol (LSP) for enhanced code intelligence. This feature provides intelligent code features like go-to-definition, hover information, and diagnostics.

### Installing Language Servers

Copilot CLI does not bundle LSP servers. You need to install them separately. For example, to set up TypeScript support:

```bash
npm install -g typescript-language-server
```

For other languages, install the corresponding LSP server and configure it following the same pattern shown below.

### Configuring LSP Servers

LSP servers are configured through a dedicated LSP configuration file. You can configure LSP servers at the user level or repository level:

**User-level configuration** (applies to all projects):
Edit `~/.copilot/lsp-config.json`

**Repository-level configuration** (applies to specific project):
Create `.github/lsp.json` in your repository root

Example configuration:

```json
{
  "lspServers": {
    "typescript": {
      "command": "typescript-language-server",
      "args": ["--stdio"],
      "fileExtensions": {
        ".ts": "typescript",
        ".tsx": "typescript"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Viewing LSP Server Status

Check configured LSP servers using the `/lsp` command in an interactive session, or view your configuration files directly.

For more information, see the [changelog](./changelog.md).

## 📢 Feedback and Participation

We're excited to have you join us early in the Copilot CLI journey.

We're building quickly. Expect frequent updates--please keep your client up to date for the latest features and fixes!

Your insights are invaluable! Open an issue in this repo, join Discussions, and run `/feedback` from the CLI to submit a confidential feedback survey!


## Help Command Output
Global
  /help - show full help
  / - commands
  @ - mention files
  # - mention issues and pull requests
  ! - execute shell command
  shift+tab - switch modes
  ctrl+s - run command, preserve input
  ctrl+o/e - expand all timeline
  ctrl+c - cancel
  ctrl+c×2 - exit
  esc - cancel
  ctrl+d - shutdown
  ctrl+l - clear screen
  ctrl+t - toggle reasoning display
  ctrl+x → b - move current task to background
  ctrl+x → o - open most recent link

Input
  ctrl+a - go to line start
  ctrl+e - go to line end
  ctrl+h - delete previous character
  ctrl+w - delete previous word
  ctrl+u - delete from cursor to beginning of line
  ctrl+k - delete from cursor to end of line
  meta+←/→ - move cursor by word
  ctrl+g - edit prompt in $EDITOR

Agent Environment
  /init - Initialize Copilot instructions for this repository.
  /agent - Browse and select from available agents (if any)
  /skills - Manage skills for enhanced capabilities
  /mcp - Manage MCP server configuration
  /plugin - Manage plugins and plugin marketplaces

Agents / Subagents
  /model - Select AI model to use
  /delegate - Send this session to GitHub and Copilot will create a PR
  /fleet - Enable fleet mode for parallel subagent execution
  /tasks - View and manage tasks (subagents and shell commands)

Code
  /ide - Connect to an IDE workspace
  /diff - Review the changes made in the current directory
  /pr - Operate on pull requests for the current branch
  /review - Run code review agent to analyze changes
  /lsp - Manage language server configuration
  /terminal-setup - Configure terminal for multiline input support (shift+enter)

Permissions
  /allow-all - Enable all permissions (tools, paths, and URLs)
  /add-dir - Add a directory to the allowed list for file access
  /list-dirs - Display all allowed directories for file access
  /cwd - Change working directory or show current directory
  /reset-allowed-tools - Reset the list of allowed tools

Session
  /resume - Switch to a different session (optionally specify session ID, task ID, or name)
  /rename - Rename the current session, or auto-generate a name from conversation
  /context - Show context window token usage and visualization
  /usage - Display session usage metrics and statistics
  /session - View and manage sessions. Use subcommands for details.
  /compact - Summarize conversation history to reduce context window usage
  /share - Share session or research report to markdown file, HTML file, or GitHub gist
  /remote - Show remote status or toggle remote control from GitHub web and mobile
  /copy - Copy the last response to the clipboard
  /rewind - Rewind the last turn and revert file changes

Help
  /help - Show help for interactive commands
  /changelog - Display changelog for CLI versions. Add 'summarize' to get an AI summary.
  /feedback - Provide feedback about the CLI
  /theme - View or set color mode
  /statusline - Configure status line items
  /footer - Configure status line items
  /update - Update the CLI to the latest version
  /version - Display version information and check for updates
  /experimental - Show available experimental features, or enable/disable experimental mode
  /clear - Abandon this session and start fresh
  /instructions - View and toggle custom instruction files
  /streamer-mode - Toggle streamer mode (hides preview model names and quota details for streaming)

Other commands
  /ask - Ask a quick side question without adding to conversation history
  /env - Show loaded environment details (instructions, MCP servers, skills, agents, plugins, LSPs, extensions)
  /exit - Exit the CLI
  /keep-alive - Manage keep-alive mode (prevents system sleep).
  /login - Log in to Copilot
  /logout - Log out of an OAuth login session
  /new - Start a new conversation
  /plan - Create an implementation plan before coding
  /research - Run deep research investigation using GitHub search and web sources
  /restart - Restart the CLI, preserving the current session
  /search - Search the conversation timeline
  /sidekicks - View running sidekick agents
  /undo - Rewind the last turn and revert file changes
  /user - Manage GitHub user list

Copilot respects instructions from these locations:
  CLAUDE.md
  GEMINI.md
  AGENTS.md (in git root & cwd)
  .github/instructions/**/*.instructions.md (in git root & cwd)
  .github/copilot-instructions.md
  $HOME/.copilot/copilot-instructions.md
  COPILOT_CUSTOM_INSTRUCTIONS_DIRS (additional directories via env var)

To learn about what I can do
  Ask me: "What can you do?"
  Or visit: https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/use-copilot-cli

Contents of https://docs.github.com/api/article/body?pathname=/en/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/use-copilot-cli:
# Using GitHub Copilot CLI

Learn how to use GitHub Copilot from the command line.

The command-line interface (CLI) for GitHub Copilot allows you to use Copilot directly from your terminal. For more information, see [About GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/concepts/agents/about-copilot-cli).

## Prerequisite

Install Copilot CLI. See [Installing GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/set-up/install-copilot-cli).

## Using Copilot CLI

1. In your terminal, navigate to a folder that contains code you want to work with.

2. Enter `copilot` to start Copilot CLI.

   Copilot will ask you to confirm that you trust the files in this folder.

   > \[!IMPORTANT]
   > During this GitHub Copilot CLI session, Copilot may attempt to read, modify, and execute files in and below this folder. You should only proceed if you trust the files in this location. For more information about trusted directories, see [About GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/concepts/agents/about-copilot-cli#trusted-directories).

3. Choose one of the options:

   **1. Yes, proceed**:

   Copilot can work with the files in this location for this session only.

   **2. Yes, and remember this folder for future sessions**:

   You trust the files in this folder for this and future sessions. You won't be asked again when you start Copilot CLI from this folder. Only choose this option if you are sure that it will always be safe for Copilot to work with files in this location.

   **3. No, exit (Esc)**:

   End your Copilot CLI session.

4. If you are not currently logged in to GitHub, you'll be prompted to use the `/login` slash command. Enter this command and follow the on-screen instructions to authenticate.

5. Enter a prompt in the CLI.

   This can be a simple chat question, or a request for Copilot to perform a specific task, such as fixing a bug, adding a feature to an existing application, or creating a new application.

   For some examples of prompts, see [About GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/concepts/agents/about-copilot-cli).

6. When Copilot wants to use a tool that could modify or execute files—for example, `touch`, `chmod`, `node`, or `sed`—it will ask you to approve the use of the tool.

   Choose one of the options:

   **1. Yes**:

   Allow Copilot to use this tool. The next time Copilot wants to use this tool, it will ask you to approve it again.

   **2. Yes, and approve TOOL for the rest of the running session**:

   Allow Copilot to use this tool—with any options—without asking again, for the rest of the currently running session. Any pending parallel permission requests of the same type will be auto-approved. You will have to approve the command again in future sessions.

   Choosing this option is useful for many tools—such as `chmod`—as it avoids you having to approve similar commands repeatedly in the same session. However, be aware of the security implications of this option. For example, choosing this option for the command `rm` would allow Copilot to delete any file in the current directory or its subdirectories without asking for your approval.

   **3. No, and tell Copilot what to do differently (Esc)**:

   Copilot will not run the command. Instead, it ends the current operation and awaits your next prompt. You can tell Copilot to continue the task but using a different approach.

   For example, if you ask Copilot to create a bash script but you do not want to use the script Copilot suggests, you can stop the current operation and enter a new prompt, such as: `Continue the previous task but include usage instructions in the script`.

   When you reject a tool permission request, you can also give Copilot inline feedback about the rejection so it can adapt its approach without stopping entirely.

## Tips

Optimize your experience with Copilot CLI with the following tips.

### Stop a currently running operation

If you enter a prompt and then decide you want to stop Copilot from completing the task while it is still "Thinking," press <kbd>Esc</kbd>.

### Use plan mode

Plan mode lets you collaborate with Copilot on an implementation plan before any code is written. Press <kbd>Shift</kbd>+<kbd>Tab</kbd> to cycle in and out of plan mode.

### Include a specific file in your prompt

To add a specific file to your prompt, use `@` followed by the relative path to the file. For example: `Explain @config/ci/ci-required-checks.yml` or `Fix the bug in @src/app.js`. This adds the contents of the file to your prompt as context for Copilot.

When you start typing a file path, the matching paths are displayed below the prompt box. Use the arrow keys to select a path and press <kbd>Tab</kbd> to complete the path in your prompt.

### Work with files in a different location

To complete a task, Copilot may need to work with files that are outside the current working directory. If a prompt you have entered in an interactive session requires Copilot to modify a file outside the current location, it will ask you to approve access to the file's directory.

You can also add a trusted directory manually at any time by using the slash command:

```shell
/add-dir /path/to/directory
```

If all of the files you want to work with are in a different location, you can switch the current working directory without starting a new Copilot CLI session by using either the `/cwd` or `/cd` slash commands:

```shell
/cwd /path/to/directory
```

### Run shell commands

You can prepend your input with `!` to directly run shell commands, without making a call to the model.

```shell
!git clone https://github.com/github/copilot-cli
```

### Resume an interactive session

You can use the `--resume` command-line option or the `/resume` slash command to select and resume an interactive CLI session, allowing you to pick up right where you left off, with the saved context. You can kick off a Copilot cloud agent session on GitHub, and then use GitHub Copilot CLI to bring that session to your local environment.

> \[!TIP]
> To quickly resume the most recently closed local session, enter this in your terminal:
>
> ```shell
> copilot --continue
> ```

### Use custom instructions

You can enhance Copilot’s performance, by adding custom instructions to the repository you are working in. Custom instructions are natural language descriptions saved in Markdown files in the repository. They are automatically included in prompts you enter while working in that repository. This helps Copilot to better understand the context of your project and how to respond to your prompts.

Copilot CLI supports:

* Repository-wide instructions in the `.github/copilot-instructions.md` file.
* Path-specific instructions files: `.github/instructions/**/*.instructions.md`.
* Agent files such as `AGENTS.md`.

For more information, see [Adding custom instructions for GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/add-custom-instructions).

### Use custom agents

A custom agent is a specialized version of Copilot. Custom agents help Copilot handle unique workflows, particular coding conventions, and specialist use cases.

Copilot CLI includes a default group of custom agents for common tasks:

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th style="width:20%">Agent</th>
      <th>Description</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>Explore</td>
      <td>Performs quick codebase analysis, allowing you to ask questions about your code without adding to your main context.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Task</td>
      <td>Executes commands such as tests and builds, providing brief summaries on success and full output on failure.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>General purpose</td>
      <td>Handles complex, multi-step tasks that require the full toolset and high-quality reasoning, running in a separate context to keep your main conversation clearly focused.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Code review</td>
      <td>Reviews changes with a focus on surfacing only genuine issues, minimizing noise.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Research</td>
      <td>Performs deep research across your codebase, relevant repositories, and the web, producing a detailed report with citations.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Rubber duck</td>
      <td>Acts as a constructive critic to provide feedback on some non-trivial tasks. Used automatically by Copilot CLI.</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

The AI model being used by the CLI can choose to delegate a task to a subsidiary subagent process, that operates using a custom agent with specific expertise, if it judges that this would result in the work being completed more effectively. The model may equally choose to handle the work directly in the main agent.

Some built-in custom agents, such as the rubber duck agent, are consulted automatically by Copilot on your behalf rather than invoked by you directly. You won't see them as separate options when you run `/agent`, but you may see Copilot mention them as it works through a task.

You can define your own custom agents using Markdown files, called agent profiles, that specify what expertise the agent should have, what tools it can use, and any specific instructions for how it should respond.

You can define custom agents at the user, repository, or organization/enterprise level:

| Type                                           | Location                                                                                 | Scope                                                       |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| User-level custom agent                        | local `~/.copilot/agents` directory                                                      | All projects                                                |
| Repository-level custom agent                  | `.github/agents` directory in your local and remote repositories                         | Current project                                             |
| Organization and Enterprise-level custom agent | `/agents` directory in the `.github-private` repository in an organization or enterprise | All projects under your organization and enterprise account |

In the case of naming conflicts, a system-level agent overrides a repository-level agent, and the repository-level agent would override an organization-level agent.

Custom agents can be used in three ways:

* Using the slash command in the CLI's interactive interface to select from the list of available custom agents:

  ```shell
  /agent
  ```

* Calling out to custom agent directly in a prompt:

  ```shell
  Use the refactoring agent to refactor this code block
  ```

  Copilot will automatically infer the agent you want to use.

* Specifying the custom agent you want to use with the command-line option. For example:

  ```shell
  copilot --agent=refactor-agent --prompt "Refactor this code block"
  ```

For more information, see [Creating custom agents for Copilot cloud agent](/en/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/cloud-agent/create-custom-agents).

### Use skills

You can create skills to enhance the ability of Copilot to perform specialized tasks with instructions, scripts, and resources.

For more information, see [Adding agent skills for GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/customize-copilot/add-skills).

### Add an MCP server

Copilot CLI comes with the GitHub MCP server already configured. This MCP server allows you to interact with resources on GitHub.com—for example, allowing you to merge pull requests from the CLI.

To extend the functionality available to you in Copilot CLI, you can add more MCP servers:

1. Use the following slash command:

   ```shell
   /mcp add
   ```

2. Fill in the details for the MCP server you want to add, using the <kbd>Tab</kbd> key to move between fields.

3. Press <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>S</kbd> to save the details.

Details of your configured MCP servers are stored in the `mcp-config.json` file, which is located, by default, in the `~/.copilot` directory. This location can be changed by setting the `COPILOT_HOME` environment variable. For information about the JSON structure of a server definition, see [Connect agents to external tools](/en/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/cloud-agent/extend-cloud-agent-with-mcp#writing-a-json-configuration-for-mcp-servers).

### Context management

Copilot CLI provides several slash commands to help you monitor and manage your context window:

* `/usage`: Lets you view your session statistics, including:
  * The amount of premium requests used in the current session
  * The session duration
  * The total lines of code edited
  * A breakdown of token usage per model

* `/context`: Provides a visual overview of your current token usage

* `/compact`: Manually compresses your conversation history to free up context space

GitHub Copilot CLI automatically compresses your history in the background when your conversation approaches 95% of the token limit, without interrupting your workflow.

### Enable all permissions

For situations where you trust Copilot to run freely, you can use the `--allow-all` or `--yolo` flags to enable all permissions at once.

### Toggle reasoning visibility

Press <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>T</kbd> to show or hide the model's reasoning process while it generates a response. This setting persists across sessions, allowing you to observe how Copilot works through complex problems.

## Find out more

For a complete list of the command line options and slash commands that you can use with Copilot CLI, do one of the following:

* Enter `?` in the prompt box in an interactive session.
* Enter `copilot help` in your terminal.

For additional information use one of the following commands in your terminal:

* **Configuration settings**:

  `copilot help config`

  You can adjust the configuration settings by editing the `settings.json` file, which is located, by default, in the `~/.copilot` directory. This location can be changed by setting the `COPILOT_HOME` environment variable.

* **Environment variables** that affect Copilot CLI:

  `copilot help environment`

* **Available logging levels**:

  `copilot help logging`

* **Permissions** for allowing or denying tool use:

  `copilot help permissions`

## Feedback

If you have any feedback about GitHub Copilot CLI, please let us know by using the `/feedback` slash command in an interactive session and choosing one of the options. You can complete a private feedback survey, submit a bug report, or suggest a new feature.

## Next steps

Copilot CLI can operate as a conversational assistant, answering questions and helping you write code interactively. Beyond chat, Copilot CLI supports a range of agentic modes that allow you to delegate tasks with greater autonomy.

You can work with agents in Copilot CLI to support a full task lifecycle, from delegating work to reviewing results:

* **Delegate tasks autonomously**: Run Copilot CLI in autopilot mode to complete multi-step tasks without requiring approval at each step. See [Delegating tasks to Copilot](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/use-copilot-cli/delegate-tasks-to-cca).
* **Invoke custom agents**: Invoke specialized agents tailored to specific tasks, such as code review, documentation, or security audits. See [Invoking custom agents](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/use-copilot-cli/invoke-custom-agents).
* **Steer agents**: Guide and refine agent behavior during task execution to keep work on track. See [Steering agents in GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/use-copilot-cli/steer-agents).
* **Request a code review**: Use Copilot CLI to get an AI-powered review of your code changes. See [Requesting a code review with GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/use-copilot-cli/agentic-code-review).

## Further reading

* [Best practices for GitHub Copilot CLI](/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli/cli-best-practices)
* [GitHub Copilot CLI command reference](/en/copilot/reference/copilot-cli-reference/cli-command-reference)
* [GitHub Copilot CLI configuration directory](/en/copilot/reference/copilot-cli-reference/cli-config-dir-reference)
* [Copilot CLI ACP server](/en/copilot/reference/copilot-cli-reference/acp-server)
````

</details>

{

<sub>⏱️ 1758m 48s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Gotowe! ✅ Dodałem `AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop` do `sessionSync` z `level: "user"`.

Od teraz sesje z tego repo będą **synchronizowane na GitHub** — członkowie zespołu z dostępem do repo mogą:
- Zobaczyć sesje na GitHub.com
- Wznowić je lokalnie przez `copilot --resume`
- Kontynuować pracę z dowolnego komputera

\> ⚠️ Zmiana zadziała przy **następnej sesji** — ta bieżąca sesja została rozpoczęta jako lokalna.

<sub>⏱️ 1759m 29s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Tak to działa krok po kroku:

### 1. Konfiguracja (już zrobione ✅)
W `~/.copilot/settings.json` dodaliśmy:
```json
{ "origin": "AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop", "level": "user" }
```

### 2. Rozpoczęcie nowej sesji
```powershell
cd C:\repos\foundry-enterprise-workshop
copilot
```
Copilot wykryje że `origin` = `AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop` → sesja zostanie zsynchronizowana na GitHub.

### 3. Udostępnienie koledze
Kolega z dostępem do repo:
```powershell
git clone https://github.com/AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop.git
cd foundry-enterprise-workshop
copilot --resume
```
Zobaczy listę sesji (Twoich i swoich) i może wybrać którąś do kontynuacji.

### 4. Wznowienie sesji z GitHub.com
Na stronie repo → zakładka **Copilot** → widoczne sesje → klik → kontynuuj w przeglądarce lub wróć do CLI z `copilot --resume \<session-id\>`.

---

**W skrócie:** `level: "user"` = sesje lecą na GitHub i każdy z dostępem do repo je widzi. `level: "local"` = zostają na dysku.

\> 💡 Ta bieżąca sesja jest lokalna (rozpoczęta przed zmianą). Nowa sesja z tego repo będzie już na GitHub.

<details>
<summary>84 lines</summary>

```
Configuration Settings:  `allowedUrls`: list of URLs or domains that are allowed to be accessed without prompting.
    Supports exact URLs (e.g., "https://github.com"), domain patterns (e.g., "github.com"),
    and wildcard subdomains (e.g., "*.github.com").  `autoUpdate`: whether to automatically download updated CLI versions; defaults to `true`.  `banner`: frequency of showing animated banner; defaults to "once".
    - "always" displays it every time
    - "never" disables it
    - "once" displays it the first time  `bashEnv`: whether to enable BASH_ENV support for bash shells; defaults to `false`.
    - Can also be set with --bash-env or --no-bash-env
    - Using either flag persists the preference to config  `beep`: whether to beep when user attention is required; defaults to `true`.  `powershellFlags`: list of flags passed to PowerShell (pwsh) on startup; defaults to `["-NoProfile", "-NoLogo"]`.
    - Overrides the default flags when set; only applies on Windows
    - Examples: `["-NoProfile"]` to keep the logo, `[]` to load your profile and show the logo,
      `["-NoProfile", "-NoLogo", "-ExecutionPolicy", "Bypass"]` for locked-down environments
    - Use caution: flags that change execution semantics (`-Command`, `-File`, `-NoExit`,
      `-NonInteractive`, `-EncodedCommand`, `-InputFormat`, `-OutputFormat`) will break
      the runtime. See the settings documentation for details.  `compactPaste`: whether to collapse large pasted content into compact tokens; defaults to `true`.
    - When `true` (default), pastes with more than 10 lines are displayed as `[Paste #N - X lines]`
    - When `false`, all pasted content is displayed directly in the input  `copyOnSelect`: whether selecting text in alt screen mode automatically copies it to the system clipboard; defaults to `true` on macOS, `false` elsewhere.
    - When `true`, text is copied to the system clipboard as soon as the mouse button is released after a selection
    - When `false` (default), use Ctrl+C or right-click to copy selected text to the system clipboard
    - On Linux, the X11 primary selection is always updated on selection regardless of this setting  `customAgents.defaultLocalOnly`: whether to default to only use local custom agents (no remote org/enterprise); defaults to `false`.  `deniedUrls`: list of URLs or domains that are denied access. Denial rules take precedence
    over allow rules.  `experimental`: whether to enable experimental features; defaults to `false`.
    - Can be enabled with --experimental flag or /experimental command  `includeCoAuthoredBy`: whether to instruct the agent to add a Co-authored-by trailer to git commits; defaults to `true`.  `keepAlive`: keep-alive mode applied at CLI startup; prevents the system from sleeping while the session is active. Defaults to `"off"`.
    - `"off"` (default): system sleeps normally.
    - `"on"`: keep-alive is enabled for the duration of the session.
    - `"busy"`: keep-alive is enabled only while the agent is working on a turn.
    - Can also be set interactively with `/keep-alive [on|off|busy|<duration>]`.  `companyAnnouncements`: list of messages to display in the banner on startup.
    - One message is randomly selected each time the CLI starts
    - Useful for team announcements or reminders  `continueOnAutoMode`: whether to automatically switch to auto mode when rate-limited; defaults to `false`.
    - When `true`, eligible rate limit errors (per-model, weekly, or integration limits) trigger an automatic switch to auto mode and retry
    - Does not apply to global rate limits, generic 429s, or BYOK providers  `logLevel`: log level for CLI; defaults to "default". Set to "all" for debug logging.  `model`: AI model to use for Copilot CLI; can be changed with /model command or --model flag option.
    - "claude-sonnet-4.6"
    - "claude-sonnet-4.5"
    - "claude-haiku-4.5"
    - "claude-opus-4.7"
    - "claude-opus-4.6"
    - "claude-opus-4.6-fast"
    - "claude-opus-4.5"
    - "claude-sonnet-4"
    - "gpt-5.5"
    - "gpt-5.4"
    - "gpt-5.3-codex"
    - "gpt-5.2-codex"
    - "gpt-5.2"
    - "gpt-5.1"
    - "gpt-5.4-mini"
    - "gpt-5-mini"
    - "gpt-4.1"

  `mouse`: whether to enable mouse support in alt screen mode; defaults to `true`.
    - Can also be set with --mouse or --no-mouse
    - Using either flag persists the preference to config
    - When disabled, scroll wheel and click events are not captured by the CLI

  `renderMarkdown`: whether to render markdown in the terminal; defaults to `true`.

  `respectGitignore`: whether to exclude gitignored files from the `@` file mention picker; defaults to `true`.
    - When `false`, the `@` picker includes files that are normally excluded by `.gitignore`
    - Useful when sub-repositories or vendor directories are gitignored but still need to be referenced

  `screenReader`: whether to enable screen reader optimizations; defaults to `false`.

  `stream`: whether to enable streaming mode; defaults to `true`.

  `streamerMode`: whether to enable streamer mode; defaults to `false`.
    - Hides preview model names and quota details (useful for streaming or screen sharing)
    - Can be toggled with /streamer-mode command; persists across sessions

  `statusLine`: configuration for a custom status line displayed below the input.
    - `type`: must be "command"
    - `command`: path to an executable script that generates status line content.
      Supports `~` (home directory) and environment variables (`$HOME`, `${HOME}`, `${VAR:-default}`).
      The script receives the current session status as JSON on stdin and should print the status line to stdout.       
    - `padding`: optional number of spaces to pad each line on the left

  `theme`: theme to color and stylize output; defaults to "auto".
    - "auto" detects terminal background and decides based on luminosity
    - "dark" uses contrasting ANSI colors for dark backgrounds
    - "light" uses contrasting ANSI colors for light backgrounds

  `trustedFolders`: list of folders where permission to read or execute files has been granted.

  `updateTerminalTitle`: whether to update the terminal tab/window title with current intent; defaults to `true`.       
    - Shows what the agent is currently working on in the terminal title bar
    - Only works on supported terminal emulators (xterm, iTerm, Windows Terminal, etc.)
    - Can also be disabled via the `COPILOT_DISABLE_TERMINAL_TITLE` environment variable

  `disableAllHooks`: whether to disable all hooks (repo-level and user-level); defaults to `false`.

  `hooks`: inline hook definitions, keyed by event name (same schema as .github/hooks/*.json).
    - In global config.json these act as user-level hooks; in repo settings.json they act as repo-level hooks

  `ide.autoConnect`: whether to automatically connect to an IDE workspace on startup; defaults to `true`.
    - When `false`, the CLI will not auto-connect to IDEs or watch for new IDE lock files
    - You can still manually connect using the /ide command

  `ide.openDiffOnEdit`: whether to open file edit diffs in the connected IDE for approval; defaults to `true`.
    - When `false`, file edit approvals are shown only in the terminal
    - The IDE connection is unaffected; diagnostics and selection features still work
<exited with exit code 0>
```

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 1760m 47s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Oto najciekawsze opcje z `settings.json` poza `sessionSync`:

### Sesje (`sessionSync`)
| `level` | Opis |
|---------|------|
| `"local"` | Sesja tylko na tym komputerze |
| `"user"` | Sync na GitHub — widoczna dla zespołu z dostępem do repo |

`"origin"` może być `"*"` (wildcard = domyślne dla wszystkich repo) albo konkretne `"org/repo"`.

### Najciekawsze inne opcje

| Opcja | Opis |
|-------|------|
| `model` | Domyślny model (np. `claude-opus-4.6`, `gpt-5.4`) |
| `effortLevel` | `"low"` / `"medium"` / `"high"` — jak bardzo agent się stara |
| `experimental` | `true` → włącza autopilot mode i inne nowe features |
| `keepAlive` | `"on"` / `"busy"` / `"off"` — blokuje usypianie komputera |
| `companyAnnouncements` | Wiadomości zespołowe na starcie CLI 😎 |
| `includeCoAuthoredBy` | `true/false` — dodaje "Co-authored-by: Copilot" do commitów |
| `allowedUrls` | Lista URL-i które agent może odwiedzać bez pytania |
| `continueOnAutoMode` | `true` → przy rate-limit automatycznie zmienia model |
| `respectGitignore` | `false` → `@` picker pokazuje też gitignored pliki |
| `ide.autoConnect` | Auto-łączy z VS Code dla podglądu diffów |

Ty masz ustawione: `effortLevel: "high"`, `logLevel: "all"`, `model` domyślny. Chcesz coś zmienić?

<sub>⏱️ 1763m 10s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Na podstawie analizy:

### Lokalnie sesja trzymana jest w:
```
~/.copilot/session-state/\<session-id\>/
├── events.jsonl          ← pełna historia konwersacji (turny)
├── session.db            ← baza SQLite (todos, stan)
├── plan.md               ← plan pracy
├── workspace.yaml        ← metadata (cwd, repo)
└── checkpoints/          ← punkty kontrolne (streszczenia)
```

### Na GitHub — to **NIE jest plik w repo**
Sesje synchronizowane przez `sessionSync` trafiają do **wewnętrznego storage GitHuba** (Copilot backend), nie jako commit/branch/plik w repozytorium. Repo służy tylko jako **identyfikator** — "ta sesja należy do tego repo".

Dlatego:
- Nie zobaczysz żadnych dodatkowych plików/branchy w repo
- Sesje widoczne są przez **GitHub UI** (zakładka Copilot) i **API**
- Dostęp do sesji = dostęp do repo (uprawnienia się dziedziczą)
- `copilot --resume` pobiera sesję z backendu GitHub, nie z gita

W skrócie: GitHub trzyma sesje w swoim cloud storage, a `origin: "AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop"` mówi "przypisz te sesje do tego repo".

### 💬 Copilot

Nie — ta sesja **nie jest syncowana**. Widać w `workspace.yaml`:
- `cwd: C:\repos\FoundryVnet` — rozpoczęta ze starego folderu (nie ma git remote)
- Brak pola `origin` / `sync` — sesja została utworzona jako lokalna

Sesja sync działa na zasadzie: w momencie **startu sesji** Copilot sprawdza `git remote` → porównuje z `sessionSync` w settings → decyduje local/cloud. Zmiana settings w trakcie sesji **nie przełącza** istniejącej sesji.

**Żeby mieć syncowaną sesję:**
```powershell
cd C:\repos\foundry-enterprise-workshop
copilot                  # nowa sesja → auto-sync na GitHub
# lub
copilot --resume         # wznów tę sesję z nowego kontekstu
```

Nowa sesja z folderu `foundry-enterprise-workshop` (gdzie `origin` = `AzureClub/foundry-enterprise-workshop`) automatycznie pójdzie na GitHub.
